rationale: The PaO2/FiO2 ratio is calculated as 58 / 0.80 = 72.5 mmHg. Wait — this actually places the patient in the severe ARDS category (P/F ≤ 100). However, let's recalculate: PaO2 58, FiO2 0.80 → P/F = 72.5. This is severe ARDS. The Berlin Definition classifies ARDS as: mild (P/F 201–300 on PEEP ≥ 5), moderate (P/F 101–200), and severe (P/F ≤ 100). This patient has a P/F of 72.5 with PEEP ≥ 5, bilateral infiltrates, and a known ARDS risk factor (pancreatitis) — meeting all criteria for severe ARDS.

distractorRationales.a: A P/F of 72.5 is far below the 201–300 range for mild ARDS.
distractorRationales.b: A P/F of 72.5 falls below 100, placing this patient in the severe, not moderate, category.
distractorRationales.d: This patient meets all four Berlin Definition criteria: acute onset, bilateral infiltrates, PEEP ≥ 5 cmH2O, and P/F ≤ 300 with a known risk factor.

tags: Respiratory
Calculate P/F ratio (PaO2 ÷ FiO2) on PEEP ≥ 5 to classify ARDS severity: mild > 200, moderate 101–200, severe ≤ 100.
takeaway: Calculate P/F ratio (PaO2 ÷ FiO2) on PEEP ≥ 5 to classify ARDS severity: mild > 200, moderate 101–200, severe ≤ 100.

visualRationale.title: P/F ratio calculation: severe ARDS classification
visualRationale.caption: Berlin Definition requires P/F on PEEP ≥ 5, bilateral infiltrates, and a known risk factor.

stem: An ARDS patient on volume control with TV 480 mL has peak airway pressure 38 cmH2O and plateau pressure 34 cmH2O. The PEEP is set at 10 cmH2O. What does the plateau pressure indicate about this patient's respiratory mechanics?

options.text: Plateau pressure is acceptable — the priority should be reducing peak pressure by treating bronchospasm.
Plateau pressure of 34 cmH2O exceeds the lung-protective threshold of 30 cmH2O, suggesting over-distension risk.
The driving pressure (plateau minus PEEP) is 24 cmH2O, which is within the safe range.
Peak and plateau pressure differences of 4 cmH2O confirm significant airway resistance.

rationale: Plateau pressure reflects alveolar distension pressure — the static pressure delivered to the alveoli at end-inspiration. The ARDSNet protocol targets plateau pressure ≤ 30 cmH2O to minimize ventilator-induced lung injury (VILI) from over-distension. A plateau of 34 cmH2O exceeds this threshold, indicating the current tidal volume is causing excessive alveolar stretch. Additionally, the driving pressure (plateau minus PEEP) is 34 − 10 = 24 cmH2O, which independently predicts mortality in ARDS when above 15 cmH2O.

distractorRationales.a: Bronchospasm causes high peak pressures with a relatively normal plateau — here the plateau itself is elevated, indicating the problem is parenchymal compliance, not airway resistance.
distractorRationales.c: Driving pressure of 24 cmH2O significantly exceeds the 15 cmH2O threshold associated with increased ARDS mortality — this is not a safe range.
distractorRationales.d: A peak-to-plateau difference of 4 cmH2O actually suggests low airway resistance — this does not explain the problem, which lies in lung compliance.

tags: Respiratory
Target plateau pressure ≤ 30 cmH2O and driving pressure ≤ 15 cmH2O in ARDS — both independently predict VILI and mortality.
takeaway: Target plateau pressure ≤ 30 cmH2O and driving pressure ≤ 15 cmH2O in ARDS — both independently predict VILI and mortality.

visualRationale.title: Plateau vs. peak: identifying the source of high pressure
visualRationale.caption: Elevated plateau means alveolar over-distension; elevated peak with normal plateau means airway resistance.

stem: A 41-year-old female with severe ARDS (P/F ratio 74 mmHg) has been supine for 18 hours. The intensivist orders prone positioning. Which statement best explains the primary physiological rationale for this intervention?

options.text: Prone positioning reduces secretion pooling by using gravity to drain the airways anteriorly.
Prone positioning redistributes ventilation to better-perfused dorsal lung regions, improving V/Q matching.
Prone positioning increases functional residual capacity by compressing the ventral lung zones.
Prone positioning reduces cardiac output, which lowers oxygen demand and improves SpO2.

rationale: In ARDS, the dorsal (posterior) lung regions are typically atelectatic due to compression from abdominal contents and edema fluid redistributing under gravity. These same dorsal regions receive the majority of pulmonary blood flow due to their gravitational position when supine. Prone positioning recruits these collapsed dorsal regions and improves V/Q matching by aligning better ventilation with preserved perfusion. The PROSEVA trial demonstrated a significant mortality reduction in severe ARDS (P/F < 150) with ≥ 16 hours/day of prone positioning.

distractorRationales.a: While secretion drainage may be a secondary benefit, it is not the primary mechanism — V/Q matching improvement is the central physiological rationale.
distractorRationales.c: Prone positioning increases FRC by decompressing the dorsal lung segments — it does not compress the ventral zones in a therapeutically meaningful way.
distractorRationales.d: Prone positioning does not reduce cardiac output as a mechanism — it improves oxygenation through ventilation redistribution, not by lowering oxygen consumption.

tags: Respiratory
Prone positioning in severe ARDS recruits dorsal atelectasis and improves V/Q matching — the PROSEVA trial showed mortality benefit with ≥ 16 hours/day.
takeaway: Prone positioning in severe ARDS recruits dorsal atelectasis and improves V/Q matching — the PROSEVA trial showed mortality benefit with ≥ 16 hours/day.

visualRationale.title: Prone positioning: V/Q redistribution in ARDS
visualRationale.caption: Supine ARDS concentrates atelectasis where blood flow is highest — proning fixes the mismatch.
visualRationale.conclusion: ≥ 16 hours prone daily improves oxygenation and reduces mortality in P/F < 150 ARDS.

stem: A 33-year-old male with a traumatic subdural hematoma is found unresponsive. Vital signs: HR 38, BP 218/104, RR 4 and irregular. Pupils are unequal — left 7 mm non-reactive, right 3 mm sluggish. What is the most likely etiology and immediate priority?

options.text: Hypertensive emergency — initiate sodium nitroprusside infusion and cardiology consult.
Transtentorial herniation with Cushing's triad — prepare for immediate surgical intervention and ICP management.
Vagal episode from pain — administer atropine and place patient in Trendelenburg position.
Opioid toxicity — administer naloxone 0.4 mg IV and secure airway.

rationale: Cushing's triad — hypertension, bradycardia, and irregular respirations — is a late sign of critically elevated intracranial pressure and impending brainstem herniation. The unequal pupils with ipsilateral dilation indicate uncal herniation compressing cranial nerve III. This is a neurosurgical emergency requiring immediate ICP reduction measures (HOB elevation, hyperventilation, osmotic therapy) and urgent neurosurgical consultation for hematoma evacuation.

distractorRationales.a: Aggressively lowering BP in the setting of elevated ICP is dangerous — the hypertension is a reflex mechanism to maintain cerebral perfusion pressure. Treating it without addressing ICP can worsen cerebral ischemia.
distractorRationales.c: The clinical picture — subdural hematoma, unequal pupils, Cushing's triad — is not consistent with a vagal episode.
distractorRationales.d: Opioid toxicity produces miosis (pinpoint pupils), not anisocoria with unilateral dilation, and would not explain the subdural hematoma context.

tags: Neurological
Cushing's triad (hypertension + bradycardia + irregular respirations) with anisocoria in a head-injured patient signals herniation — this is a neurosurgical emergency.
takeaway: Cushing's triad (hypertension + bradycardia + irregular respirations) with anisocoria in a head-injured patient signals herniation — this is a neurosurgical emergency.

visualRationale.title: Cushing's triad: the brainstem compression alarm
visualRationale.caption: Each component of the triad reflects a different brainstem response to uncal herniation.
visualRationale.conclusion: This is herniation — reverse ICP, protect airway, and activate neurosurgery immediately.

stem: A 25-year-old male with severe TBI has an ICP monitor in place. Current values: ICP 28 mmHg, MAP 84 mmHg. The nurse calculates a CPP of 56 mmHg. Which intervention takes priority?

options.text: Lower MAP to 70 mmHg to reduce cerebral hyperemia.
Raise the head of bed to 90 degrees to maximize venous drainage.
Increase MAP to achieve a CPP of at least 60–70 mmHg while simultaneously treating elevated ICP.
Administer furosemide to reduce brain water content and lower ICP.

rationale: Cerebral perfusion pressure = MAP − ICP. With an ICP of 28 and MAP of 84, the CPP is 56 mmHg — below the Brain Trauma Foundation guideline target of 60–70 mmHg. The dual priority is to raise MAP (norepinephrine is first-line) to maintain CPP while simultaneously treating the elevated ICP with osmotic therapy (mannitol or hypertonic saline), appropriate sedation/analgesia, and optimizing head positioning (HOB 30°, neutral head alignment).

distractorRationales.a: Lowering MAP in a patient with elevated ICP would further reduce CPP and worsen cerebral ischemia — the opposite of the goal.
distractorRationales.b: HOB at 30° is appropriate, but 90° is excessive and can impair venous drainage and reduce MAP, worsening CPP.
distractorRationales.d: Furosemide has a limited role in acute TBI-related ICP management and is not first-line — hyperosmolar therapy (mannitol or 3% saline) is preferred.

tags: Neurological
CPP = MAP − ICP; target CPP 60–70 mmHg in severe TBI by raising MAP and treating elevated ICP simultaneously.
takeaway: CPP = MAP − ICP; target CPP 60–70 mmHg in severe TBI by raising MAP and treating elevated ICP simultaneously.

visualRationale.title: CPP calculation and target management
visualRationale.caption: Both sides of the equation matter — treat ICP and support MAP in parallel.

metrics.label: MAP
metrics.directionLabel: needs to increase to support CPP

stem: A 62-year-old woman with a history of alcohol use disorder is admitted after a witnessed seizure. She continues to have generalized tonic-clonic activity 12 minutes after IV lorazepam 4 mg was given. What is the most appropriate next pharmacological intervention?

options.text: Repeat lorazepam 4 mg IV and observe for another 10 minutes.
Administer fosphenytoin 20 mg PE/kg IV or levetiracetam 60 mg/kg IV as second-line therapy.
Initiate propofol infusion at 5 mcg/kg/min as first-line status epilepticus rescue therapy.
Perform emergent EEG monitoring and defer further medication until results are available.

rationale: This patient is in established status epilepticus — seizures continuing more than 5 minutes, or failure to stop after adequate benzodiazepine dosing. After first-line benzodiazepine failure, the 2016 Neurocritical Care Society guidelines recommend second-line therapy with levetiracetam, fosphenytoin, or valproic acid at weight-based doses. Levetiracetam (60 mg/kg, max 4,500 mg) and fosphenytoin (20 mg PE/kg) are preferred for speed and safety profile.

distractorRationales.a: Repeating the full lorazepam dose after first-line failure is not evidence-based and increases respiratory depression risk without improved seizure termination.
distractorRationales.c: Propofol is a third-line agent used for refractory status epilepticus (RSE) after second-line agents have failed — it requires intubation and is not the next step.
distractorRationales.d: Continuous EEG monitoring is important but should not delay pharmacological intervention in active convulsive status epilepticus — treatment is the immediate priority.

tags: Neurological
After benzodiazepine failure in status epilepticus, give second-line therapy (levetiracetam, fosphenytoin, or valproate) promptly — do not wait or repeat benzos.
takeaway: After benzodiazepine failure in status epilepticus, give second-line therapy (levetiracetam, fosphenytoin, or valproate) promptly — do not wait or repeat benzos.

visualRationale.title: Status epilepticus treatment ladder
visualRationale.caption: Time is brain — escalate rapidly through the treatment tiers without delay.

stem: A 27-year-old immunocompetent patient presents with fever 39.8°C, stiff neck, photophobia, and petechial rash. CT head shows no mass effect. The provider plans a lumbar puncture. Which action should the nurse prioritize before and alongside the LP procedure?

options.text: Initiate antibiotics only after the LP result confirms bacterial meningitis to avoid confounding cultures.
Administer empirical IV antibiotics (ceftriaxone + vancomycin) and dexamethasone immediately — the LP should not delay treatment.
Obtain three sets of blood cultures over 90 minutes before starting any antimicrobial therapy.
Begin IV acyclovir only, as the petechial rash suggests viral meningococcemia that does not require antibiotics.

rationale: Bacterial meningitis is a time-critical emergency where delays in antibiotics worsen morbidity and mortality. The current standard of care is to administer empirical antibiotics (ceftriaxone + vancomycin to cover resistant Streptococcus pneumoniae) plus dexamethasone (to reduce inflammatory CNS injury) immediately — even before the LP, as long as the LP can follow without unsafe delay. Blood cultures should be drawn simultaneously. CSF culture positivity is only modestly affected by early antibiotic administration.

distractorRationales.a: Delaying antibiotics until culture confirmation in bacterial meningitis is dangerous and deviates from standard of care — mortality increases significantly with each hour of delay.
distractorRationales.c: Blood culture series over 90 minutes is not appropriate here — draw one or two sets simultaneously and start antibiotics immediately.
distractorRationales.d: Petechial or purpuric rash with meningismus is classic for Neisseria meningitidis — this is a bacterial emergency requiring IV penicillin or ceftriaxone, not acyclovir alone.

tags: Neurological
Start antibiotics and dexamethasone in suspected bacterial meningitis before the LP if the LP will be delayed — every hour without treatment worsens outcomes.
takeaway: Start antibiotics and dexamethasone in suspected bacterial meningitis before the LP if the LP will be delayed — every hour without treatment worsens outcomes.

visualRationale.title: Bacterial meningitis: treat first, culture immediately
visualRationale.caption: Antibiotics should not be withheld while waiting for LP results when the clinical picture is compelling.

stem: A 48-year-old male admitted for abdominal surgery has a CIWA-Ar score of 18 on post-op day 2. He is diaphoretic, tremulous, and reporting visual hallucinations. HR 118, BP 156/96. Which intervention is the highest priority?

options.text: Place the patient in soft restraints and call for a sitter to prevent falls.
Administer symptom-triggered benzodiazepine therapy (lorazepam or diazepam) per the CIWA protocol.
Initiate a beta-blocker to control the hypertension and tachycardia.
Administer haloperidol 5 mg IM to treat the visual hallucinations.

rationale: A CIWA-Ar score of 18 indicates severe alcohol withdrawal requiring immediate pharmacological intervention. Benzodiazepines (lorazepam, diazepam, or chlordiazepoxide) are the first-line treatment and most strongly evidence-based therapy for preventing progression to delirium tremens and seizures. Symptom-triggered dosing is preferred over fixed-schedule dosing. Hallucinations, diaphoresis, tremor, and autonomic instability in this score range require urgent benzodiazepine administration.

distractorRationales.a: Restraints do not address the pharmacological emergency of alcohol withdrawal and may increase agitation — benzodiazepines address both the neurological and autonomic instability.
distractorRationales.c: Beta-blockers control autonomic symptoms but do not prevent seizures or delirium tremens — they should not replace benzodiazepines as primary therapy.
distractorRationales.d: Haloperidol may help with hallucinations but lowers the seizure threshold and should not be used as a substitute for benzodiazepines in alcohol withdrawal.

tags: Neurological
CIWA-Ar ≥ 15 requires urgent benzodiazepine therapy — it is the only evidence-based intervention that prevents withdrawal seizures and delirium tremens.
takeaway: CIWA-Ar ≥ 15 requires urgent benzodiazepine therapy — it is the only evidence-based intervention that prevents withdrawal seizures and delirium tremens.

visualRationale.title: CIWA-Ar severity tiers and treatment escalation
visualRationale.caption: CIWA-Ar guides symptom-triggered dosing — do not wait for a seizure to initiate benzos.
visualRationale.conclusion: Benzodiazepines are the only first-line agent proven to prevent delirium tremens.

stem: A 22-year-old male with type 1 diabetes arrives with nausea, vomiting, and Kussmaul breathing. Labs: Na 132 mEq/L, K 5.8 mEq/L, Cl 94 mEq/L, HCO3 8 mEq/L, glucose 544 mg/dL, pH 7.14. What is the anion gap and what does it indicate?

options.text: Anion gap 14 — normal gap, suggesting a hyperchloremic non-gap acidosis.
Anion gap 30 — elevated gap consistent with diabetic ketoacidosis.
Anion gap 22 — mildly elevated, likely from lactic acidosis unrelated to diabetes.
Anion gap cannot be calculated without an albumin level.

rationale: Anion gap = Na − (Cl + HCO3) = 132 − (94 + 8) = 30 mEq/L. A normal anion gap is 8–12 mEq/L (uncorrected). A gap of 30 is markedly elevated and, in the context of type 1 diabetes, hyperglycemia, metabolic acidosis, and Kussmaul breathing, is diagnostic of DKA. The elevated gap reflects accumulation of ketoacid anions (acetoacetate and beta-hydroxybutyrate) that replace bicarbonate as unmeasured anions.

distractorRationales.a: An anion gap of 14 would be normal — the actual calculated gap of 30 is markedly elevated and is not consistent with hyperchloremic acidosis.
distractorRationales.c: The gap of 30 is significantly elevated — lactic acidosis typically occurs in the setting of hypoperfusion or sepsis, not the classic DKA presentation described here.
distractorRationales.d: While albumin correction is useful for borderline gaps, the gap here is 30 — clearly elevated without needing correction, and the diagnosis is evident from the clinical context.

tags: Endocrine
Anion gap = Na − (Cl + HCO3); a gap > 12 with hyperglycemia, ketosis, and metabolic acidosis confirms DKA.
takeaway: Anion gap = Na − (Cl + HCO3); a gap > 12 with hyperglycemia, ketosis, and metabolic acidosis confirms DKA.

visualRationale.title: DKA anion gap: ketoacids replace bicarbonate
visualRationale.caption: As ketoacid accumulation rises, bicarbonate is consumed and the gap widens.

stem: A 74-year-old nursing home resident is brought in obtunded, with glucose 1,140 mg/dL, Na 152 mEq/L, BUN 68 mg/dL, and creatinine 2.4 mg/dL. There is no ketonuria. Calculated serum osmolality is 388 mOsm/kg. What is the most likely diagnosis and the most critical initial intervention?

options.text: DKA — begin insulin infusion at 0.1 units/kg/hr immediately.
Hyperosmolar hyperglycemic state (HHS) — begin aggressive IV fluid resuscitation with 0.9% NaCl.
HHS — begin insulin infusion immediately at high doses to rapidly lower glucose.
Acute kidney injury from dehydration — begin dialysis to clear the osmolar load.

rationale: Hyperosmolar hyperglycemic state (HHS) is characterized by extreme hyperglycemia (> 600 mg/dL), high serum osmolality (> 320 mOsm/kg), profound dehydration, and absence of significant ketosis. The fluid deficit in HHS is typically 8–12 liters. The priority intervention is aggressive fluid resuscitation with 0.9% NaCl to restore intravascular volume and reduce hyperosmolality. Insulin is held until fluid resuscitation is underway and potassium is replete, as premature insulin can cause dangerous hypokalemia and rapid osmolar shifts.

distractorRationales.a: DKA requires ketonuria/ketonemia and significant metabolic acidosis — neither is present here. Beginning insulin before adequate fluid resuscitation in HHS can be dangerous.
distractorRationales.c: High-dose insulin before fluid replacement in HHS causes osmotic fluid shifts from the extracellular space and worsens hypovolemia — fluids must lead the treatment.
distractorRationales.d: The kidney injury is prerenal from dehydration — restoring volume will improve renal function; dialysis is not indicated without refractory uremic complications.

tags: Endocrine
In HHS, fluids first — the fluid deficit is 8–12 L; insulin is started only after volume is restored and potassium is ≥ 3.3 mEq/L.
takeaway: In HHS, fluids first — the fluid deficit is 8–12 L; insulin is started only after volume is restored and potassium is ≥ 3.3 mEq/L.

visualRationale.title: HHS vs. DKA: critical initial distinction
visualRationale.caption: HHS demands fluid replacement before insulin — the osmolar crisis is primarily a volume emergency.
visualRationale.conclusion: Resuscitate with 1–1.5 L/hr NS initially, then transition to 0.45% NaCl once hemodynamically stable.

stem: A 55-year-old male on the neuro ICU following subarachnoid hemorrhage has a serum sodium of 121 mEq/L, urine osmolality 680 mOsm/kg, and urine sodium 58 mEq/L. He is euvolemic on exam. Which diagnosis and management approach is most appropriate?

options.text: Cerebral salt wasting — initiate 3% hypertonic saline and fludrocortisone.
SIADH — restrict free water to 800–1,000 mL/day and avoid rapid sodium correction.
Hypovolemic hyponatremia — administer isotonic saline at 150 mL/hr.
Central diabetes insipidus — begin intranasal DDAVP.

rationale: SIADH is characterized by hyponatremia, inappropriately concentrated urine (urine osm > 100 mOsm/kg), elevated urine sodium (> 40 mEq/L), and euvolemia — all present here. Following SAH, SIADH is the more common cause of hyponatremia compared to cerebral salt wasting (CSW), though distinguishing them is critical. CSW patients are hypovolemic, whereas this patient is euvolemic. Fluid restriction is first-line for SIADH. Correction rate should not exceed 6–8 mEq/L per 24 hours to prevent osmotic demyelination syndrome.

distractorRationales.a: CSW also presents with hyponatremia and elevated urine sodium but is distinguished by hypovolemia — this patient is euvolemic, making SIADH more likely.
distractorRationales.c: Isotonic saline in euvolemic SIADH can worsen hyponatremia — the kidneys retain the saline sodium and excrete more free water.
distractorRationales.d: Central DI presents with hypernatremia and dilute urine, not concentrated urine and hyponatremia — the findings here are the opposite.

tags: Endocrine
SIADH post-SAH: euvolemic hyponatremia with concentrated urine and high urine sodium — fluid restriction is first-line, never correct faster than 6–8 mEq/L/24 hours.
takeaway: SIADH post-SAH: euvolemic hyponatremia with concentrated urine and high urine sodium — fluid restriction is first-line, never correct faster than 6–8 mEq/L/24 hours.

visualRationale.title: SIADH vs. cerebral salt wasting: the euvolemia key
visualRationale.caption: Volume status is the most important distinguishing feature — get it right before treating.
visualRationale.conclusion: Misidentifying CSW as SIADH and restricting fluids can worsen cerebral vasospasm after SAH.

stem: A 38-year-old male with Addison's disease is admitted for a surgical procedure. On post-op day 1, he develops BP 72/40, HR 134, nausea, and abdominal pain refractory to IV fluids. Random cortisol is 3 mcg/dL. Which is the most appropriate immediate treatment?

options.text: Administer oral hydrocortisone 20 mg with breakfast once the patient can take PO.
Give hydrocortisone 100 mg IV bolus immediately, followed by continuous infusion or frequent bolus dosing.
Initiate fludrocortisone 0.1 mg orally to restore mineralocorticoid function.
Begin ACTH stimulation testing to confirm adrenal crisis before initiating steroids.

rationale: Adrenal crisis (acute adrenal insufficiency) in the surgical stress context is life-threatening. The combination of profound hypotension, tachycardia, abdominal pain, and a cortisol of 3 mcg/dL (normal stress response > 18–20 mcg/dL) confirms the diagnosis. Treatment is hydrocortisone 100 mg IV bolus immediately, followed by 50–100 mg IV every 6–8 hours or a continuous infusion of 200 mg/day. Normal saline resuscitation should continue simultaneously. Fludrocortisone is not necessary acutely — high-dose hydrocortisone has sufficient mineralocorticoid activity at these doses.

distractorRationales.a: Oral hydrocortisone cannot be absorbed reliably in a hemodynamically unstable post-op patient — IV administration is mandatory.
distractorRationales.c: Fludrocortisone alone does not provide adequate glucocorticoid coverage during adrenal crisis — it also cannot be given IV.
distractorRationales.d: Never delay steroid treatment for confirmatory testing in suspected adrenal crisis — the diagnosis is clinically supported and the risk of delaying treatment is death.

tags: Endocrine
Adrenal crisis requires immediate IV hydrocortisone 100 mg — do not delay for confirmatory testing when the clinical picture is compelling.
takeaway: Adrenal crisis requires immediate IV hydrocortisone 100 mg — do not delay for confirmatory testing when the clinical picture is compelling.

visualRationale.title: Adrenal crisis: steroid stress response failure
visualRationale.caption: Cortisol of 3 mcg/dL in a peri-operative patient in shock means adrenal crisis — treat now.

stem: A 31-year-old female with uncontrolled Graves' disease is admitted post-partum with a Burch-Wartofsky score of 65. She has a temperature of 40.2°C, HR 148 with AF, BP 88/52, and altered mental status. What is the correct order of thyroid storm treatment steps?

options.text: Radioactive iodine ablation → beta-blockade → glucocorticoids → PTU.
PTU → iodine solution (SSKI/Lugol's) → glucocorticoids → beta-blockade → supportive care.
Iodine solution → PTU → glucocorticoids → beta-blockade → temperature control and supportive care.
Beta-blockade → then iodine solution → then PTU → then glucocorticoids.

rationale: The critical sequence in thyroid storm is: (1) PTU (propylthiouracil) first — blocks new thyroid hormone synthesis AND peripheral T4-to-T3 conversion; (2) iodine solution (SSKI or Lugol's) given at least 1 hour AFTER PTU to prevent iodine from being used as substrate; (3) hydrocortisone/dexamethasone to reduce peripheral T4-to-T3 conversion and treat relative adrenal insufficiency; (4) beta-blockade (propranolol preferred — also blocks T4-to-T3 conversion) for tachycardia and hemodynamic stabilization; (5) aggressive supportive care including cooling, IVF, and ICU monitoring.

distractorRationales.a: Radioactive iodine is absolutely contraindicated in thyroid storm — the radiation-induced thyroid destruction would release massive stored hormone and worsen the crisis.
distractorRationales.c: Giving iodine before PTU is a critical error — the excess iodine will be used by the gland to synthesize more hormone (Jod-Basedow effect) if synthesis is not first blocked.
distractorRationales.d: Beta-blockade before PTU delays the most critical intervention — blocking synthesis must come first, then iodine at least 1 hour later.

tags: Endocrine
Thyroid storm sequence: PTU first, iodine ≥ 1 hour later, then glucocorticoids and beta-blockade — never give iodine before PTU.
takeaway: Thyroid storm sequence: PTU first, iodine ≥ 1 hour later, then glucocorticoids and beta-blockade — never give iodine before PTU.

visualRationale.title: Thyroid storm treatment sequence: timing is everything
visualRationale.caption: Iodine given before PTU will worsen the crisis — block synthesis first.

stem: An ICU nurse has four patients. Which patient should be assessed first? (A) A 2-day post-CABG patient with stable vital signs asking for pain medication; (B) A septic patient with a new MAP of 58 who was 72 two hours ago; (C) A COPD patient on BiPAP whose SpO2 is 93% and is at their baseline; (D) A post-op appendectomy patient with a temperature of 38.2°C on post-op day 1.

options.text: Patient A — post-cardiac surgery patients require priority pain management to reduce myocardial oxygen demand.
Patient B — a drop in MAP to 58 in a septic patient represents acute hemodynamic deterioration requiring immediate assessment.
Patient C — SpO2 of 93% warrants concern even at COPD baseline.
Patient D — new fever on post-op day 1 may signal anastomotic leak requiring urgent intervention.

rationale: Patient B has the most acutely life-threatening finding — MAP of 58 (below the 65 mmHg target in sepsis) and a 14-point drop from baseline over 2 hours indicates escalating hemodynamic instability requiring immediate assessment and intervention. The ABCs of prioritization prioritize the patient with the most acute physiological decompensation. Septic shock with falling MAP carries immediate risk of end-organ failure and death.

distractorRationales.a: Patient A's pain is important but not life-threatening — stable vital signs place this patient lower in acuity.
distractorRationales.c: Patient C's SpO2 of 93% at their COPD baseline is expected and not an acute change — stable patients are not priority over decompensating ones.
distractorRationales.d: Patient D's fever on post-op day 1 is common and often benign (atelectasis, inflammatory response) — while it warrants assessment, it is not as immediately life-threatening as a dropping MAP in sepsis.

tags: Management/Prioritization
Prioritize the patient with acute physiological decompensation — a falling MAP below 65 in a septic patient signals immediate danger.
takeaway: Prioritize the patient with acute physiological decompensation — a falling MAP below 65 in a septic patient signals immediate danger.

visualRationale.title: Four-patient prioritization: acuity triage
visualRationale.caption: Rank by trajectory and immediate threat, not just abnormal values.
visualRationale.conclusion: Acute deterioration from a known baseline always outranks chronic abnormal values.

stem: An ICU RN is charge nurse during a code response. Which task is most appropriate to delegate to an unlicensed assistive personnel (UAP) during the code?

options.text: Interpreting the patient's cardiac rhythm and calling out the next defibrillation timing.
Performing chest compressions under direct supervision of the RN team leader.
Drawing up and preparing medication syringes from the crash cart.
Documenting ACLS medication dosing times and outcomes during the code.

rationale: Chest compressions are a task that falls within the scope of a UAP when performed under the direct supervision of a licensed nurse or physician in an emergency setting. UAPs are trained in CPR and can safely provide this physical task. The RN maintains clinical oversight and team leadership. Medication preparation, rhythm interpretation, and official code documentation require RN or higher clinical judgment and cannot be delegated to a UAP.

distractorRationales.a: Rhythm interpretation requires clinical knowledge and licensure — this is an RN or physician responsibility.
distractorRationales.c: Medication preparation during a code involves high-risk drug dosing calculations and must remain within RN scope of practice.
distractorRationales.d: Code documentation is a clinical and legal responsibility that requires a licensed nurse who understands the clinical context — UAPs should not complete official code records.

tags: Management/Prioritization
During a code, UAPs can perform CPR compressions under supervision — medication tasks and clinical interpretation remain within RN scope.
takeaway: During a code, UAPs can perform CPR compressions under supervision — medication tasks and clinical interpretation remain within RN scope.

visualRationale.title: Code team delegation: RN vs. UAP scope
visualRationale.caption: Effective delegation during emergencies keeps clinical roles aligned with licensure.
visualRationale.conclusion: Delegate physical tasks within UAP training; retain all clinical judgment tasks at the RN level.

stem: An ICU RN is completing a shift handoff for a patient with worsening respiratory status. Which SBAR component is missing from this report: 'Mr. K is a 66-year-old with COPD who came in for a COPD exacerbation two days ago. His SpO2 has been dropping from 95% to 88% over the last two hours and he's working harder to breathe. I need you to call the intensivist and consider BiPAP initiation.'

options.text: Situation — the nurse has not stated what is happening right now.
Background — the nurse has not provided the patient's relevant medical history.
Assessment — the nurse has not stated their clinical interpretation of the findings.
Recommendation — the nurse has not suggested a course of action.

rationale: The SBAR framework includes Situation (what is happening), Background (relevant history), Assessment (the nurse's clinical interpretation), and Recommendation (suggested action). In this report, the Situation (dropping SpO2, increased work of breathing) and Background (COPD, day 2) are present. A Recommendation is included (call intensivist, consider BiPAP). However, the Assessment — the nurse's own clinical interpretation of why this is happening and what they believe is occurring — is absent. For example: 'I believe he is having a COPD exacerbation flare with impending respiratory failure.'

distractorRationales.a: The Situation is clearly communicated — SpO2 dropping to 88% with increased work of breathing over 2 hours.
distractorRationales.b: Background is present — COPD patient, admitted 2 days ago for exacerbation.
distractorRationales.d: Recommendation is present — call the intensivist and consider BiPAP.

tags: Management/Prioritization
SBAR Assessment = the nurse's clinical interpretation — without it, the handoff lacks the provider's synthesis and can delay appropriate response.
takeaway: SBAR Assessment = the nurse's clinical interpretation — without it, the handoff lacks the provider's synthesis and can delay appropriate response.

visualRationale.title: SBAR framework: what's present vs. missing
visualRationale.caption: Structured handoff tools only work when all four components are deliberately included.
visualRationale.conclusion: Add: 'I believe he is having a COPD flare with impending respiratory failure requiring NIV.'

stem: A 58-year-old male with septic shock has a MAP of 56 on 0.25 mcg/kg/min of norepinephrine. He has a history of atrial fibrillation currently in AF with HR 128. The intensivist asks the nurse to add a second vasopressor. Which agent is most appropriate as adjunctive vasopressor therapy in this context?

options.text: Dopamine 5 mcg/kg/min — add for additional vasopressor effect and renal protection.
Vasopressin 0.03–0.04 units/min — adjunctive vasopressor that does not increase heart rate.
Epinephrine 0.1 mcg/kg/min — add for combined alpha and beta effects to support both pressure and HR.
Phenylephrine 1 mcg/kg/min — pure alpha agonist to elevate SVR without beta stimulation.

rationale: Vasopressin (AVP) is the recommended second-line vasopressor in septic shock (SSC guidelines) added to norepinephrine to achieve MAP goals while allowing norepinephrine sparing. Importantly, vasopressin acts on V1 receptors in vascular smooth muscle and does not have chronotropic effects, making it particularly beneficial in this patient with pre-existing atrial fibrillation and rate of 128 — adding vasopressin will not further accelerate the heart rate. It may also have a catecholamine-sparing effect.

distractorRationales.a: Dopamine is not recommended as a vasopressor in septic shock in most patients — it has a higher risk of arrhythmias, including AF, which would worsen this patient's tachycardia.
distractorRationales.c: Epinephrine's potent beta-1 stimulation would accelerate the already rapid AF rate and increase myocardial oxygen demand — not ideal in this context.
distractorRationales.d: Phenylephrine is a reasonable vasopressor but it can reflexively slow the heart rate via baroreceptor activation, potentially worsening AF-related hemodynamic compromise if rate decreases below adequate ventricular filling time; vasopressin has a cleaner profile here.

tags: Pharmacological
Vasopressin is the preferred second vasopressor in septic shock — it raises MAP without increasing heart rate, making it ideal when tachyarrhythmia is a concern.
takeaway: Vasopressin is the preferred second vasopressor in septic shock — it raises MAP without increasing heart rate, making it ideal when tachyarrhythmia is a concern.

visualRationale.title: Second vasopressor selection in septic shock + AF
visualRationale.caption: Rate-neutral vasopressor therapy protects hemodynamics without worsening arrhythmia burden.
visualRationale.conclusion: SSC guidelines support vasopressin as second-line to norepinephrine for MAP goal attainment.

stem: A 64-year-old post-orthopedic surgery patient has been on IV heparin for 7 days for DVT prophylaxis. Platelet count has dropped from 234,000 to 68,000 over the last 48 hours. She has a new right popliteal vein thrombosis noted on duplex scan. The 4T score is calculated at 7. What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Increase the heparin infusion rate — the platelet drop may be from the DVT itself consuming platelets.
Stop heparin immediately and initiate a non-heparin anticoagulant such as argatroban or fondaparinux.
Switch to low molecular weight heparin (enoxaparin) — it carries a lower risk of HIT than unfractionated heparin.
Transfuse platelets to > 100,000 and continue heparin with close monitoring.

rationale: A 4T score of 7 indicates high probability of heparin-induced thrombocytopenia (HIT). HIT is a prothrombotic condition caused by immune-mediated platelet activation against PF4-heparin complexes, causing paradoxical thrombosis despite low platelets. All heparin products must be stopped immediately, including line flushes and heparin-coated catheters. A non-heparin anticoagulant (argatroban — direct thrombin inhibitor, or fondaparinux — factor Xa inhibitor) must be initiated immediately because the thrombotic risk in HIT is substantial. Warfarin is not started until platelet count recovers above 150,000.

distractorRationales.a: Continuing heparin in HIT is contraindicated — it will perpetuate platelet activation and dramatically increase the thrombotic burden.
distractorRationales.c: LMWH cross-reacts with HIT antibodies and is also contraindicated — switching from UFH to LMWH does not resolve the immune mechanism.
distractorRationales.d: Platelet transfusion in HIT is generally contraindicated — it provides substrate for continued PF4-heparin immune complex formation and may worsen thrombosis.

tags: Pharmacological
4T score ≥ 6 means high-probability HIT — stop ALL heparin immediately and start a non-heparin anticoagulant; LMWH also cross-reacts and must be avoided.
takeaway: 4T score ≥ 6 means high-probability HIT — stop ALL heparin immediately and start a non-heparin anticoagulant; LMWH also cross-reacts and must be avoided.

visualRationale.title: HIT: platelet drop + new clot = stop heparin now
visualRationale.caption: HIT is paradoxically thrombotic — the platelet drop signals clot risk, not bleeding risk.

stem: A DKA patient has been on an insulin infusion at 6 units/hr for 3 hours. Current glucose is 248 mg/dL (down from 524). Anion gap is now 18. The patient's urine output has been 280 mL over the last hour. What is the most appropriate next action regarding insulin and fluid management?

options.text: Stop the insulin infusion — glucose has improved and the patient no longer needs insulin.
Add dextrose to the IV fluid and continue the insulin infusion to allow ongoing anion gap closure.
Reduce the insulin infusion to 1 unit/hr and switch to oral fluids.
Administer subcutaneous insulin and discontinue the drip immediately to prevent rebound ketoacidosis.

rationale: In DKA management, the insulin infusion must continue until the anion gap closes (target < 12 mEq/L), not just until glucose reaches a threshold. When glucose falls below 200–250 mg/dL with the anion gap still elevated (18 mEq/L here), dextrose (D5W or D10W) is added to the IV fluid while the insulin continues at 0.05–0.1 units/kg/hr. Stopping insulin prematurely would halt ketoacid clearance and risk rebound ketoacidosis. The infusion is only discontinued after gap closure, an adequate oral intake, and a successful subcutaneous insulin transition overlap.

distractorRationales.a: Stopping insulin based on glucose alone in DKA is incorrect — the anion gap of 18 confirms ongoing ketoacidosis requiring continued insulin.
distractorRationales.c: Reducing insulin to 1 unit/hr without adding dextrose risks hypoglycemia, and switching to oral fluids is premature with an open anion gap.
distractorRationales.d: Transitioning to subcutaneous insulin before gap closure causes rebound ketoacidosis — the subcutaneous-to-drip overlap requires gap closure first.

tags: Pharmacological
In DKA, when glucose drops to 200–250 mg/dL but the anion gap is still open, add dextrose to the fluid and keep the insulin infusion running until gap closure.
takeaway: In DKA, when glucose drops to 200–250 mg/dL but the anion gap is still open, add dextrose to the fluid and keep the insulin infusion running until gap closure.

visualRationale.title: DKA endpoint: close the gap, not just the glucose
visualRationale.caption: Glucose improvement is early — anion gap closure is the true treatment endpoint.

metrics.label: Action
metrics.directionLabel: protect from hypoglycemia while closing gap

stem: A 78-year-old male post-stroke patient with left-sided weakness and urinary urgency is assessed by the ICU nurse. His Morse Fall Scale score is 65. He is alert and oriented but impulsive. Which intervention is the most important safety measure to implement?

options.text: Place the patient in a vest restraint to prevent unsupervised ambulation.
Ensure the bed alarm is active, the call light is within reach, and institute hourly rounding with toileting assistance.
Instruct the patient to call for help before getting out of bed and document the education.
Place the bed in the highest position to facilitate nurse transfer assistance.

rationale: A Morse Fall Scale score of 65 places this patient in the high-fall-risk category (≥ 45). For an impulsive patient with motor deficits and urinary urgency, a multi-component intervention is required: active bed/chair alarm, call light accessibility, low bed position, non-skid footwear, adequate lighting, and proactive hourly rounding with scheduled toileting to address urinary urgency before the patient attempts to self-transfer. Physical restraints are a last resort and can increase injury risk from struggling.

distractorRationales.a: Physical restraints are not a first-line fall prevention measure — they can worsen agitation and injury risk, and are associated with deconditioning and psychological harm.
distractorRationales.c: Education alone is insufficient for an impulsive patient with motor deficits — cognitive impulsivity means the patient may not follow the instruction despite understanding it.
distractorRationales.d: The bed should be kept in its LOWEST position — a high bed dramatically increases fall injury severity if the patient attempts to exit unassisted.

tags: Safety
High fall risk requires multi-component interventions — bed alarm, low bed, hourly rounding with toileting, and call light access are the evidence-based core bundle.
takeaway: High fall risk requires multi-component interventions — bed alarm, low bed, hourly rounding with toileting, and call light access are the evidence-based core bundle.

visualRationale.title: Fall prevention bundle for high Morse score patients
visualRationale.caption: No single intervention prevents falls — bundle them and address the root cause (urgency).
visualRationale.conclusion: Proactive toileting is the most underutilized and effective fall prevention intervention in elderly patients with urgency.

stem: During medication administration, an ICU nurse is about to hang a new bag of potassium chloride 40 mEq in 100 mL to infuse over one hour. The patient's most recent potassium was 3.1 mEq/L. Before infusing, which safety check is the highest priority?

options.text: Verify the patient's allergy list for documented potassium allergy.
Confirm central venous access — concentrated potassium (> 10 mEq/hr) must not infuse through a peripheral IV.
Double-check the infusion pump rate calculation against the pharmacy label.
Assess the patient for signs of hyperkalemia before adding more potassium.

rationale: Concentrated potassium chloride solutions (> 10 mEq/hr or concentrations > 10 mEq/100 mL) are high-alert medications that can cause cardiac arrest if infused too rapidly or via the wrong route. KCl 40 mEq in 100 mL infused over 1 hour = 40 mEq/hr, which exceeds the safe peripheral infusion threshold. This concentration must only be administered through a confirmed central venous catheter — peripheral infusion of this concentration causes severe vein sclerosis, extravasation injury, and can cause fatal cardiac arrhythmias if given as an unintended IV push.

distractorRationales.a: True potassium allergy is essentially nonexistent — potassium is an electrolyte, not an allergen, and this check would not be meaningful.
distractorRationales.c: Pump rate verification is important but secondary to the more immediately dangerous route-of-administration error.
distractorRationales.d: Checking for hyperkalemia is relevant before any potassium replacement, but the patient's level of 3.1 already indicates hypokalemia — the access site safety check is the priority before initiating infusion.

tags: Safety
KCl > 10 mEq/hr must infuse through a central line only — peripheral infusion of concentrated potassium is a high-alert medication error risk.
takeaway: KCl > 10 mEq/hr must infuse through a central line only — peripheral infusion of concentrated potassium is a high-alert medication error risk.

visualRationale.title: Concentrated potassium: high-alert IV medication safety
visualRationale.caption: Route of administration is the most critical safety check for concentrated KCl.
visualRationale.conclusion: Confirm central access before any KCl infusion > 10 mEq/hr — this is a Joint Commission NPSG.

stem: A physician calls the ICU at 0300 to give a verbal order for metoprolol tartrate 25 mg PO every 6 hours for a patient with new-onset atrial fibrillation. The night nurse writes the order. Which action best reflects evidence-based safe communication practice?

options.text: Accept the verbal order and document it in the chart immediately without delay.
Ask the physician to call back during business hours to enter the order electronically.
Repeat the order back to the physician completely and obtain verbal confirmation before documenting and executing.
Ask a second nurse to witness the verbal order and co-sign the documentation.

rationale: The read-back process is a Joint Commission National Patient Safety Goal requirement for verbal and telephone orders. The nurse must write down the complete order, read it back in full to the provider, and receive explicit confirmation before executing or documenting the order as verified. This closed-loop communication catches transcription errors, dose misunderstandings, and wrong-patient orders. For high-alert medications like beta-blockers in patients with compromised cardiac function, read-back is especially critical.

distractorRationales.a: Accepting and documenting without read-back violates JCAHO NPSG requirements and misses an important error-catching step.
distractorRationales.b: Deferring acute cardiac rate-control orders until business hours is clinically inappropriate — new-onset AF requires prompt management regardless of time.
distractorRationales.d: A co-signing witness is not the primary safety mechanism for verbal orders — read-back with confirmation is the required standard, and a witness does not replace it.

tags: Safety
All verbal and telephone orders require immediate read-back and explicit provider confirmation before execution — this is a Joint Commission NPSG.
takeaway: All verbal and telephone orders require immediate read-back and explicit provider confirmation before execution — this is a Joint Commission NPSG.

visualRationale.title: Closed-loop communication: verbal order safety cycle
visualRationale.caption: Read-back closes the communication loop and catches errors before they reach the patient.

stem: A 44-year-old male with severe anemia (Hgb 5.2 g/dL) after a GI bleed has the following values: SaO2 98%, PaO2 96 mmHg, HR 118, BP 102/64, ScvO2 58%. He is not actively bleeding. Which concept best explains the ScvO2 of 58% despite a normal SaO2?

options.text: The patient has pulmonary venous admixture causing poor oxygen loading at the alveolar level.
Tissues are extracting a greater proportion of delivered oxygen because oxygen delivery is inadequate due to low hemoglobin content.
ScvO2 of 58% reflects normal tissue oxygen consumption for a resting patient.
The low ScvO2 is caused by excessive cardiac output increasing oxygen demand at the tissue level.

rationale: Oxygen delivery (DO2) = CO × (Hgb × 1.34 × SaO2 + 0.003 × PaO2). Despite a normal SaO2 and PaO2, the critically low hemoglobin (5.2 g/dL) severely limits DO2 — the oxygen-carrying capacity is the limiting variable, not the pulmonary function. When DO2 falls below metabolic demand, tissues compensate by extracting a higher percentage of delivered oxygen (increased O2 extraction ratio), which is reflected in a falling ScvO2. Normal ScvO2 is 70–75%; a value of 58% confirms global tissue oxygen debt despite adequate alveolar gas exchange.

distractorRationales.a: Pulmonary venous admixture would produce a low SaO2 — this patient's SaO2 of 98% confirms normal oxygen loading at the lung, making this explanation incorrect.
distractorRationales.c: ScvO2 of 58% is below the normal threshold of 70% — this value indicates inadequate oxygen delivery relative to demand, not a normal state.
distractorRationales.d: Elevated CO does not increase tissue O2 demand — it would actually raise ScvO2 by delivering more oxygen per unit time; this interpretation is physiologically reversed.

tags: Physiological Adaptation
Normal SaO2 with low ScvO2 means the lungs are working but oxygen delivery is inadequate — check hemoglobin and cardiac output as the delivery variables.
takeaway: Normal SaO2 with low ScvO2 means the lungs are working but oxygen delivery is inadequate — check hemoglobin and cardiac output as the delivery variables.

visualRationale.title: DO2 components: why SaO2 is normal but ScvO2 is low
visualRationale.caption: Hemoglobin content drives oxygen delivery more than SaO2 — a normal saturation on minimal Hgb still means insufficient DO2.
visualRationale.conclusion: Transfuse to target Hgb ≥ 7–8 g/dL to restore DO2 and normalize tissue oxygen extraction.

stem: A patient with ARDS is on volume-controlled ventilation with the following settings: FiO2 0.80, PEEP 10 cmH2O, tidal volume (TV) 480 mL, RR 22. The patient weighs 60 kg (ideal body weight). ABGs: pH 7.28, PaCO2 52 mmHg, PaO2 58 mmHg, SpO2 88%. The intensivist asks the nurse to assist in adjusting ventilator settings per the ARDSnet protocol. Which change is MOST consistent with lung-protective ventilation?

options.text: Increase tidal volume to 600 mL to improve CO2 clearance
Reduce tidal volume to 360 mL (6 mL/kg IBW) and increase RR to 28
Reduce PEEP to 5 cmH2O to decrease barotrauma risk
Increase FiO2 to 1.0 and decrease RR to 18 to allow permissive hypercapnia

rationale: The ARDSnet protocol mandates a tidal volume of 4-8 mL/kg ideal body weight (IBW), with a target of 6 mL/kg IBW. For a 60 kg IBW patient, TV = 360 mL. The current TV of 480 mL (8 mL/kg) is at the upper limit; reducing to 6 mL/kg is the priority lung-protective change. Increasing RR to compensate for reduced TV is appropriate to maintain minute ventilation and manage the pH 7.28 (permissive hypercapnia is acceptable down to pH 7.20 in ARDSnet). Increasing TV violates lung protection. Reducing PEEP worsens oxygenation in ARDS. Maximizing FiO2 without adjusting TV does not address barotrauma/volutrauma.
tags: Respiratory / Ventilator Management
ARDSnet lung protection: TV 4-8 mL/kg IBW (target 6), plateau pressure ≤ 30 cmH2O, adequate PEEP to maintain oxygenation, permissive hypercapnia allowed to pH ≥ 7.20.
takeaway: ARDSnet lung protection: TV 4-8 mL/kg IBW (target 6), plateau pressure ≤ 30 cmH2O, adequate PEEP to maintain oxygenation, permissive hypercapnia allowed to pH ≥ 7.20.

visualRationale.title: ARDSnet lung-protective ventilation targets
visualRationale.caption: Lower tidal volumes prevent volutrauma. Permissive hypercapnia is acceptable to protect the lung.

metrics.label: Plateau pressure
metrics.directionLabel: safety ceiling — check with inspiratory hold

visualRationale.conclusion: Lung protection = lower TV + controlled Pplat ≤ 30 + adequate PEEP. Accept pH ≥ 7.20.

stem: A 68-year-old patient with COPD is admitted for acute exacerbation. ABGs on 2L nasal cannula: pH 7.32, PaCO2 62 mmHg, HCO3 30 mEq/L, PaO2 55 mmHg. Which interpretation and priority action is MOST appropriate?

options.text: Respiratory alkalosis with metabolic compensation; increase O2 to 5L/min
Respiratory acidosis with metabolic compensation (chronic); initiate non-invasive positive pressure ventilation (NIPPV) and notify provider
Metabolic alkalosis with respiratory compensation; administer sodium bicarbonate
Mixed acidosis; prepare for emergent intubation

rationale: pH 7.32 (acidotic), PaCO2 62 (elevated — respiratory acidosis), HCO3 30 (elevated — compensatory metabolic alkalosis). The elevated bicarbonate indicates chronic CO2 retention with renal compensation — classic COPD pattern. The primary problem is respiratory acidosis. With pH 7.32 and PaO2 55 mmHg, this patient meets criteria for NIPPV (BiPAP) which can reduce work of breathing, improve ventilation, and avoid intubation. High-flow O2 must be used cautiously in COPD to avoid eliminating the hypoxic drive, though SpO2 target of 88-92% is acceptable. Sodium bicarb is not indicated. Emergent intubation is not yet warranted with this pH.
tags: Respiratory / ABG Interpretation

In COPD exacerbation with respiratory acidosis and elevated HCO3: the compensation is chronic. Treat with NIPPV (BiPAP) first. Target SpO2 88-92% to preserve hypoxic drive. Intubation is a last resort.
takeaway: In COPD exacerbation with respiratory acidosis and elevated HCO3: the compensation is chronic. Treat with NIPPV (BiPAP) first. Target SpO2 88-92% to preserve hypoxic drive. Intubation is a last resort.

visualRationale.title: COPD exacerbation ABG: chronic respiratory acidosis
visualRationale.caption: Elevated HCO3 = renal compensation = chronic CO2 retention. This is not an acute emergency pattern alone.
visualRationale.conclusion: Chronic respiratory acidosis. Treat: NIPPV (BiPAP), target SpO2 88-92%, avoid high-flow O2.

stem: A nurse is caring for a mechanically ventilated patient in the ICU. The high-pressure ventilator alarm suddenly activates. The nurse notices the patient is agitated, breath sounds are absent on the left, the trachea appears deviated to the right, and SpO2 is dropping from 97% to 84%. What is the PRIORITY nursing action?

options.text: Increase FiO2 to 1.0 and suction the endotracheal tube
Disconnect the patient from the ventilator and manually bag-ventilate while calling for immediate provider assistance
Administer a sedation bolus to decrease patient agitation and airway resistance
Obtain a portable chest X-ray to confirm suspected pneumothorax

rationale: Absent unilateral breath sounds, tracheal deviation, agitation, high-pressure alarm, and acute SpO2 decline are classic signs of tension pneumothorax — a life-threatening emergency. The tracheal deviation away from the affected side indicates mediastinal shift with cardiovascular compromise imminent. The immediate nursing priority is to manually ventilate (hand-bagging) to assess and temporarily manage oxygenation while the provider is called for emergent needle decompression. Suctioning will not address pneumothorax. Increasing FiO2 alone does not treat the mechanical obstruction. Sedation is inappropriate. An X-ray delays definitive treatment when the patient is hemodynamically compromised — tension pneumothorax is a clinical diagnosis.
tags: Respiratory / Airway Management

Tension pneumothorax triad in ventilated patients: absent unilateral breath sounds + tracheal deviation (away from affected side) + hemodynamic deterioration. Disconnect and bag → call provider → emergent needle decompression. Do NOT delay for X-ray.
takeaway: Tension pneumothorax triad in ventilated patients: absent unilateral breath sounds + tracheal deviation (away from affected side) + hemodynamic deterioration. Disconnect and bag → call provider → emergent needle decompression. Do NOT delay for X-ray.

visualRationale.title: Tension pneumothorax: recognize and act
visualRationale.caption: Clinical diagnosis — do NOT wait for X-ray when the patient is decompensating on the vent.

nodes.label: High-pressure vent alarm + unilateral absent breath sounds
Tracheal deviation away from affected side
Emergent needle decompression
nodes.value: 2nd ICS, MCL → chest tube for definitive treatment

visualRationale.conclusion: Tension PTX = clinical diagnosis. Act before imaging. Every minute delays risks cardiac arrest.

stem: A patient has been mechanically ventilated for 5 days following ARDS. The provider orders a spontaneous breathing trial (SBT). Which assessment finding would indicate the patient has PASSED the SBT and is ready for extubation consideration?

options.text: RR 30/min, SpO2 90% on FiO2 0.40, patient using significant accessory muscles at 30 minutes
RR 18/min, SpO2 96% on FiO2 0.40, TV 450 mL, minimal accessory muscle use, alert and cooperative after 30 minutes
RR 24/min, SpO2 94%, patient becomes diaphoretic at 20 minutes; SBT stopped early
RR 14/min, SpO2 98%, but patient has copious secretions requiring suctioning every 5 minutes

rationale: A successful SBT is characterized by: RR < 25/min, SpO2 ≥ 90-95%, adequate tidal volume (typically > 4-5 mL/kg IBW), absence of significant respiratory distress (no accessory muscle use, diaphoresis, or agitation), and hemodynamic stability for at least 30 minutes. Option B meets all criteria. Option A has tachypnea and accessory muscle use — SBT failure. Option C required early termination due to distress — SBT failure. Option D: despite adequate ventilatory parameters, copious secretions requiring q5min suctioning indicate inability to protect/clear the airway — this is a separate extubation contraindication.
tags: Respiratory / Weaning and Extubation

SBT success criteria: RR < 25, SpO2 ≥ 90%, adequate TV, no accessory muscle use, hemodynamically stable, tolerates 30 minutes. Passing the SBT is necessary but not sufficient — also assess cough strength and secretion management before extubation.
takeaway: SBT success criteria: RR < 25, SpO2 ≥ 90%, adequate TV, no accessory muscle use, hemodynamically stable, tolerates 30 minutes. Passing the SBT is necessary but not sufficient — also assess cough strength and secretion management before extubation.

stem: A 55-year-old patient presents with acute respiratory failure. ABGs: pH 7.22, PaCO2 35 mmHg, HCO3 14 mEq/L, PaO2 88 mmHg on 4L NC. Lactic acid is 6.8 mmol/L. What type of respiratory failure is present and what is the underlying mechanism?

options.text: Type I (hypoxemic) respiratory failure due to ventilation-perfusion mismatch
Type II (hypercapnic) respiratory failure due to pump failure
Type I respiratory failure with a superimposed metabolic acidosis due to tissue hypoperfusion
Pure metabolic acidosis with appropriate respiratory compensation; no respiratory failure

rationale: PaO2 88 mmHg on 4L NC is hypoxemic relative to O2 delivery (P:F ratio would require full FiO2 calculation, but supplemental O2 requirement establishes Type I failure). The pH 7.22 (acidotic), PaCO2 35 (normal-low — not retaining CO2), HCO3 14 (low — metabolic acidosis) pattern indicates a metabolic acidosis with partial respiratory compensation (lungs are blowing off CO2 to compensate). Lactic acid 6.8 signals severe tissue hypoperfusion/anaerobic metabolism — likely septic or cardiogenic shock. This is Type I respiratory failure (oxygenation failure from parenchymal disease or V/Q mismatch) superimposed on a high anion gap metabolic acidosis from lactic acidosis.
tags: Respiratory / Respiratory Failure

Classify respiratory failure: Type I = hypoxemic (PaO2 low, PaCO2 normal/low); Type II = hypercapnic (PaCO2 high, pH low). Metabolic acidosis can coexist — always check pH, HCO3, lactate, and anion gap together.
takeaway: Classify respiratory failure: Type I = hypoxemic (PaO2 low, PaCO2 normal/low); Type II = hypercapnic (PaCO2 high, pH low). Metabolic acidosis can coexist — always check pH, HCO3, lactate, and anion gap together.

visualRationale.title: Type I vs. Type II respiratory failure
visualRationale.caption: Classify by which failure dominates: oxygenation (gas exchange) or ventilation (pump).
visualRationale.conclusion: High PaCO2 + low pH = Type II. Low PaO2 with normal PaCO2 = Type I. Both can coexist.

stem: A mechanically ventilated patient develops sudden desaturation (SpO2 90% → 78%). The nurse performs the DOPE mnemonic assessment. Which finding would indicate the problem is 'Obstruction'?

options.text: The ETT has migrated and breath sounds are unilateral
A mucus plug is visualized in the ETT and resolved after suctioning
The ventilator circuit is disconnected at the Y-piece
A new pneumothorax is identified on chest X-ray

rationale: The DOPE mnemonic for acute ventilator desaturation: D = Displacement (ETT migration/dislodgement), O = Obstruction (mucus plug, biting ETT, kinked tubing), P = Pneumothorax, E = Equipment failure. A mucus plug visualized and cleared by suctioning is the classic example of Obstruction. ETT migration causing unilateral breath sounds = Displacement. Disconnected circuit = Equipment failure. Pneumothorax on X-ray = Pneumothorax. Systematically applying DOPE ensures the cause is identified and corrected quickly.
tags: Respiratory / Ventilator Management

DOPE mnemonic for vent desaturation: Displacement → Obstruction → Pneumothorax → Equipment. Always disconnect and hand-bag if uncertain — this immediately tests for equipment failure and buys time while assessing the other categories.
takeaway: DOPE mnemonic for vent desaturation: Displacement → Obstruction → Pneumothorax → Equipment. Always disconnect and hand-bag if uncertain — this immediately tests for equipment failure and buys time while assessing the other categories.

visualRationale.title: DOPE mnemonic: acute vent desaturation
visualRationale.caption: Systematic four-cause framework prevents missing the diagnosis during crisis.

nodes.label: D — Displacement
nodes.value: ETT migrated or dislodged → check position, capnography

nodes.label: O — Obstruction
nodes.value: Mucus plug, biting, kinked circuit → suction ETT

nodes.label: P — Pneumothorax
nodes.value: Absent breath sounds, tracheal deviation → needle decompression

nodes.label: E — Equipment failure
nodes.value: Disconnection or malfunction → disconnect and bag manually

visualRationale.conclusion: Disconnect and bag FIRST if uncertain — immediately tests E while managing oxygenation.

stem: A patient with severe ARDS has a PaO2/FiO2 (P:F ratio) of 82 despite FiO2 0.80 and PEEP 14 cmH2O. The intensivist considers prone positioning. Which nursing assessment finding is a CONTRAINDICATION to prone positioning?

options.text: SpO2 88% on current settings
Unstable spinal fracture identified on imaging
BMI of 36
Recent central line placement 48 hours ago

rationale: Prone positioning significantly improves oxygenation in severe ARDS (P:F < 150) by recruiting dorsal lung segments and redistributing perfusion. Contraindications include: unstable spinal fractures (risk of cord injury with turning), open abdominal wounds, hemodynamic instability not responsive to vasopressors, elevated ICP, and recent tracheal surgery. An unstable spinal fracture is an absolute contraindication. Low SpO2 (88%) is the indication FOR proning — it is why the intervention is being considered. Obesity (BMI 36) requires additional staff and planning but is not a contraindication. A 48-hour-old central line is appropriate — lines must be secured but do not contraindicate proning.
tags: Respiratory / ARDS Management

Prone positioning indications: severe ARDS (P:F < 150) on optimal vent settings. Key absolute contraindications: unstable spinal injury, open abdomen, uncontrolled elevated ICP. Lines and tubes can be managed — the spine cannot be compromised.
takeaway: Prone positioning indications: severe ARDS (P:F < 150) on optimal vent settings. Key absolute contraindications: unstable spinal injury, open abdomen, uncontrolled elevated ICP. Lines and tubes can be managed — the spine cannot be compromised.

visualRationale.title: Prone positioning decision gate
visualRationale.caption: Severe ARDS benefits from proning — but check absolute contraindications first.

nodes.label: P:F ratio < 150 despite FiO2 >0.60 and PEEP ≥ 5?
Check absolute contraindications
nodes.value: Unstable spine, open abdomen, uncontrolled ICP

nodes.label: Absolute contraindication present?
nodes.value: → do NOT prone — consider alternative rescue strategies

visualRationale.conclusion: Unstable spinal injury = absolute contraindication. Lines and tubes are manageable. Spine is not.

stem: A nurse is caring for a patient who was extubated 3 hours ago following a 6-day intubation. The patient is now becoming increasingly stridorous, with a RR of 32 and SpO2 declining to 91%. What is the priority intervention?

options.text: Apply a non-rebreather mask at 15L/min and notify the provider
Administer nebulized racemic epinephrine and place the patient upright, call provider immediately, and prepare for possible re-intubation
Encourage the patient to take slow, deep breaths to reduce stridor
Obtain a throat culture and start empiric antibiotics for suspected laryngeal infection

rationale: Post-extubation stridor indicates laryngeal or subglottic edema, which is a medical emergency with risk of complete airway obstruction. Racemic epinephrine via nebulization is the first-line pharmacologic treatment (causes vasoconstriction, reduces mucosal edema). The patient should be sitting upright to maximize airway diameter. Immediate provider notification is mandatory, as re-intubation may be needed urgently if stridor worsens or SpO2 does not improve. Applying a non-rebreather alone treats oxygenation but not the obstructive cause. Breathing exercises are insufficient for stridor. Antibiotics are not indicated for post-extubation edema.
tags: Respiratory / Airway / Escalation

Post-extubation stridor = laryngeal edema. Treat with racemic epinephrine nebulization + upright positioning + immediate provider call. Re-intubation equipment must be at bedside. Heliox (helium-oxygen) is an alternative bridge. Act fast — airway edema can close completely.
takeaway: Post-extubation stridor = laryngeal edema. Treat with racemic epinephrine nebulization + upright positioning + immediate provider call. Re-intubation equipment must be at bedside. Heliox (helium-oxygen) is an alternative bridge. Act fast — airway edema can close completely.

stem: A nurse is triaging three ICU patients with respiratory deterioration. Which patient requires intervention FIRST?

options.text: Patient A: pH 7.30, PaCO2 55, PaO2 72 on 4L NC; alert, able to speak full sentences
Patient B: RR 36/min, paradoxical abdominal movement, SpO2 84% on BiPAP, increasing somnolence over the last 30 minutes
Patient C: SpO2 93% on 2L NC, new productive cough, temperature 38.8°C
Patient D: pH 7.35, PaCO2 48, PaO2 80 on Venturi mask at 40% FiO2; clinically stable

rationale: Patient B is in acute respiratory failure with impending arrest: SpO2 84% despite BiPAP (indicating BiPAP failure), RR 36 with paradoxical breathing (a sign of fatigue and imminent respiratory arrest), and progressive somnolence (altered mental status indicating CO2 narcosis or cerebral hypoxia). This patient requires immediate escalation — likely emergent intubation. Patient A is compensating and able to maintain conversation (not yet critical). Patient C has a mild to moderate illness (pneumonia) with manageable oxygenation. Patient D is stable on supplemental O2 with a nearly normal pH.
tags: Respiratory / Respiratory Failure / Prioritization

Paradoxical abdominal movement + somnolence + SpO2 < 88% despite BiPAP = impending respiratory arrest. Escalate to intubation immediately. Fatigue signs (paradoxical breathing) and mental status change override absolute numbers.
takeaway: Paradoxical abdominal movement + somnolence + SpO2 < 88% despite BiPAP = impending respiratory arrest. Escalate to intubation immediately. Fatigue signs (paradoxical breathing) and mental status change override absolute numbers.

stem: A mechanically ventilated ARDS patient has the following: TV 420 mL (6 mL/kg IBW 70 kg), PEEP 12, RR 24, FiO2 0.65. During an inspiratory hold maneuver, the plateau pressure (Pplat) is 34 cmH2O. Which intervention is MOST appropriate?

options.text: Decrease TV to 4 mL/kg IBW (280 mL) and accept permissive hypercapnia
Increase PEEP to 18 cmH2O to improve oxygenation
Increase RR to 30 to compensate for high plateau pressure
Add a recruitment maneuver to open collapsed alveoli

rationale: The ARDSnet protocol sets a plateau pressure ceiling of ≤ 30 cmH2O. A Pplat of 34 cmH2O exceeds the safe limit and indicates overdistension risk (barotrauma/volutrauma). When Pplat > 30, the protocol directs reduction of TV by 1 mL/kg IBW until Pplat ≤ 30 or TV reaches 4 mL/kg minimum. Permissive hypercapnia (pH ≥ 7.20) is acceptable to achieve this. Increasing PEEP when Pplat is already high would worsen overdistension. Increasing RR does not directly reduce Pplat. Recruitment maneuvers are considered when Pplat is acceptable and oxygenation remains inadequate — not when pressure is already too high.
tags: Respiratory / Ventilator / Plateau Pressure

Pplat > 30 cmH2O in ARDS = reduce TV (down to 4 mL/kg IBW minimum). Permissive hypercapnia is acceptable. Pplat is the gold standard for assessing static compliance and overdistension risk — check it with every significant vent change.
takeaway: Pplat > 30 cmH2O in ARDS = reduce TV (down to 4 mL/kg IBW minimum). Permissive hypercapnia is acceptable. Pplat is the gold standard for assessing static compliance and overdistension risk — check it with every significant vent change.

visualRationale.title: Plateau pressure > 30: step down TV
visualRationale.caption: Pplat reflects static lung distension — exceeding 30 cmH2O signals barotrauma/volutrauma risk.

metrics.label: TV reduction
metrics.value: step down 1 mL/kg at a time

metrics.label: Permissive hypercapnia
metrics.directionLabel: accept CO2 rise to protect the lung

visualRationale.conclusion: Reduce TV stepwise until Pplat ≤ 30 cmH2O. Never increase PEEP when Pplat is already high.

stem: A 28-year-old with type 1 diabetes is admitted with DKA. Labs: glucose 520 mg/dL, pH 7.18, HCO3 10 mEq/L, potassium 3.2 mEq/L, sodium 132 mEq/L. The provider orders insulin infusion. What is the PRIORITY nursing action BEFORE starting the insulin infusion?

options.text: Administer sodium bicarbonate to correct the acidosis first
Replace potassium to at least 3.5 mEq/L before initiating insulin
Begin the insulin infusion at the ordered rate and monitor glucose hourly
Administer hypertonic saline to correct the hyponatremia first

rationale: In DKA, insulin drives potassium intracellularly, rapidly dropping serum potassium. If insulin is initiated when potassium is already < 3.5 mEq/L, the patient is at significant risk for life-threatening hypokalemia (cardiac arrhythmias, cardiac arrest). The standard DKA protocol mandates that potassium be replaced and confirmed ≥ 3.5 mEq/L before insulin is started. Sodium bicarbonate is not recommended for DKA unless pH < 6.9 and is used cautiously; it does not address the underlying insulin deficiency and can paradoxically worsen cellular acidosis. Hypertonic saline is not indicated — corrected sodium and isotonic fluids are used. Beginning insulin without addressing the hypokalemia is dangerous.
tags: Endocrine / DKA Management

DKA protocol gate: K+ must be ≥ 3.5 mEq/L before insulin is started. Insulin shifts K+ intracellularly. Starting insulin with K+ < 3.5 can cause fatal hypokalemia. Replace K+ → start insulin → monitor glucose q1h.
takeaway: DKA protocol gate: K+ must be ≥ 3.5 mEq/L before insulin is started. Insulin shifts K+ intracellularly. Starting insulin with K+ < 3.5 can cause fatal hypokalemia. Replace K+ → start insulin → monitor glucose q1h.

visualRationale.title: DKA protocol gate: K+ before insulin
visualRationale.caption: Insulin drives K+ into cells — starting it with low potassium risks fatal arrhythmia.
visualRationale.conclusion: K+ must be ≥ 3.5 mEq/L before insulin. No exceptions in DKA.

stem: A 76-year-old with type 2 diabetes presents with altered mental status and glucose of 890 mg/dL. ABGs: pH 7.38, HCO3 24 mEq/L, serum osmolality 348 mOsm/kg. Which condition does this BEST represent and what is the priority treatment?

options.text: DKA — start insulin infusion at 0.1 units/kg/hr immediately
Hyperosmolar hyperglycemic state (HHS) — aggressive IV fluid resuscitation is the priority intervention
SIADH — restrict fluids and correct hyponatremia
DKA — administer sodium bicarbonate and insulin concurrently

rationale: HHS is characterized by severe hyperglycemia (> 600 mg/dL), hyperosmolality (> 320 mOsm/kg), minimal or absent ketoacidosis (pH > 7.30, HCO3 > 18), and significant mental status changes correlating with osmolality. This patient has glucose 890, osmolality 348, normal pH and HCO3 — classic HHS, not DKA. HHS is driven by severe dehydration and osmotic diuresis. The priority treatment is aggressive IV fluid resuscitation (typically 1-1.5L NS over the first hour, then adjusted). Insulin is used more cautiously in HHS — rapid glucose lowering without adequate fluid can precipitate hypotension. SIADH causes hyponatremia with low osmolality — the opposite pattern.
tags: Endocrine / HHS vs. DKA Differentiation

HHS vs. DKA: HHS = very high glucose + very high osmolality + NO acidosis. DKA = glucose typically < 800 + acidosis + ketones. HHS priority = FLUIDS first; DKA priority = insulin after K+ correction. Both are life-threatening.
takeaway: HHS vs. DKA: HHS = very high glucose + very high osmolality + NO acidosis. DKA = glucose typically < 800 + acidosis + ketones. HHS priority = FLUIDS first; DKA priority = insulin after K+ correction. Both are life-threatening.

visualRationale.title: HHS vs. DKA: two hyperglycemic emergencies
visualRationale.caption: Same chief complaint (high glucose) — completely different drivers and priority treatments.
visualRationale.conclusion: HHS = rehydrate first. DKA = potassium check then insulin. Both are life-threatening.

stem: A patient on chronic prednisone therapy for rheumatoid arthritis is admitted after a motor vehicle accident. Three hours post-admission, the patient develops hypotension (BP 74/40), fever 38.9°C, confusion, and hyponatremia (Na 126). The patient's home prednisone was withheld since admission. Which diagnosis is MOST likely and what is the priority intervention?

options.text: Septic shock — obtain blood cultures and start broad-spectrum antibiotics immediately
Adrenal crisis — administer IV hydrocortisone 100 mg immediately and aggressive IV fluid resuscitation
Distributive shock from trauma — administer vasopressors and packed red blood cells
SIADH from traumatic brain injury — restrict fluids and correct sodium slowly

rationale: Adrenal crisis (acute adrenal insufficiency) presents with hemodynamic collapse, fever, confusion, hyponatremia, and hyperkalemia in patients who take chronic corticosteroids and have had their steroids abruptly withheld during physiologic stress (trauma, surgery, illness). The hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal (HPA) axis suppression means the patient cannot mount an endogenous cortisol stress response. Treatment is emergent IV hydrocortisone (100 mg IV push, then continuous infusion or q6-8h dosing) plus aggressive normal saline resuscitation. This can be life-saving within minutes. Do not delay treatment for lab confirmation when clinical suspicion is high. Septic shock is possible but adrenal crisis takes priority given the steroid history.
tags: Endocrine / Adrenal Crisis

Adrenal crisis triad in steroid-dependent patients: hypotension + hyponatremia + steroid withdrawal under stress. Give IV hydrocortisone IMMEDIATELY — do not wait for cortisol level results. Physiologic stress reveals HPA suppression.
takeaway: Adrenal crisis triad in steroid-dependent patients: hypotension + hyponatremia + steroid withdrawal under stress. Give IV hydrocortisone IMMEDIATELY — do not wait for cortisol level results. Physiologic stress reveals HPA suppression.

visualRationale.title: Adrenal crisis in steroid-dependent patient
visualRationale.caption: Chronic steroid use suppresses the HPA axis — physiologic stress unmasks the deficiency.
visualRationale.conclusion: Do NOT wait for cortisol level — treat on clinical suspicion. Minutes matter.

stem: A patient with traumatic brain injury has an ICP of 28 mmHg (normal < 20). The nurse calculates the cerebral perfusion pressure (CPP = MAP – ICP). Current BP is 108/68 mmHg. Which intervention is MOST appropriate to optimize CPP?

options.text: Lower the head of the bed to 15 degrees to increase venous return to the brain
Administer a 250 mL bolus of hypotonic saline to expand plasma volume and increase MAP
Elevate the head of the bed to 30 degrees, ensure neutral head position, and prepare to administer hypertonic saline or mannitol per order
Administer a sedation bolus to reduce agitation and decrease ICP transiently

rationale: CPP = MAP – ICP. MAP = (108 + 2×68)/3 = 81 mmHg. CPP = 81 – 28 = 53 mmHg (target ≥ 60-70 mmHg). To improve CPP, either lower ICP or raise MAP. Head of bed at 30° with neutral head alignment reduces ICP by promoting venous drainage without compromising cerebral arterial inflow. Osmotic agents (hypertonic saline or mannitol) are first-line ICP-lowering interventions. Hypotonic saline (like D5W or 0.45% NaCl) is contraindicated in TBI — it worsens cerebral edema. Lowering the HOB increases ICP by impairing venous drainage. Sedation may reduce ICP transiently but does not address the underlying osmotic imbalance and is not the primary definitive intervention.
tags: Neuro / Increased Intracranial Pressure

ICP management: HOB 30° + neutral head position + osmotic therapy (hypertonic saline preferred in TBI, mannitol alternative). Monitor CPP ≥ 60-70 mmHg. Hypotonic fluids are absolutely contraindicated in cerebral edema.
takeaway: ICP management: HOB 30° + neutral head position + osmotic therapy (hypertonic saline preferred in TBI, mannitol alternative). Monitor CPP ≥ 60-70 mmHg. Hypotonic fluids are absolutely contraindicated in cerebral edema.

visualRationale.title: CPP = MAP − ICP: optimize both sides
visualRationale.caption: To raise CPP, either lower ICP or protect MAP. Both levers matter.
