Immediate cardioversion + IV amiodarone + supplemental oxygen via simple mask
IV morphine + sublingual nitroglycerin + oral digoxin loading dose

rationale: Acute decompensated heart failure (ADHF) with pulmonary edema is treated with: (1) positioning — upright/sitting position reduces preload by pooling blood in the lower extremities and reduces work of breathing; (2) diuresis — IV furosemide removes the excess fluid volume that is flooding the pulmonary vasculature; (3) noninvasive positive pressure ventilation (BiPAP or CPAP) — improves oxygenation and reduces work of breathing, and is associated with reduced intubation rates in ADHF. IV fluids would worsen pulmonary congestion. Dobutamine is used for cardiogenic shock with low CO, not for standard ADHF with adequate BP. IV morphine is no longer recommended in ADHF guidelines due to increased mortality signal. Cardioversion is only appropriate for hemodynamically unstable tachyarrhythmia contributing to the HF.
tags: Cardiovascular / ACS / Heart Failure

ADHF with pulmonary edema = upright position + IV furosemide + BiPAP. Avoid IV fluids (worsens edema). Avoid morphine (increased mortality in ADHF). BiPAP reduces intubation rates and improves oxygenation.
takeaway: ADHF with pulmonary edema = upright position + IV furosemide + BiPAP. Avoid IV fluids (worsens edema). Avoid morphine (increased mortality in ADHF). BiPAP reduces intubation rates and improves oxygenation.

stem: A patient with a C6 spinal cord injury suddenly develops a severe pounding headache, BP 208/118 mmHg, bradycardia of 48 bpm, and profuse diaphoresis above the level of injury. His urinary catheter bag is empty. What is the PRIORITY nursing action?

options.text: Administer IV labetalol immediately for hypertensive emergency
Sit the patient upright and identify and remove the noxious stimulus causing autonomic dysreflexia
Place the patient flat to promote spinal cord perfusion
Prepare for emergency intubation due to impending respiratory failure

rationale: Autonomic dysreflexia (AD) is a potentially life-threatening syndrome occurring in spinal cord injuries at T6 or above. It is triggered by a noxious stimulus below the level of injury (most commonly bladder distension, then bowel distension, pressure ulcers, or tight clothing). The body generates a massive, unregulated sympathetic response causing severe hypertension, while the normal inhibitory signals cannot pass the spinal cord injury. Reflex bradycardia occurs via intact vagal (parasympathetic) stimulation above the lesion. PRIORITY: (1) Sit the patient upright — this lowers BP via orthostatic redistribution; (2) immediately identify and remove the noxious stimulus — check/drain the bladder first (most common cause), then check for fecal impaction. Antihypertensives are used if stimulus removal does not resolve the BP within minutes. Laying the patient flat would worsen hypertension.
tags: Neurology / Spinal Cord Injury

Autonomic dysreflexia (SCI ≥ T6): severe HTN + bradycardia + diaphoresis above injury = noxious stimulus below injury. Priority: SIT UP + find and remove trigger (bladder first!). Antihypertensives are secondary if stimulus removal fails.
takeaway: Autonomic dysreflexia (SCI ≥ T6): severe HTN + bradycardia + diaphoresis above injury = noxious stimulus below injury. Priority: SIT UP + find and remove trigger (bladder first!). Antihypertensives are secondary if stimulus removal fails.

visualRationale.title: Autonomic dysreflexia: identify and treat the trigger
visualRationale.caption: Massive, uncontrolled sympathetic response to noxious stimulus below SCI level.

nodes.label: Recognize AD
nodes.value: SCI ≥ T6 + severe HTN + bradycardia + diaphoresis above injury

nodes.label: Immediate: SIT UP
nodes.value: Upright position lowers BP via orthostatic redistribution

nodes.label: Find and remove trigger
nodes.value: 1st: Drain bladder (most common) → 2nd: Check bowel → 3rd: Skin/pressure source

nodes.label: If BP stays elevated
nodes.value: Nitro paste or IV nicardipine/hydralazine; avoid giving antihypertensives before finding trigger

visualRationale.conclusion: Remove the trigger FIRST. BP will often self-correct when noxious stimulus is eliminated.

stem: A patient with an acute C5 cervical spine injury has BP 84/54 mmHg, HR 52 bpm, warm dry skin, and priapism. Which condition is MOST consistent with these findings, and what is the first-line treatment?

options.text: Hypovolemic shock — administer a 2-liter NS bolus
Neurogenic shock — vasopressors (norepinephrine) and atropine for bradycardia
Autonomic dysreflexia — sit the patient upright and remove noxious stimuli
Cardiogenic shock — initiate dobutamine infusion

rationale: Neurogenic shock results from disruption of sympathetic pathways following a high cervical or thoracic spinal cord injury. Loss of sympathetic tone causes: vasodilation (warm, flushed, dry skin below the lesion), hypotension, and paradoxical bradycardia (unopposed vagal tone), with priapism as a classic finding. This distinguishes neurogenic shock from hypovolemic shock, which presents with compensatory tachycardia, cool/clammy skin, and no priapism. Treatment: vasopressors (norepinephrine preferred; phenylephrine if bradycardia is severe) to restore SVR; atropine for symptomatic bradycardia. Judicious fluid resuscitation is appropriate, but fluid alone is insufficient without vasopressors for the neurogenic component. Target MAP ≥ 85 mmHg for 7 days post-injury to maintain spinal cord perfusion.
tags: Neurology / Spinal Cord Injury

Neurogenic shock: hypotension + bradycardia + warm/dry skin + priapism after high SCI. Pathophysiology: loss of sympathetic tone → vasodilation + unopposed vagal bradycardia. Treatment: vasopressors (NE) + atropine. Target MAP ≥ 85 mmHg for 7 days.
takeaway: Neurogenic shock: hypotension + bradycardia + warm/dry skin + priapism after high SCI. Pathophysiology: loss of sympathetic tone → vasodilation + unopposed vagal bradycardia. Treatment: vasopressors (NE) + atropine. Target MAP ≥ 85 mmHg for 7 days.

visualRationale.title: Neurogenic shock vs. hypovolemic shock: key distinctions
visualRationale.caption: Both cause hypotension — but the mechanism and presentation are opposite.
visualRationale.conclusion: Bradycardia + warm skin + priapism = neurogenic, not hypovolemic. Vasopressors, not just fluids.

stem: A 68-year-old presents with sudden-onset left-sided facial droop, left arm weakness, and aphasia. Last known well time was 3 hours ago. CT head shows no hemorrhage. BP is 178/102 mmHg. Which intervention is MOST appropriate?

options.text: Administer IV alteplase (tPA) 0.9 mg/kg and lower BP to < 140/90 mmHg before tPA
Hold antihypertensives unless BP > 185/110 mmHg, then administer IV tPA 0.9 mg/kg
Administer aspirin 325 mg immediately and start heparin infusion for anticoagulation
Emergent mechanical thrombectomy without tPA since the symptom onset is > 2 hours

rationale: For acute ischemic stroke presenting within 4.5 hours of last known well, IV alteplase (tPA) at 0.9 mg/kg (max 90 mg) is the standard of care. Key BP threshold for tPA eligibility: BP must be < 185/110 mmHg before and during tPA administration. This patient's BP is 178/102 — within the acceptable range, so antihypertensives are NOT required before tPA. Guidelines recommend avoiding aggressive BP lowering in ischemic stroke unless BP > 185/110 (for tPA candidates) or > 220/120 (for non-tPA candidates), as the ischemic penumbra is dependent on adequate CPP. Aspirin is given after 24 hours post-tPA, not concurrently. Mechanical thrombectomy is an adjunct for large vessel occlusion, not a replacement for tPA in eligible patients.
tags: Neurology / Stroke Management

Ischemic stroke tPA window: 4.5 hours from last known well. BP threshold: hold antihypertensives unless BP > 185/110 mmHg. Give tPA if no hemorrhage, within window, BP controlled. Do NOT aggressively lower BP in ischemic stroke — penumbra depends on perfusion pressure.
takeaway: Ischemic stroke tPA window: 4.5 hours from last known well. BP threshold: hold antihypertensives unless BP > 185/110 mmHg. Give tPA if no hemorrhage, within window, BP controlled. Do NOT aggressively lower BP in ischemic stroke — penumbra depends on perfusion pressure.

stem: A patient on warfarin for AFib presents with a sudden severe headache and vomiting. CT scan shows a subarachnoid hemorrhage. INR is 3.8. Which agent should be administered FIRST to address the coagulopathy?

options.text: Fresh frozen plasma (FFP) alone — administer 2 units IV
4-factor prothrombin complex concentrate (4F-PCC) — faster and more complete reversal than FFP alone
Vitamin K 10 mg IV — corrects INR within 6–12 hours
Recombinant factor VIIa — best agent for subarachnoid hemorrhage reversal

rationale: In warfarin-associated intracranial hemorrhage (including subarachnoid hemorrhage), immediate reversal of anticoagulation is critical to limit hematoma expansion. 4-factor prothrombin complex concentrate (4F-PCC, containing factors II, VII, IX, X and proteins C and S) is preferred over FFP for several reasons: faster onset (minutes vs. 30–60+ minutes for FFP), smaller volume (critical in patients with cardiovascular or fluid restrictions), and more complete and predictable INR correction. Vitamin K is administered concurrently but takes 6–12 hours for effect — it is not fast enough as the sole agent. Recombinant factor VIIa has been studied but is not guideline-preferred for warfarin reversal in ICH. The combination of 4F-PCC + vitamin K IV is the current standard for emergent warfarin reversal in life-threatening hemorrhage.
tags: Neurology / Hemorrhagic Stroke

Warfarin reversal in intracranial hemorrhage: 4F-PCC + IV Vitamin K. 4F-PCC = fastest reversal (minutes). Vitamin K = corrects INR but takes 6-12 hours. FFP is an alternative but slower, higher volume. Emergent reversal is critical to stop hematoma expansion.
takeaway: Warfarin reversal in intracranial hemorrhage: 4F-PCC + IV Vitamin K. 4F-PCC = fastest reversal (minutes). Vitamin K = corrects INR but takes 6-12 hours. FFP is an alternative but slower, higher volume. Emergent reversal is critical to stop hematoma expansion.

stem: A 45-year-old is found in generalized convulsive status epilepticus (GCSE) — continuous seizure activity for 12 minutes. IV access is established. What is the FIRST-LINE pharmacologic intervention?

options.text: IV phenytoin (fosphenytoin) 20 mg/kg
IV lorazepam 0.1 mg/kg (up to 4 mg)
IV levetiracetam 60 mg/kg
IV phenobarbital 20 mg/kg

rationale: Benzodiazepines are the universally established first-line treatment for convulsive status epilepticus. IV lorazepam 0.1 mg/kg (up to 4 mg, repeat once if needed) is the preferred agent due to its rapid onset, efficacy, and favorable duration of action. Midazolam IM (0.2 mg/kg, up to 10 mg) is an effective alternative when IV access is unavailable. The Established Status Epilepticus Treatment Trial (ESETT) established that second-line agents (fosphenytoin, levetiracetam, valproate) are all roughly equivalent in efficacy and are used when benzodiazepines fail. Phenobarbital is typically a third-line agent. The key principle: benzodiazepines → second-line anticonvulsant → anesthetic agents (propofol, midazolam, ketamine, or pentobarbital for refractory SE).
tags: Neurology / Status Epilepticus

Status epilepticus treatment sequence: (1) Benzodiazepine FIRST — lorazepam IV 0.1 mg/kg or midazolam IM if no IV. (2) Second-line: fosphenytoin, levetiracetam, or valproate (ESETT: equal efficacy). (3) Refractory SE: anesthetic agents (propofol, midazolam, pentobarbital). Benzodiazepines are always first.
takeaway: Status epilepticus treatment sequence: (1) Benzodiazepine FIRST — lorazepam IV 0.1 mg/kg or midazolam IM if no IV. (2) Second-line: fosphenytoin, levetiracetam, or valproate (ESETT: equal efficacy). (3) Refractory SE: anesthetic agents (propofol, midazolam, pentobarbital). Benzodiazepines are always first.

visualRationale.title: Status epilepticus treatment algorithm
visualRationale.caption: Time-critical — seizure duration correlates with neuronal injury and treatment resistance.

nodes.label: Seizure > 5 minutes
nodes.value: Treat as status epilepticus — do not wait for spontaneous resolution

nodes.label: First-line (0-20 min)
nodes.value: Lorazepam IV 0.1 mg/kg (≤4 mg) OR midazolam IM if no IV

nodes.label: Second-line (20-40 min)
nodes.value: Fosphenytoin 20 mg/kg OR levetiracetam 60 mg/kg OR valproate 40 mg/kg

nodes.label: Refractory SE (>40 min)
nodes.value: ICU: propofol, midazolam, or pentobarbital infusion + continuous EEG monitoring

visualRationale.conclusion: Benzodiazepine is always first. Delayed treatment increases mortality and resistance to subsequent medications.

stem: A patient has received two doses of IV lorazepam and IV fosphenytoin without cessation of seizure activity (now 55 minutes). EEG confirms continued electrographic seizure activity. What is the NEXT step in management?

options.text: Administer a third dose of IV lorazepam and reassess in 15 minutes
Initiate continuous IV anesthetic infusion (propofol or midazolam) with continuous EEG monitoring in the ICU
Administer IV magnesium sulfate 4 g over 20 minutes for seizure suppression
Transfer to neurology floor for extended observation and oral antiepileptic titration

rationale: This is refractory status epilepticus (RSE) — defined as GCSE that fails to respond to first-line benzodiazepines AND second-line anticonvulsants. RSE carries a mortality of 30–50%. The standard of care for RSE is ICU admission with continuous anesthetic infusion to achieve burst suppression on continuous EEG monitoring. Agents used: propofol (rapid onset, easy titration), midazolam infusion, or pentobarbital (most powerful, longest duration). The goal is burst suppression pattern on EEG — this demonstrates seizure suppression. A third benzodiazepine dose is not indicated in established RSE and adds risk of respiratory depression without added benefit. IV magnesium is used for eclamptic seizures, not RSE in non-obstetric patients.
tags: Neurology / Status Epilepticus

Refractory status epilepticus (failed benzo + 2nd-line drug) = ICU admission + continuous anesthetic infusion (propofol, midazolam, or pentobarbital) + continuous EEG monitoring for burst suppression. Mortality 30-50%.
takeaway: Refractory status epilepticus (failed benzo + 2nd-line drug) = ICU admission + continuous anesthetic infusion (propofol, midazolam, or pentobarbital) + continuous EEG monitoring for burst suppression. Mortality 30-50%.

stem: A nurse is preparing a patient for a formal brain death evaluation. Which finding is INCONSISTENT with brain death and would INVALIDATE the determination?

options.text: Absent corneal reflexes bilaterally
Core body temperature of 34.2°C (93.6°F)
Fixed and dilated pupils with no response to direct light
Absent gag and cough reflexes with ETT manipulation

rationale: Brain death is defined as the irreversible cessation of all functions of the entire brain, including the brainstem. A valid brain death determination requires that confounders be excluded before testing begins. Hypothermia (core temperature < 36°C / 96.8°F) is a major confounder — at low temperatures, the brain may appear non-functional due to cold-induced neuronal suppression, and the patient may actually have reversible neurological depression. Brain death testing must not proceed until core temperature is ≥ 36°C. Other confounders that invalidate testing: severe metabolic disturbances, sedatives/paralytics in the system, severe hemodynamic instability. The clinical examination findings (absent corneal reflexes, fixed dilated pupils, absent gag/cough) are all consistent with brain death but are valid only in the appropriate clinical context without confounders.
tags: Neurology / Brain Death

Brain death prerequisites: (1) Known cause of irreversible brain injury; (2) Core temp ≥ 36°C; (3) No sedatives/paralytics on board; (4) No severe metabolic disturbances; (5) Hemodynamic stability. Hypothermia < 36°C INVALIDATES the brain death exam.
takeaway: Brain death prerequisites: (1) Known cause of irreversible brain injury; (2) Core temp ≥ 36°C; (3) No sedatives/paralytics on board; (4) No severe metabolic disturbances; (5) Hemodynamic stability. Hypothermia < 36°C INVALIDATES the brain death exam.

stem: A patient with a traumatic brain injury has an ICP of 28 mmHg and a mean arterial pressure (MAP) of 80 mmHg. The provider orders hypertonic saline (3% NaCl). What is the cerebral perfusion pressure (CPP), and which intervention would MOST directly and immediately lower ICP?

options.text: CPP = 52 mmHg; administer hypertonic saline to reduce cerebral edema
CPP = 52 mmHg; elevate the head of bed to 30 degrees and ensure neutral neck alignment
CPP = 108 mmHg; administer mannitol to osmotically draw fluid out of neurons
CPP = 52 mmHg; lower MAP to 65 mmHg with antihypertensives

rationale: CPP = MAP – ICP = 80 – 28 = 52 mmHg. Target CPP is 60–70 mmHg in severe TBI (per Brain Trauma Foundation guidelines). The patient has inadequate CPP, not excessive. Positioning interventions to optimize ICP: HOB at 30 degrees reduces ICP by promoting venous drainage from the brain; neutral neck alignment prevents jugular venous compression that can increase ICP. This is the most immediately applicable nursing intervention. Hypertonic saline is also appropriate (it creates an osmotic gradient to pull edema fluid from brain cells and reduces ICP), but positioning is faster to implement and does not require a physician order. Lowering MAP would WORSEN CPP. CPP = MAP – ICP; reducing MAP reduces CPP when ICP is already high.
tags: Neurology / ICP Management

CPP = MAP – ICP. Target CPP 60-70 mmHg. HOB 30 degrees + neutral neck = immediate nursing intervention to optimize ICP. Do NOT lower MAP in elevated ICP — it worsens CPP. Hypertonic saline/mannitol reduce ICP osmotically.
takeaway: CPP = MAP – ICP. Target CPP 60-70 mmHg. HOB 30 degrees + neutral neck = immediate nursing intervention to optimize ICP. Do NOT lower MAP in elevated ICP — it worsens CPP. Hypertonic saline/mannitol reduce ICP osmotically.

visualRationale.title: ICP management: nursing priority sequence
visualRationale.caption: CPP = MAP − ICP. Protect the formula — raise MAP or lower ICP to maintain CPP 60-70.

nodes.label: Positioning
nodes.value: HOB 30° + neutral neck → promotes venous drainage → ↓ ICP

nodes.label: Osmotic therapy
nodes.value: Hypertonic saline or mannitol → draw edema from neurons → ↓ ICP

nodes.label: Sedation / Analgesia
nodes.value: Reduce agitation → ↓ CMRO2 and venous engorgement → ↓ ICP

nodes.label: Avoid
nodes.value: Hypoxia (SpO2 <95%), hypercapnia (PaCO2 >45), hyperthermia — all raise ICP

visualRationale.conclusion: HOB 30°, neutral neck, osmotherapy, normocapnia. Never lower MAP when ICP is high.

stem: A patient with massive intracranial hemorrhage develops a fixed and dilated right pupil, decorticate posturing, and deteriorating consciousness. Which herniation syndrome is MOST likely, and what is the emergency intervention?

options.text: Uncal herniation — administer IV mannitol and emergent neurosurgical evaluation
Transtentorial herniation — perform lumbar puncture to reduce CSF pressure
Central herniation — administer high-dose methylprednisolone
Tonsillar herniation — place the patient in Trendelenburg position

rationale: Uncal herniation occurs when the medial temporal lobe (uncus) herniates through the tentorial notch, compressing cranial nerve III (oculomotor nerve). This produces the classic triad: ipsilateral fixed dilated pupil (CN III compression), contralateral hemiplegia (corticospinal tract compression), and progressive loss of consciousness. This is a neurosurgical emergency. Emergency management: IV osmotic therapy (mannitol 1 g/kg or hypertonic saline) to reduce ICP immediately, HOB elevation, ensure normocapnia, and emergent neurosurgical evaluation for possible hematoma evacuation. Lumbar puncture is absolutely CONTRAINDICATED in suspected herniation — it can precipitate brainstem herniation by suddenly releasing CSF pressure below. Methylprednisolone has no role in TBI or hemorrhagic herniation. Trendelenburg would worsen ICP.
tags: Neurology / Herniation Syndromes

Uncal herniation: ipsilateral fixed dilated pupil + contralateral hemiplegia + declining consciousness. Emergency: IV mannitol or HTS + emergent neurosurgical evaluation. LP is ABSOLUTELY CONTRAINDICATED in herniation.
takeaway: Uncal herniation: ipsilateral fixed dilated pupil + contralateral hemiplegia + declining consciousness. Emergency: IV mannitol or HTS + emergent neurosurgical evaluation. LP is ABSOLUTELY CONTRAINDICATED in herniation.

stem: A 72-year-old with new-onset dizziness, diplopia, dysphagia, and ataxia is brought to the ICU. MRI-DWI shows restricted diffusion in the brainstem. Which statement about this stroke presentation is MOST accurate?

options.text: This is an anterior circulation stroke — tPA is contraindicated for posterior strokes
This is a posterior circulation (vertebrobasilar) stroke — tPA is appropriate if within the treatment window and no contraindications exist
Basilar artery occlusion is confirmed — immediate lumbar puncture should be performed to rule out SAH
Posterior circulation strokes have better prognosis than anterior strokes and do not require emergent intervention

rationale: This presentation — dizziness, diplopia, dysphagia, dysarthria, and ataxia (the 'D' signs of posterior circulation stroke) — with brainstem DWI changes indicates a posterior circulation (vertebrobasilar) stroke. Basilar artery occlusion is among the most devastating strokes, with mortality exceeding 80% if untreated. tPA is appropriate for posterior circulation ischemic strokes within the treatment window (4.5 hours from last known well for IV tPA) if no contraindications exist. Mechanical thrombectomy of basilar artery occlusion has shown benefit up to 24 hours in select patients. tPA is NOT contraindicated for posterior strokes. LP is not indicated here — MRI-DWI confirms ischemic stroke. Posterior circulation strokes (particularly basilar) carry higher mortality than many anterior strokes.
tags: Neurology / Stroke Management

Posterior circulation stroke signs: diplopia, dysphagia, dysarthria, dizziness, ataxia (5 Ds). Basilar artery occlusion = catastrophic — mortality >80% untreated. tPA is appropriate if within window. Mechanical thrombectomy can be considered up to 24 hours for basilar occlusion.
takeaway: Posterior circulation stroke signs: diplopia, dysphagia, dysarthria, dizziness, ataxia (5 Ds). Basilar artery occlusion = catastrophic — mortality >80% untreated. tPA is appropriate if within window. Mechanical thrombectomy can be considered up to 24 hours for basilar occlusion.

stem: A 55-year-old presents with hematemesis and melena. BP 92/60 mmHg, HR 124 bpm, Hgb 7.2 g/dL. Which combination of interventions reflects the PRIORITY care bundle for acute upper GI bleeding?

options.text: NPO, IV pantoprazole infusion, emergent upper endoscopy within 24 hours of stabilization
Immediate colonoscopy, IV octreotide infusion, NG tube placement for lavage
Oral omeprazole, blood transfusion to Hgb > 10 g/dL, elective endoscopy in 48–72 hours
NG tube lavage, IV heparin to prevent thrombus formation at the bleeding site, urgent surgical consult

rationale: The management bundle for acute non-variceal upper GI bleeding (UGIB): (1) NPO for endoscopy; (2) Resuscitation — IV fluids, transfusion if Hgb < 7–8 g/dL or hemodynamically unstable (restrictive transfusion strategy: target Hgb 7–8 g/dL, NOT >10); (3) IV PPI infusion (pantoprazole 80 mg bolus + 8 mg/hr infusion) — reduces rebleeding rate and surgical need; (4) Urgent endoscopy within 24 hours of hemodynamic stabilization. High-risk features (active bleeding, non-bleeding visible vessel) may require endoscopy < 12 hours. Colonoscopy is for lower GI bleeding. Octreotide is for variceal bleeding. Heparin is contraindicated in active GI bleeding. Restrictive transfusion strategy (Hgb target 7–8 g/dL) is associated with better outcomes than liberal transfusion in UGIB.
tags: GI / Upper GI Bleed

Acute UGIB bundle: NPO + IV PPI (pantoprazole) + resuscitate (restrictive transfusion: target Hgb 7-8) + urgent endoscopy < 24h. Restrictive transfusion improves outcomes in UGIB. IV PPI reduces rebleeding rate.
takeaway: Acute UGIB bundle: NPO + IV PPI (pantoprazole) + resuscitate (restrictive transfusion: target Hgb 7-8) + urgent endoscopy < 24h. Restrictive transfusion improves outcomes in UGIB. IV PPI reduces rebleeding rate.

stem: A 48-year-old with alcoholic cirrhosis presents with massive hematemesis and BP 78/46 mmHg. He is actively vomiting blood. Endoscopy confirms esophageal variceal hemorrhage. Which pharmacologic agent should be initiated BEFORE or AT THE TIME OF endoscopy to reduce portal pressure and improve endoscopic outcomes?

options.text: IV pantoprazole (PPI) infusion — reduces mucosal acid exposure at the bleeding site
IV octreotide (somatostatin analog) — reduces splanchnic blood flow and portal pressure
IV vasopressin alone — most potent vasoconstrictor for portal hypertension
IV propranolol — non-selective beta-blocker reduces portal hypertension acutely

rationale: Esophageal variceal hemorrhage is managed with: (1) IV octreotide (or terlipressin/vasopressin+nitroglycerin where available) — initiated at first suspicion of variceal bleeding and continued for 3–5 days; octreotide reduces splanchnic vasodilation and portal pressure, improving hemostasis; (2) Emergent upper endoscopy with band ligation (preferred) or sclerotherapy; (3) Antibiotic prophylaxis (ceftriaxone or norfloxacin) — reduces bacterial translocation and infection, reduces rebleeding and mortality; (4) Cautious volume resuscitation — over-transfusion worsens portal pressure and can increase rebleeding. PPI infusion is for non-variceal UGIB (peptic ulcer disease). IV propranolol is used for secondary prevention of variceal bleeding, not acute hemorrhage. Vasopressin alone causes significant systemic vasoconstriction and adverse effects — it is used with nitroglycerin to mitigate this.
tags: GI / Variceal Bleeding

Variceal bleed = IV octreotide + emergent band ligation endoscopy + antibiotic prophylaxis. Octreotide reduces portal pressure before/during endoscopy. Do NOT over-transfuse (raises portal pressure). Antibiotics are mandatory — reduce mortality.
takeaway: Variceal bleed = IV octreotide + emergent band ligation endoscopy + antibiotic prophylaxis. Octreotide reduces portal pressure before/during endoscopy. Do NOT over-transfuse (raises portal pressure). Antibiotics are mandatory — reduce mortality.

visualRationale.title: Acute variceal hemorrhage: resuscitation + hemostasis bundle
visualRationale.caption: Portal hypertension drives variceal hemorrhage — reduce it pharmacologically while preparing endoscopy.

nodes.label: Resuscitate cautiously
nodes.value: Transfuse to Hgb 7-8 g/dL; avoid overloading portal circulation

nodes.label: IV Octreotide
nodes.value: Reduce splanchnic flow + portal pressure; start immediately, continue 3-5 days

nodes.label: Antibiotic prophylaxis
nodes.value: Ceftriaxone 1g IV q24h × 5-7 days — reduces rebleeding and mortality

nodes.label: Emergent endoscopy
nodes.value: Band ligation preferred; Sengstaken-Blakemore tube if endoscopy fails or delayed

visualRationale.conclusion: Octreotide + antibiotics + endoscopic ligation = the variceal bleed triad. PPI is for ulcers, not varices.

stem: A 38-year-old with septic shock develops new bleeding from IV sites, oozing from a surgical wound, and petechiae. Labs: platelets 42,000/μL, PT 22 sec, aPTT 68 sec, fibrinogen 92 mg/dL, D-dimer 18 μg/mL (markedly elevated). Which condition BEST explains this laboratory pattern?

options.text: Heparin-induced thrombocytopenia (HIT) — stop all heparin products immediately
Disseminated intravascular coagulation (DIC) — simultaneous thrombosis and hemorrhage due to uncontrolled clotting cascade activation
Thrombotic thrombocytopenic purpura (TTP) — initiate plasmapheresis
Idiopathic thrombocytopenic purpura (ITP) — administer IVIG and corticosteroids

rationale: Disseminated intravascular coagulation (DIC) is a consumptive coagulopathy triggered by a precipitating illness (sepsis, trauma, malignancy, obstetric complications). The pathophysiology: massive systemic activation of the clotting cascade → simultaneous microvascular thrombosis (consuming clotting factors and platelets) and secondary fibrinolysis → depletion of all clotting factors + thrombocytopenia + hemorrhage. Classic DIC labs: low platelets, prolonged PT and aPTT, low fibrinogen (consumed), very high D-dimer (fibrin degradation product). HIT causes thrombocytopenia with thrombosis but does NOT cause prolonged PT/aPTT or low fibrinogen. TTP presents with thrombocytopenia + microangiopathic hemolytic anemia + neurological signs — PT/aPTT are typically NORMAL. ITP is an isolated thrombocytopenia without coagulopathy.
tags: Multisystem / DIC

DIC labs: low platelets + prolonged PT/aPTT + low fibrinogen + very high D-dimer = consumptive coagulopathy. Cause: sepsis, trauma, malignancy, obstetric emergency. Treatment: treat the UNDERLYING CAUSE first; replace factors/platelets only if actively bleeding.
takeaway: DIC labs: low platelets + prolonged PT/aPTT + low fibrinogen + very high D-dimer = consumptive coagulopathy. Cause: sepsis, trauma, malignancy, obstetric emergency. Treatment: treat the UNDERLYING CAUSE first; replace factors/platelets only if actively bleeding.

visualRationale.title: DIC vs. HIT vs. TTP: thrombocytopenia differentials
visualRationale.caption: All three cause low platelets — mechanism, labs, and treatment are completely different.
visualRationale.conclusion: DIC: PT/aPTT up + fibrinogen down. HIT: heparin exposure + thrombosis. TTP: MAHA + neuro.

stem: A patient with obstetric DIC following placental abruption is bleeding from multiple sites. Fibrinogen is 68 mg/dL and the patient is hemodynamically unstable. Which blood product MOST directly replaces fibrinogen?

options.text: Packed red blood cells (PRBCs) — most important for hemoglobin restoration
Fresh frozen plasma (FFP) — contains all clotting factors including fibrinogen
Cryoprecipitate — most concentrated source of fibrinogen (and factor VIII, vWF)
Platelet concentrate — restores platelet count and adhesion

rationale: Cryoprecipitate is the blood product of choice for isolated or severe fibrinogen deficiency in DIC. It is derived by thawing FFP and collecting the cold-insoluble precipitate, which is rich in fibrinogen, Factor VIII, von Willebrand factor, Factor XIII, and fibronectin. One standard dose (10 units pooled) raises fibrinogen by approximately 70–100 mg/dL. Target fibrinogen in bleeding DIC: ≥ 150 mg/dL. FFP contains fibrinogen but at a much lower concentration — it would require very large volumes to achieve similar fibrinogen replacement. PRBCs are important for red cell mass but do not address coagulopathy. Platelets are transfused in DIC when platelets < 50,000 and the patient is actively bleeding. In DIC management: treat the cause first; transfuse to support hemostasis (not to 'normalize' all labs).
tags: Multisystem / DIC Treatment

Cryoprecipitate = most concentrated fibrinogen source. Use in DIC when fibrinogen < 100 mg/dL (or < 150 if bleeding). Target fibrinogen ≥ 150 mg/dL. FFP replaces all clotting factors but delivers dilute fibrinogen. Treat the underlying DIC trigger simultaneously.
takeaway: Cryoprecipitate = most concentrated fibrinogen source. Use in DIC when fibrinogen < 100 mg/dL (or < 150 if bleeding). Target fibrinogen ≥ 150 mg/dL. FFP replaces all clotting factors but delivers dilute fibrinogen. Treat the underlying DIC trigger simultaneously.

stem: A 32-year-old with acetaminophen-induced acute liver failure develops asterixis, confusion, and INR of 4.2. Ammonia is 148 μmol/L. Serum glucose is 52 mg/dL. What is the PRIORITY nursing assessment and intervention at this time?

options.text: Administer IV N-acetylcysteine (NAC) to reverse acetaminophen toxicity and continue it regardless of INR
Correct the coagulopathy by administering FFP and vitamin K before any other intervention
Assess neurological status and treat the hypoglycemia with IV dextrose — hypoglycemia is life-threatening in ALF
Administer lactulose 30 mL via NG tube to clear ammonia from the GI tract

rationale: In acute liver failure (ALF), the liver fails to perform gluconeogenesis and glycogen stores are depleted. Hypoglycemia (glucose < 60 mg/dL) is a common and immediately life-threatening complication that must be treated without delay. Simultaneously, the nurse must assess neurological status — hepatic encephalopathy (from ammonia) combined with hypoglycemia can rapidly progress to coma and cerebral edema. IV dextrose (D10W or D50W as appropriate) should be administered immediately. N-acetylcysteine (NAC) is important in acetaminophen ALF — it is given as a standard infusion protocol and should be continued, but it does not address the immediate hypoglycemia. FFP is not indicated solely to correct INR in ALF unless there is active bleeding or a procedure is planned — INR in ALF reflects synthetic dysfunction and is also a prognostic marker. Lactulose is appropriate for hepatic encephalopathy but is not the priority when glucose is 52 mg/dL.
tags: GI / Acute Liver Failure

Acute liver failure priorities: (1) Hypoglycemia — treat immediately with IV dextrose; (2) Neurological monitoring for encephalopathy/cerebral edema; (3) NAC for acetaminophen ALF; (4) Avoid lactulose in ALF-associated encephalopathy (may be harmful — rifaximin preferred). Do NOT correct INR unless bleeding — it's a prognostic marker.
takeaway: Acute liver failure priorities: (1) Hypoglycemia — treat immediately with IV dextrose; (2) Neurological monitoring for encephalopathy/cerebral edema; (3) NAC for acetaminophen ALF; (4) Avoid lactulose in ALF-associated encephalopathy (may be harmful — rifaximin preferred). Do NOT correct INR unless bleeding — it's a prognostic marker.

stem: A patient with cirrhosis and hepatic encephalopathy (Grade II — lethargy, asterixis) is receiving lactulose 30 mL every 2 hours. The nurse notes 8 liquid stools in the past 12 hours and Na+ 128 mEq/L. Which nursing action is MOST appropriate?

options.text: Continue current lactulose dose — 8 stools per day is the target for encephalopathy treatment
Reduce the lactulose dose to achieve 2–3 soft stools per day and reassess for hypernatremia risk from excessive diarrhea
Discontinue lactulose entirely and switch to rifaximin 550 mg PO BID
Increase the lactulose dose to clear more ammonia and reassess mental status in 4 hours

rationale: The therapeutic target for lactulose in hepatic encephalopathy is 2–3 soft bowel movements per day — this is sufficient to reduce ammonia-producing colonic bacteria and enhance ammonia excretion. More than this causes adverse effects: excessive diarrhea leads to dehydration, electrolyte imbalances (hyponatremia, hypokalemia), and paradoxically worsening encephalopathy through metabolic derangements. This patient has 8 stools per day AND hyponatremia (Na+ 128) — consistent with lactulose-induced fluid loss. The lactulose dose should be reduced. Rifaximin (550 mg BID) is an effective adjunct or alternative for hepatic encephalopathy, particularly for prevention of recurrence, but it is not the immediate intervention here. Increasing lactulose would worsen dehydration and hyponatremia.
tags: GI / Hepatic Encephalopathy

Lactulose target in hepatic encephalopathy: 2-3 soft stools/day. Excessive diarrhea (>3-4 watery stools) = reduce dose — causes dehydration, hyponatremia, and worsening encephalopathy. Rifaximin is an effective adjunct, especially for recurrence prevention.
takeaway: Lactulose target in hepatic encephalopathy: 2-3 soft stools/day. Excessive diarrhea (>3-4 watery stools) = reduce dose — causes dehydration, hyponatremia, and worsening encephalopathy. Rifaximin is an effective adjunct, especially for recurrence prevention.

stem: A 42-year-old with severe acute pancreatitis has a Ranson score of 5. Amylase is 1,820 U/L, lipase 4,200 U/L. BP is 94/62 mmHg, HR 118 bpm. The provider orders pain management and IV fluids. What is the PRIORITY intervention and fluid strategy?

options.text: Morphine IV for pain and restrict IV fluids to 50–75 mL/hr to prevent pancreatic edema
Aggressive IV lactated Ringer's resuscitation (250–500 mL/hr) with goal-directed titration to MAP and urine output
NPO, NG tube to suction, and early TPN to rest the pancreas
Emergent endoscopic retrograde cholangiopancreatography (ERCP) to decompress the pancreatic duct

rationale: Aggressive fluid resuscitation is the cornerstone of early acute pancreatitis management. Pancreatitis causes massive fluid sequestration into the retroperitoneum and mesentery (third-spacing), leading to intravascular volume depletion, decreased pancreatic microcirculation, and risk of pancreatic necrosis. Lactated Ringer's is the preferred fluid over NS — it is associated with lower rates of SIRS in pancreatitis. Goal-directed resuscitation: initial bolus 250–500 mL/hr, titrate to MAP ≥ 65 mmHg, urine output ≥ 0.5 mL/kg/hr, and improved heart rate. Restricting fluids worsens pancreatic ischemia and necrosis. Morphine is appropriate for pain (the historical concern about morphine causing sphincter of Oddi spasm is not well-supported). Early enteral nutrition (not TPN) via NG/NJ tube is preferred over NPO when tolerated — it maintains gut barrier integrity and reduces infection risk. ERCP is indicated for gallstone pancreatitis with cholangitis.
tags: GI / Acute Pancreatitis

Severe acute pancreatitis: aggressive IV LR resuscitation is the priority (250-500 mL/hr, titrate to UO and MAP). LR preferred over NS. Early enteral nutrition preferred over TPN. ERCP only if gallstone pancreatitis + cholangitis.
takeaway: Severe acute pancreatitis: aggressive IV LR resuscitation is the priority (250-500 mL/hr, titrate to UO and MAP). LR preferred over NS. Early enteral nutrition preferred over TPN. ERCP only if gallstone pancreatitis + cholangitis.

stem: A post-operative patient develops nausea, vomiting, abdominal distension, and absent bowel sounds 3 days after abdominal surgery. CT scan shows a small bowel obstruction (SBO) without signs of strangulation or perforation. What is the PRIORITY nursing intervention?

options.text: Prepare the patient for immediate surgical laparotomy to relieve the obstruction
Administer a high-volume tap-water enema to relieve the obstruction
Insert a nasogastric tube to low intermittent suction for decompression and initiate IV fluid resuscitation
Encourage early ambulation and a clear liquid diet to stimulate bowel motility

rationale: For small bowel obstruction without signs of strangulation (fever, leukocytosis, peritoneal signs) or perforation, the initial management is conservative: (1) NGT to low intermittent suction — decompresses the bowel, relieves nausea/vomiting, prevents further distension and aspiration; (2) IV fluid resuscitation — SBO causes significant fluid shifts and third-spacing; (3) NPO; (4) Serial abdominal exams and labs to monitor for deterioration. The majority of partial SBOs and post-operative SBOs (adhesions) resolve with conservative management within 48–72 hours. Surgery is indicated for complete obstruction, strangulation, or failure of conservative management. Enemas are used for large bowel obstruction (volvulus, Ogilvie syndrome), not SBO. Clear liquids with ambulation is appropriate for paralytic ileus, not mechanical obstruction.
tags: GI / Bowel Obstruction

SBO without strangulation/perforation = NGT to low suction + IV fluids + NPO (conservative management). Monitor for worsening (strangulation signs: fever, peritonitis, leukocytosis). Most partial SBOs resolve without surgery. Strangulation = surgical emergency.
takeaway: SBO without strangulation/perforation = NGT to low suction + IV fluids + NPO (conservative management). Monitor for worsening (strangulation signs: fever, peritonitis, leukocytosis). Most partial SBOs resolve without surgery. Strangulation = surgical emergency.

stem: A patient meets the following criteria: temperature 38.9°C, HR 108 bpm, RR 24 breaths/min, WBC 14,200/μL with 12% bands, and suspected pneumonia. His lactate is 3.8 mmol/L and BP 88/52 mmHg despite 1 L IV fluid. Which best describes this presentation, and what is the ONE-HOUR bundle priority?

options.text: SIRS without infection — obtain cultures and monitor for 24 hours before initiating antibiotics
Septic shock — obtain blood cultures, administer broad-spectrum antibiotics, and begin vasopressors (norepinephrine) targeting MAP ≥ 65 mmHg
Severe sepsis — initiate antibiotics and aggressive IV fluid resuscitation of 30 mL/kg over 6 hours
Uncomplicated sepsis — IV antibiotics and reassess response to the first 1 L fluid bolus

rationale: This patient meets criteria for septic shock per Sepsis-3 definitions: suspected infection + organ dysfunction (elevated lactate 3.8 mmol/L indicating hypoperfusion) + hypotension requiring vasopressors (BP 88/52 mmHg refractory to initial fluid challenge). The 2018 SSC One-Hour Bundle for septic shock: (1) Measure lactate; (2) Obtain blood cultures before antibiotics; (3) Administer broad-spectrum IV antibiotics; (4) IV fluid resuscitation 30 mL/kg crystalloid for hypotension or lactate ≥ 4 mmol/L; (5) Start vasopressors (norepinephrine is first-line) if hypotension persists after fluid resuscitation, targeting MAP ≥ 65 mmHg. Vasopressors should not be delayed if the patient remains hypotensive. 'Severe sepsis' is no longer a Sepsis-3 term — the current definitions are sepsis (infection + organ dysfunction) and septic shock (sepsis + vasopressor requirement + lactate ≥ 2 mmol/L).
tags: Multisystem / SIRS and Sepsis

Septic shock (Sepsis-3): infection + need for vasopressors to maintain MAP ≥ 65 + lactate ≥ 2 mmol/L. 1-hour bundle: lactate + blood cultures + broad-spectrum antibiotics + 30 mL/kg crystalloid + vasopressors (norepinephrine first-line) if hypotension persists.
takeaway: Septic shock (Sepsis-3): infection + need for vasopressors to maintain MAP ≥ 65 + lactate ≥ 2 mmol/L. 1-hour bundle: lactate + blood cultures + broad-spectrum antibiotics + 30 mL/kg crystalloid + vasopressors (norepinephrine first-line) if hypotension persists.

visualRationale.title: Septic shock: SSC 1-hour bundle
visualRationale.caption: Every hour of delayed antibiotics in septic shock increases mortality ~7%.

nodes.label: Recognize Septic Shock
nodes.value: Infection + organ dysfunction + vasopressor-requiring hypotension + lactate ≥ 2

nodes.label: Measure lactate
nodes.value: Lactate ≥ 4 = high-risk; initiate bundle regardless of BP

nodes.label: Blood cultures × 2
nodes.value: Before antibiotics — do not delay antibiotics to get cultures

nodes.label: Broad-spectrum antibiotics
nodes.value: Within 1 hour of recognition — every hour delay = ↑ mortality

nodes.label: 30 mL/kg crystalloid IV
nodes.value: Bolus for hypotension or lactate ≥ 4; reassess response

nodes.label: Vasopressors if needed
nodes.value: Norepinephrine first-line; target MAP ≥ 65 mmHg; do not wait to maximize fluids

visualRationale.conclusion: SSC 1-hour bundle: lactate → cultures → antibiotics → 30 mL/kg fluids → vasopressors. All within 1 hour.

stem: A patient with massive anterior MI develops cardiogenic shock: BP 76/52 mmHg, HR 122 bpm, CI 1.6 L/min/m², PCWP 26 mmHg, SVR 1,800 dynes/sec/cm5. Which hemodynamic profile and treatment best match this presentation?

options.text: High CO, low SVR — consistent with distributive shock; start vasopressors
Low CO, high SVR, high PCWP — consistent with cardiogenic shock; dobutamine + consider IABP/MCS
Low CO, low SVR, low PCWP — consistent with hypovolemic shock; aggressive fluid resuscitation
Low CO, high SVR, low PCWP — RV failure; aggressive fluid resuscitation and pulmonary vasodilators

rationale: Cardiogenic shock hemodynamic profile: Low cardiac index (< 2.2 L/min/m²), high PCWP (> 18 mmHg — elevated LV filling pressures from pump failure), high SVR (compensatory vasoconstriction). This patient's CI of 1.6, PCWP 26, and SVR 1,800 are textbook cardiogenic shock. Treatment: (1) Inotropic support — dobutamine (positive inotrope) to improve contractility; (2) Consider vasopressors (norepinephrine) if MAP < 65 mmHg; (3) Emergent PCI for the culprit MI lesion — the most important survival intervention; (4) Mechanical circulatory support (IABP, Impella, or ECMO) for refractory cardiogenic shock. Aggressive IV fluids would worsen pulmonary edema (PCWP already 26). Vasopressors alone without inotropes worsen afterload in a failing ventricle.
tags: Multisystem / Cardiogenic Shock

Cardiogenic shock profile: low CI (<2.2) + high PCWP (>18) + high SVR. Treatment: dobutamine (inotrope) + emergent PCI + consider IABP/Impella for MCS. Do NOT give aggressive IV fluids — PCWP is already elevated. Norepinephrine for MAP support.
takeaway: Cardiogenic shock profile: low CI (<2.2) + high PCWP (>18) + high SVR. Treatment: dobutamine (inotrope) + emergent PCI + consider IABP/Impella for MCS. Do NOT give aggressive IV fluids — PCWP is already elevated. Norepinephrine for MAP support.

visualRationale.title: Shock hemodynamic profiles: CO, PCWP, SVR patterns
visualRationale.caption: The PA catheter (or clinical surrogates) differentiates shock type — treatment follows the profile.
visualRationale.conclusion: PCWP differentiates cardiogenic (elevated) from hypovolemic (low). CO differentiates cardiogenic/hypovolemic from distributive (high CO in sepsis).

stem: A 67-year-old male in the CVICU is post-op day 1 following mitral valve replacement. His pulmonary artery catheter readings are: PAP 42/24 mmHg, PCWP 22 mmHg, CVP 14 mmHg, CO 3.2 L/min. BP is 88/54 mmHg. Which interpretation BEST describes this hemodynamic profile?

options.text: Right ventricular failure with pulmonary hypertension
Left ventricular failure with elevated filling pressures
Distributive shock with low systemic vascular resistance
Hypovolemia with compensatory tachycardia

rationale: An elevated PCWP (normal 6–12 mmHg) of 22 mmHg reflects elevated left ventricular end-diastolic pressure, indicating left ventricular failure and fluid backup into the pulmonary circulation. The elevated PAP is secondary to the high PCWP. Low CO (normal 4–8 L/min) confirms impaired forward flow. CVP elevation indicates right-sided congestion from the failing left ventricle. Distributive shock would show low SVR and high CO. Hypovolemia would present with low CVP, low PCWP, and high SVR. Pure RV failure would show elevated CVP and PAP without the elevated PCWP.
tags: Hemodynamic Monitoring / PA Catheter
PCWP >18 mmHg = left heart failure / pulmonary congestion. Low CO + elevated PCWP + elevated PAP = classic cardiogenic shock pattern. PCWP reflects LVEDP when the mitral valve is intact.
takeaway: PCWP >18 mmHg = left heart failure / pulmonary congestion. Low CO + elevated PCWP + elevated PAP = classic cardiogenic shock pattern. PCWP reflects LVEDP when the mitral valve is intact.

stem: A 72-year-old woman with septic shock is on norepinephrine 0.15 mcg/kg/min and vasopressin 0.03 units/min. Her thermodilution CO is 8.4 L/min, BSA is 1.7 m², MAP is 65 mmHg, CVP is 6 mmHg. Calculate her cardiac index (CI) and systemic vascular resistance (SVR). Which result correctly identifies her current hemodynamic state?

options.text: CI 4.9 L/min/m², SVR 556 dyn·s·cm⁻⁵ — hyperdynamic with low resistance
CI 2.1 L/min/m², SVR 1120 dyn·s·cm⁻⁵ — cardiogenic pattern with high resistance
CI 4.9 L/min/m², SVR 1280 dyn·s·cm⁻⁵ — normal CI with borderline SVR
CI 3.2 L/min/m², SVR 556 dyn·s·cm⁻⁵ — compensated distributive shock

rationale: CI = CO ÷ BSA = 8.4 ÷ 1.7 = 4.94 L/min/m² (normal 2.5–4.0; this is elevated). SVR = [(MAP − CVP) ÷ CO] × 80 = [(65 − 6) ÷ 8.4] × 80 = [59 ÷ 8.4] × 80 = 7.02 × 80 ≈ 561 dyn·s·cm⁻⁵ (normal 800–1200; this is low). This hyperdynamic, low-SVR state is the hallmark of distributive (septic) shock: high CO due to inflammatory vasodilation, low SVR because of pathologic vasodilation, and vasopressors are compensating the MAP. The vasopressors are working but SVR remains below normal.
tags: Hemodynamic Monitoring / CO/CI/SVR
CI = CO ÷ BSA. SVR = [(MAP − CVP) ÷ CO] × 80. Septic shock = high CI + low SVR = hyperdynamic. Cardiogenic shock = low CI + high SVR = hypodynamic. Know both formulas cold.
takeaway: CI = CO ÷ BSA. SVR = [(MAP − CVP) ÷ CO] × 80. Septic shock = high CI + low SVR = hyperdynamic. Cardiogenic shock = low CI + high SVR = hypodynamic. Know both formulas cold.

stem: The CCU receives two patients simultaneously. Patient A: post-PE arrest, HR 138, BP 70/40, JVD present, trachea deviated left, SpO₂ 78% on 15L NRB. Patient B: 3 days post-abdominal surgery, HR 124, BP 82/50, T 39.8°C, WBC 28,000, lactate 5.2 mmol/L. Which correctly matches the shock type to hemodynamic mechanism?

options.text: Patient A = distributive shock; Patient B = obstructive shock
Patient A = obstructive shock; Patient B = distributive shock
Both patients have obstructive shock from different causes
Both patients have distributive shock; the tracheal deviation is incidental

rationale: Patient A has obstructive shock: massive PE causes outflow obstruction at the pulmonary vasculature, preventing right heart ejection → JVD from RV backup, hypotension, and severe hypoxemia. The tracheal deviation plus clinical context suggests tension pneumothorax may coexist. The mechanism is mechanical obstruction to flow. Patient B has distributive shock (sepsis): post-op infection causing systemic inflammatory vasodilation, high fever, leukocytosis, and elevated lactate indicating tissue hypoperfusion despite adequate (or high) cardiac output. Distributive = vasodilation; obstructive = mechanical blockage.
tags: Shock Differentiation / Distributive vs Obstructive

Obstructive shock = mechanical block to flow (PE, tension PTX, tamponade) → JVD + hypotension + no fever. Distributive shock = pathologic vasodilation (sepsis, anaphylaxis, neurogenic) → warm/flushed early, high fever, lactate elevation. Mechanism drives treatment.
takeaway: Obstructive shock = mechanical block to flow (PE, tension PTX, tamponade) → JVD + hypotension + no fever. Distributive shock = pathologic vasodilation (sepsis, anaphylaxis, neurogenic) → warm/flushed early, high fever, lactate elevation. Mechanism drives treatment.

stem: A 55-year-old presents to the ED with anaphylaxis after a bee sting. BP is 74/38 mmHg, HR 142, SpO₂ 91%, skin is flushed/warm with urticaria. He received epinephrine 0.3 mg IM 8 minutes ago with no improvement. Which SECOND-LINE intervention is MOST appropriate to address the hemodynamic instability?

options.text: Norepinephrine 0.1–0.2 mcg/kg/min IV infusion
Normal saline 250 mL bolus and reassess
Dopamine 5 mcg/kg/min IV infusion
Repeat epinephrine 0.3 mg IM and add IV diphenhydramine

rationale: Refractory anaphylactic shock unresponsive to epinephrine IM requires vasopressor support. Norepinephrine is first-line vasopressor for anaphylaxis refractory to epinephrine due to its strong alpha-1 (vasoconstriction) and moderate beta-1 (inotropy) effects, directly counteracting the massive histamine-mediated vasodilation. IV fluid boluses alone are insufficient for refractory hemodynamic collapse. Dopamine is second-line and less preferred vs. norepinephrine for anaphylaxis. Repeating IM epinephrine (3rd option) is appropriate if IV access is unavailable, but IV vasopressor is superior with access present.
tags: Shock Differentiation / Perfusion Assessment

Epinephrine IM = first-line anaphylaxis. If refractory after 2 doses or with established IV access, escalate to IV epinephrine infusion or norepinephrine. Anaphylaxis = distributive shock → vasopressors restore SVR.
takeaway: Epinephrine IM = first-line anaphylaxis. If refractory after 2 doses or with established IV access, escalate to IV epinephrine infusion or norepinephrine. Anaphylaxis = distributive shock → vasopressors restore SVR.

stem: A 63-year-old post-cardiac surgery patient has the following assessment: MAP 58 mmHg, ScvO₂ 54%, lactate 4.1 mmol/L rising from 2.3 two hours ago, urine output 12 mL/hr × 3 hours, capillary refill 4 seconds, skin mottled to knees. Which finding is the MOST reliable indicator that oxygen delivery is inadequate to meet tissue demands?

options.text: MAP 58 mmHg below target of 65 mmHg
ScvO₂ 54% with rising lactate indicating oxygen extraction > delivery
Urine output 12 mL/hr indicating renal hypoperfusion
Capillary refill 4 seconds indicating peripheral vasoconstriction

rationale: ScvO₂ (central venous oxygen saturation) normally 70–80% reflects the balance between oxygen delivery (DO₂) and consumption (VO₂). ScvO₂ of 54% means tissues are extracting far more oxygen than normal (>25% extraction), confirming DO₂ is severely inadequate. Combined with a rising lactate (anaerobic metabolism), this is the strongest evidence of global tissue hypoperfusion. Low MAP is a trigger, but MAP alone doesn't quantify tissue adequacy. Oliguria and mottling are signs of hypoperfusion but are less specific and quantitative than ScvO₂ + lactate trend together.
tags: Perfusion Assessment

ScvO₂ <70% = tissues compensating for low DO₂ by extracting more O₂. ScvO₂ <65% = critical. Rising lactate + falling ScvO₂ = most reliable evidence of DO₂/VO₂ mismatch requiring immediate intervention.
takeaway: ScvO₂ <70% = tissues compensating for low DO₂ by extracting more O₂. ScvO₂ <65% = critical. Rising lactate + falling ScvO₂ = most reliable evidence of DO₂/VO₂ mismatch requiring immediate intervention.

stem: A patient with ARDS is on volume-controlled ventilation with the following settings: FiO2 0.80, PEEP 10 cmH2O, tidal volume (TV) 480 mL, RR 22. The patient weighs 60 kg (ideal body weight). ABGs: pH 7.28, PaCO2 52 mmHg, PaO2 58 mmHg, SpO2 88%. The intensivist asks the nurse to assist in adjusting ventilator settings per the ARDSnet protocol. Which change is MOST consistent with lung-protective ventilation?

options.text: Increase tidal volume to 600 mL to improve CO2 clearance
Reduce tidal volume to 360 mL (6 mL/kg IBW) and increase RR to 28
Reduce PEEP to 5 cmH2O to decrease barotrauma risk
Increase FiO2 to 1.0 and decrease RR to 18 to allow permissive hypercapnia

rationale: The ARDSnet protocol mandates a tidal volume of 4-8 mL/kg ideal body weight (IBW), with a target of 6 mL/kg IBW. For a 60 kg IBW patient, TV = 360 mL. The current TV of 480 mL (8 mL/kg) is at the upper limit; reducing to 6 mL/kg is the priority lung-protective change. Increasing RR to compensate for reduced TV is appropriate to maintain minute ventilation and manage the pH 7.28 (permissive hypercapnia is acceptable down to pH 7.20 in ARDSnet). Increasing TV violates lung protection. Reducing PEEP worsens oxygenation in ARDS. Maximizing FiO2 without adjusting TV does not address barotrauma/volutrauma.
tags: Respiratory / Ventilator Management
ARDSnet lung protection: TV 4-8 mL/kg IBW (target 6), plateau pressure ≤ 30 cmH2O, adequate PEEP to maintain oxygenation, permissive hypercapnia allowed to pH ≥ 7.20.
takeaway: ARDSnet lung protection: TV 4-8 mL/kg IBW (target 6), plateau pressure ≤ 30 cmH2O, adequate PEEP to maintain oxygenation, permissive hypercapnia allowed to pH ≥ 7.20.

visualRationale.title: ARDSnet lung-protective ventilation targets
visualRationale.caption: Lower tidal volumes prevent volutrauma. Permissive hypercapnia is acceptable to protect the lung.

metrics.label: Plateau pressure
metrics.directionLabel: safety ceiling — check with inspiratory hold

visualRationale.conclusion: Lung protection = lower TV + controlled Pplat ≤ 30 + adequate PEEP. Accept pH ≥ 7.20.

stem: A 68-year-old patient with COPD is admitted for acute exacerbation. ABGs on 2L nasal cannula: pH 7.32, PaCO2 62 mmHg, HCO3 30 mEq/L, PaO2 55 mmHg. Which interpretation and priority action is MOST appropriate?

options.text: Respiratory alkalosis with metabolic compensation; increase O2 to 5L/min
Respiratory acidosis with metabolic compensation (chronic); initiate non-invasive positive pressure ventilation (NIPPV) and notify provider
Metabolic alkalosis with respiratory compensation; administer sodium bicarbonate
Mixed acidosis; prepare for emergent intubation

rationale: pH 7.32 (acidotic), PaCO2 62 (elevated — respiratory acidosis), HCO3 30 (elevated — compensatory metabolic alkalosis). The elevated bicarbonate indicates chronic CO2 retention with renal compensation — classic COPD pattern. The primary problem is respiratory acidosis. With pH 7.32 and PaO2 55 mmHg, this patient meets criteria for NIPPV (BiPAP) which can reduce work of breathing, improve ventilation, and avoid intubation. High-flow O2 must be used cautiously in COPD to avoid eliminating the hypoxic drive, though SpO2 target of 88-92% is acceptable. Sodium bicarb is not indicated. Emergent intubation is not yet warranted with this pH.
tags: Respiratory / ABG Interpretation

In COPD exacerbation with respiratory acidosis and elevated HCO3: the compensation is chronic. Treat with NIPPV (BiPAP) first. Target SpO2 88-92% to preserve hypoxic drive. Intubation is a last resort.
takeaway: In COPD exacerbation with respiratory acidosis and elevated HCO3: the compensation is chronic. Treat with NIPPV (BiPAP) first. Target SpO2 88-92% to preserve hypoxic drive. Intubation is a last resort.

visualRationale.title: COPD exacerbation ABG: chronic respiratory acidosis
visualRationale.caption: Elevated HCO3 = renal compensation = chronic CO2 retention. This is not an acute emergency pattern alone.
visualRationale.conclusion: Chronic respiratory acidosis. Treat: NIPPV (BiPAP), target SpO2 88-92%, avoid high-flow O2.

stem: A nurse is caring for a mechanically ventilated patient in the ICU. The high-pressure ventilator alarm suddenly activates. The nurse notices the patient is agitated, breath sounds are absent on the left, the trachea appears deviated to the right, and SpO2 is dropping from 97% to 84%. What is the PRIORITY nursing action?

options.text: Increase FiO2 to 1.0 and suction the endotracheal tube
Disconnect the patient from the ventilator and manually bag-ventilate while calling for immediate provider assistance
Administer a sedation bolus to decrease patient agitation and airway resistance
Obtain a portable chest X-ray to confirm suspected pneumothorax

rationale: Absent unilateral breath sounds, tracheal deviation, agitation, high-pressure alarm, and acute SpO2 decline are classic signs of tension pneumothorax — a life-threatening emergency. The tracheal deviation away from the affected side indicates mediastinal shift with cardiovascular compromise imminent. The immediate nursing priority is to manually ventilate (hand-bagging) to assess and temporarily manage oxygenation while the provider is called for emergent needle decompression. Suctioning will not address pneumothorax. Increasing FiO2 alone does not treat the mechanical obstruction. Sedation is inappropriate. An X-ray delays definitive treatment when the patient is hemodynamically compromised — tension pneumothorax is a clinical diagnosis.
tags: Respiratory / Airway Management

Tension pneumothorax triad in ventilated patients: absent unilateral breath sounds + tracheal deviation (away from affected side) + hemodynamic deterioration. Disconnect and bag → call provider → emergent needle decompression. Do NOT delay for X-ray.
takeaway: Tension pneumothorax triad in ventilated patients: absent unilateral breath sounds + tracheal deviation (away from affected side) + hemodynamic deterioration. Disconnect and bag → call provider → emergent needle decompression. Do NOT delay for X-ray.

visualRationale.title: Tension pneumothorax: recognize and act
visualRationale.caption: Clinical diagnosis — do NOT wait for X-ray when the patient is decompensating on the vent.

nodes.label: High-pressure vent alarm + unilateral absent breath sounds
Tracheal deviation away from affected side
Emergent needle decompression
nodes.value: 2nd ICS, MCL → chest tube for definitive treatment

visualRationale.conclusion: Tension PTX = clinical diagnosis. Act before imaging. Every minute delays risks cardiac arrest.

stem: A patient has been mechanically ventilated for 5 days following ARDS. The provider orders a spontaneous breathing trial (SBT). Which assessment finding would indicate the patient has PASSED the SBT and is ready for extubation consideration?

options.text: RR 30/min, SpO2 90% on FiO2 0.40, patient using significant accessory muscles at 30 minutes
RR 18/min, SpO2 96% on FiO2 0.40, TV 450 mL, minimal accessory muscle use, alert and cooperative after 30 minutes
RR 24/min, SpO2 94%, patient becomes diaphoretic at 20 minutes; SBT stopped early
RR 14/min, SpO2 98%, but patient has copious secretions requiring suctioning every 5 minutes

rationale: A successful SBT is characterized by: RR < 25/min, SpO2 ≥ 90-95%, adequate tidal volume (typically > 4-5 mL/kg IBW), absence of significant respiratory distress (no accessory muscle use, diaphoresis, or agitation), and hemodynamic stability for at least 30 minutes. Option B meets all criteria. Option A has tachypnea and accessory muscle use — SBT failure. Option C required early termination due to distress — SBT failure. Option D: despite adequate ventilatory parameters, copious secretions requiring q5min suctioning indicate inability to protect/clear the airway — this is a separate extubation contraindication.
tags: Respiratory / Weaning and Extubation

SBT success criteria: RR < 25, SpO2 ≥ 90%, adequate TV, no accessory muscle use, hemodynamically stable, tolerates 30 minutes. Passing the SBT is necessary but not sufficient — also assess cough strength and secretion management before extubation.
takeaway: SBT success criteria: RR < 25, SpO2 ≥ 90%, adequate TV, no accessory muscle use, hemodynamically stable, tolerates 30 minutes. Passing the SBT is necessary but not sufficient — also assess cough strength and secretion management before extubation.

stem: A 55-year-old patient presents with acute respiratory failure. ABGs: pH 7.22, PaCO2 35 mmHg, HCO3 14 mEq/L, PaO2 88 mmHg on 4L NC. Lactic acid is 6.8 mmol/L. What type of respiratory failure is present and what is the underlying mechanism?

options.text: Type I (hypoxemic) respiratory failure due to ventilation-perfusion mismatch
Type II (hypercapnic) respiratory failure due to pump failure
Type I respiratory failure with a superimposed metabolic acidosis due to tissue hypoperfusion
Pure metabolic acidosis with appropriate respiratory compensation; no respiratory failure

rationale: PaO2 88 mmHg on 4L NC is hypoxemic relative to O2 delivery (P:F ratio would require full FiO2 calculation, but supplemental O2 requirement establishes Type I failure). The pH 7.22 (acidotic), PaCO2 35 (normal-low — not retaining CO2), HCO3 14 (low — metabolic acidosis) pattern indicates a metabolic acidosis with partial respiratory compensation (lungs are blowing off CO2 to compensate). Lactic acid 6.8 signals severe tissue hypoperfusion/anaerobic metabolism — likely septic or cardiogenic shock. This is Type I respiratory failure (oxygenation failure from parenchymal disease or V/Q mismatch) superimposed on a high anion gap metabolic acidosis from lactic acidosis.
tags: Respiratory / Respiratory Failure

Classify respiratory failure: Type I = hypoxemic (PaO2 low, PaCO2 normal/low); Type II = hypercapnic (PaCO2 high, pH low). Metabolic acidosis can coexist — always check pH, HCO3, lactate, and anion gap together.
takeaway: Classify respiratory failure: Type I = hypoxemic (PaO2 low, PaCO2 normal/low); Type II = hypercapnic (PaCO2 high, pH low). Metabolic acidosis can coexist — always check pH, HCO3, lactate, and anion gap together.

visualRationale.title: Type I vs. Type II respiratory failure
visualRationale.caption: Classify by which failure dominates: oxygenation (gas exchange) or ventilation (pump).
visualRationale.conclusion: High PaCO2 + low pH = Type II. Low PaO2 with normal PaCO2 = Type I. Both can coexist.

stem: A mechanically ventilated patient develops sudden desaturation (SpO2 90% → 78%). The nurse performs the DOPE mnemonic assessment. Which finding would indicate the problem is 'Obstruction'?

options.text: The ETT has migrated and breath sounds are unilateral
A mucus plug is visualized in the ETT and resolved after suctioning
The ventilator circuit is disconnected at the Y-piece
A new pneumothorax is identified on chest X-ray

rationale: The DOPE mnemonic for acute ventilator desaturation: D = Displacement (ETT migration/dislodgement), O = Obstruction (mucus plug, biting ETT, kinked tubing), P = Pneumothorax, E = Equipment failure. A mucus plug visualized and cleared by suctioning is the classic example of Obstruction. ETT migration causing unilateral breath sounds = Displacement. Disconnected circuit = Equipment failure. Pneumothorax on X-ray = Pneumothorax. Systematically applying DOPE ensures the cause is identified and corrected quickly.
tags: Respiratory / Ventilator Management

DOPE mnemonic for vent desaturation: Displacement → Obstruction → Pneumothorax → Equipment. Always disconnect and hand-bag if uncertain — this immediately tests for equipment failure and buys time while assessing the other categories.
takeaway: DOPE mnemonic for vent desaturation: Displacement → Obstruction → Pneumothorax → Equipment. Always disconnect and hand-bag if uncertain — this immediately tests for equipment failure and buys time while assessing the other categories.

visualRationale.title: DOPE mnemonic: acute vent desaturation
visualRationale.caption: Systematic four-cause framework prevents missing the diagnosis during crisis.

nodes.label: D — Displacement
nodes.value: ETT migrated or dislodged → check position, capnography

nodes.label: O — Obstruction
nodes.value: Mucus plug, biting, kinked circuit → suction ETT

nodes.label: P — Pneumothorax
nodes.value: Absent breath sounds, tracheal deviation → needle decompression

nodes.label: E — Equipment failure
nodes.value: Disconnection or malfunction → disconnect and bag manually

visualRationale.conclusion: Disconnect and bag FIRST if uncertain — immediately tests E while managing oxygenation.

stem: A patient with severe ARDS has a PaO2/FiO2 (P:F ratio) of 82 despite FiO2 0.80 and PEEP 14 cmH2O. The intensivist considers prone positioning. Which nursing assessment finding is a CONTRAINDICATION to prone positioning?

options.text: SpO2 88% on current settings
Unstable spinal fracture identified on imaging
BMI of 36
Recent central line placement 48 hours ago

rationale: Prone positioning significantly improves oxygenation in severe ARDS (P:F < 150) by recruiting dorsal lung segments and redistributing perfusion. Contraindications include: unstable spinal fractures (risk of cord injury with turning), open abdominal wounds, hemodynamic instability not responsive to vasopressors, elevated ICP, and recent tracheal surgery. An unstable spinal fracture is an absolute contraindication. Low SpO2 (88%) is the indication FOR proning — it is why the intervention is being considered. Obesity (BMI 36) requires additional staff and planning but is not a contraindication. A 48-hour-old central line is appropriate — lines must be secured but do not contraindicate proning.
tags: Respiratory / ARDS Management

Prone positioning indications: severe ARDS (P:F < 150) on optimal vent settings. Key absolute contraindications: unstable spinal injury, open abdomen, uncontrolled elevated ICP. Lines and tubes can be managed — the spine cannot be compromised.
takeaway: Prone positioning indications: severe ARDS (P:F < 150) on optimal vent settings. Key absolute contraindications: unstable spinal injury, open abdomen, uncontrolled elevated ICP. Lines and tubes can be managed — the spine cannot be compromised.

visualRationale.title: Prone positioning decision gate
visualRationale.caption: Severe ARDS benefits from proning — but check absolute contraindications first.

nodes.label: P:F ratio < 150 despite FiO2 >0.60 and PEEP ≥ 5?
Check absolute contraindications
nodes.value: Unstable spine, open abdomen, uncontrolled ICP

nodes.label: Absolute contraindication present?
nodes.value: → do NOT prone — consider alternative rescue strategies

visualRationale.conclusion: Unstable spinal injury = absolute contraindication. Lines and tubes are manageable. Spine is not.

stem: A nurse is caring for a patient who was extubated 3 hours ago following a 6-day intubation. The patient is now becoming increasingly stridorous, with a RR of 32 and SpO2 declining to 91%. What is the priority intervention?

options.text: Apply a non-rebreather mask at 15L/min and notify the provider
Administer nebulized racemic epinephrine and place the patient upright, call provider immediately, and prepare for possible re-intubation
Encourage the patient to take slow, deep breaths to reduce stridor
Obtain a throat culture and start empiric antibiotics for suspected laryngeal infection

rationale: Post-extubation stridor indicates laryngeal or subglottic edema, which is a medical emergency with risk of complete airway obstruction. Racemic epinephrine via nebulization is the first-line pharmacologic treatment (causes vasoconstriction, reduces mucosal edema). The patient should be sitting upright to maximize airway diameter. Immediate provider notification is mandatory, as re-intubation may be needed urgently if stridor worsens or SpO2 does not improve. Applying a non-rebreather alone treats oxygenation but not the obstructive cause. Breathing exercises are insufficient for stridor. Antibiotics are not indicated for post-extubation edema.
tags: Respiratory / Airway / Escalation

Post-extubation stridor = laryngeal edema. Treat with racemic epinephrine nebulization + upright positioning + immediate provider call. Re-intubation equipment must be at bedside. Heliox (helium-oxygen) is an alternative bridge. Act fast — airway edema can close completely.
takeaway: Post-extubation stridor = laryngeal edema. Treat with racemic epinephrine nebulization + upright positioning + immediate provider call. Re-intubation equipment must be at bedside. Heliox (helium-oxygen) is an alternative bridge. Act fast — airway edema can close completely.

stem: A nurse is triaging three ICU patients with respiratory deterioration. Which patient requires intervention FIRST?

options.text: Patient A: pH 7.30, PaCO2 55, PaO2 72 on 4L NC; alert, able to speak full sentences
Patient B: RR 36/min, paradoxical abdominal movement, SpO2 84% on BiPAP, increasing somnolence over the last 30 minutes
Patient C: SpO2 93% on 2L NC, new productive cough, temperature 38.8°C
Patient D: pH 7.35, PaCO2 48, PaO2 80 on Venturi mask at 40% FiO2; clinically stable

rationale: Patient B is in acute respiratory failure with impending arrest: SpO2 84% despite BiPAP (indicating BiPAP failure), RR 36 with paradoxical breathing (a sign of fatigue and imminent respiratory arrest), and progressive somnolence (altered mental status indicating CO2 narcosis or cerebral hypoxia). This patient requires immediate escalation — likely emergent intubation. Patient A is compensating and able to maintain conversation (not yet critical). Patient C has a mild to moderate illness (pneumonia) with manageable oxygenation. Patient D is stable on supplemental O2 with a nearly normal pH.
tags: Respiratory / Respiratory Failure / Prioritization

Paradoxical abdominal movement + somnolence + SpO2 < 88% despite BiPAP = impending respiratory arrest. Escalate to intubation immediately. Fatigue signs (paradoxical breathing) and mental status change override absolute numbers.
takeaway: Paradoxical abdominal movement + somnolence + SpO2 < 88% despite BiPAP = impending respiratory arrest. Escalate to intubation immediately. Fatigue signs (paradoxical breathing) and mental status change override absolute numbers.

stem: A mechanically ventilated ARDS patient has the following: TV 420 mL (6 mL/kg IBW 70 kg), PEEP 12, RR 24, FiO2 0.65. During an inspiratory hold maneuver, the plateau pressure (Pplat) is 34 cmH2O. Which intervention is MOST appropriate?

options.text: Decrease TV to 4 mL/kg IBW (280 mL) and accept permissive hypercapnia
Increase PEEP to 18 cmH2O to improve oxygenation
Increase RR to 30 to compensate for high plateau pressure
Add a recruitment maneuver to open collapsed alveoli

rationale: The ARDSnet protocol sets a plateau pressure ceiling of ≤ 30 cmH2O. A Pplat of 34 cmH2O exceeds the safe limit and indicates overdistension risk (barotrauma/volutrauma). When Pplat > 30, the protocol directs reduction of TV by 1 mL/kg IBW until Pplat ≤ 30 or TV reaches 4 mL/kg minimum. Permissive hypercapnia (pH ≥ 7.20) is acceptable to achieve this. Increasing PEEP when Pplat is already high would worsen overdistension. Increasing RR does not directly reduce Pplat. Recruitment maneuvers are considered when Pplat is acceptable and oxygenation remains inadequate — not when pressure is already too high.
tags: Respiratory / Ventilator / Plateau Pressure

Pplat > 30 cmH2O in ARDS = reduce TV (down to 4 mL/kg IBW minimum). Permissive hypercapnia is acceptable. Pplat is the gold standard for assessing static compliance and overdistension risk — check it with every significant vent change.
takeaway: Pplat > 30 cmH2O in ARDS = reduce TV (down to 4 mL/kg IBW minimum). Permissive hypercapnia is acceptable. Pplat is the gold standard for assessing static compliance and overdistension risk — check it with every significant vent change.

visualRationale.title: Plateau pressure > 30: step down TV
visualRationale.caption: Pplat reflects static lung distension — exceeding 30 cmH2O signals barotrauma/volutrauma risk.

metrics.label: TV reduction
metrics.value: step down 1 mL/kg at a time

metrics.label: Permissive hypercapnia
metrics.directionLabel: accept CO2 rise to protect the lung

visualRationale.conclusion: Reduce TV stepwise until Pplat ≤ 30 cmH2O. Never increase PEEP when Pplat is already high.

stem: A 28-year-old with type 1 diabetes is admitted with DKA. Labs: glucose 520 mg/dL, pH 7.18, HCO3 10 mEq/L, potassium 3.2 mEq/L, sodium 132 mEq/L. The provider orders insulin infusion. What is the PRIORITY nursing action BEFORE starting the insulin infusion?

options.text: Administer sodium bicarbonate to correct the acidosis first
Replace potassium to at least 3.5 mEq/L before initiating insulin
Begin the insulin infusion at the ordered rate and monitor glucose hourly
Administer hypertonic saline to correct the hyponatremia first

rationale: In DKA, insulin drives potassium intracellularly, rapidly dropping serum potassium. If insulin is initiated when potassium is already < 3.5 mEq/L, the patient is at significant risk for life-threatening hypokalemia (cardiac arrhythmias, cardiac arrest). The standard DKA protocol mandates that potassium be replaced and confirmed ≥ 3.5 mEq/L before insulin is started. Sodium bicarbonate is not recommended for DKA unless pH < 6.9 and is used cautiously; it does not address the underlying insulin deficiency and can paradoxically worsen cellular acidosis. Hypertonic saline is not indicated — corrected sodium and isotonic fluids are used. Beginning insulin without addressing the hypokalemia is dangerous.
tags: Endocrine / DKA Management

DKA protocol gate: K+ must be ≥ 3.5 mEq/L before insulin is started. Insulin shifts K+ intracellularly. Starting insulin with K+ < 3.5 can cause fatal hypokalemia. Replace K+ → start insulin → monitor glucose q1h.
takeaway: DKA protocol gate: K+ must be ≥ 3.5 mEq/L before insulin is started. Insulin shifts K+ intracellularly. Starting insulin with K+ < 3.5 can cause fatal hypokalemia. Replace K+ → start insulin → monitor glucose q1h.

visualRationale.title: DKA protocol gate: K+ before insulin
visualRationale.caption: Insulin drives K+ into cells — starting it with low potassium risks fatal arrhythmia.
visualRationale.conclusion: K+ must be ≥ 3.5 mEq/L before insulin. No exceptions in DKA.

stem: A 76-year-old with type 2 diabetes presents with altered mental status and glucose of 890 mg/dL. ABGs: pH 7.38, HCO3 24 mEq/L, serum osmolality 348 mOsm/kg. Which condition does this BEST represent and what is the priority treatment?

options.text: DKA — start insulin infusion at 0.1 units/kg/hr immediately
Hyperosmolar hyperglycemic state (HHS) — aggressive IV fluid resuscitation is the priority intervention
SIADH — restrict fluids and correct hyponatremia
DKA — administer sodium bicarbonate and insulin concurrently

rationale: HHS is characterized by severe hyperglycemia (> 600 mg/dL), hyperosmolality (> 320 mOsm/kg), minimal or absent ketoacidosis (pH > 7.30, HCO3 > 18), and significant mental status changes correlating with osmolality. This patient has glucose 890, osmolality 348, normal pH and HCO3 — classic HHS, not DKA. HHS is driven by severe dehydration and osmotic diuresis. The priority treatment is aggressive IV fluid resuscitation (typically 1-1.5L NS over the first hour, then adjusted). Insulin is used more cautiously in HHS — rapid glucose lowering without adequate fluid can precipitate hypotension. SIADH causes hyponatremia with low osmolality — the opposite pattern.
tags: Endocrine / HHS vs. DKA Differentiation

HHS vs. DKA: HHS = very high glucose + very high osmolality + NO acidosis. DKA = glucose typically < 800 + acidosis + ketones. HHS priority = FLUIDS first; DKA priority = insulin after K+ correction. Both are life-threatening.
takeaway: HHS vs. DKA: HHS = very high glucose + very high osmolality + NO acidosis. DKA = glucose typically < 800 + acidosis + ketones. HHS priority = FLUIDS first; DKA priority = insulin after K+ correction. Both are life-threatening.

visualRationale.title: HHS vs. DKA: two hyperglycemic emergencies
visualRationale.caption: Same chief complaint (high glucose) — completely different drivers and priority treatments.
visualRationale.conclusion: HHS = rehydrate first. DKA = potassium check then insulin. Both are life-threatening.

stem: A patient on chronic prednisone therapy for rheumatoid arthritis is admitted after a motor vehicle accident. Three hours post-admission, the patient develops hypotension (BP 74/40), fever 38.9°C, confusion, and hyponatremia (Na 126). The patient's home prednisone was withheld since admission. Which diagnosis is MOST likely and what is the priority intervention?

options.text: Septic shock — obtain blood cultures and start broad-spectrum antibiotics immediately
Adrenal crisis — administer IV hydrocortisone 100 mg immediately and aggressive IV fluid resuscitation
Distributive shock from trauma — administer vasopressors and packed red blood cells
SIADH from traumatic brain injury — restrict fluids and correct sodium slowly

rationale: Adrenal crisis (acute adrenal insufficiency) presents with hemodynamic collapse, fever, confusion, hyponatremia, and hyperkalemia in patients who take chronic corticosteroids and have had their steroids abruptly withheld during physiologic stress (trauma, surgery, illness). The hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal (HPA) axis suppression means the patient cannot mount an endogenous cortisol stress response. Treatment is emergent IV hydrocortisone (100 mg IV push, then continuous infusion or q6-8h dosing) plus aggressive normal saline resuscitation. This can be life-saving within minutes. Do not delay treatment for lab confirmation when clinical suspicion is high. Septic shock is possible but adrenal crisis takes priority given the steroid history.
tags: Endocrine / Adrenal Crisis
