Adrenal crisis triad in steroid-dependent patients: hypotension + hyponatremia + steroid withdrawal under stress. Give IV hydrocortisone IMMEDIATELY — do not wait for cortisol level results. Physiologic stress reveals HPA suppression.
takeaway: Adrenal crisis triad in steroid-dependent patients: hypotension + hyponatremia + steroid withdrawal under stress. Give IV hydrocortisone IMMEDIATELY — do not wait for cortisol level results. Physiologic stress reveals HPA suppression.

visualRationale.title: Adrenal crisis in steroid-dependent patient
visualRationale.caption: Chronic steroid use suppresses the HPA axis — physiologic stress unmasks the deficiency.
visualRationale.conclusion: Do NOT wait for cortisol level — treat on clinical suspicion. Minutes matter.

stem: A patient with traumatic brain injury has an ICP of 28 mmHg (normal < 20). The nurse calculates the cerebral perfusion pressure (CPP = MAP – ICP). Current BP is 108/68 mmHg. Which intervention is MOST appropriate to optimize CPP?

options.text: Lower the head of the bed to 15 degrees to increase venous return to the brain
Administer a 250 mL bolus of hypotonic saline to expand plasma volume and increase MAP
Elevate the head of the bed to 30 degrees, ensure neutral head position, and prepare to administer hypertonic saline or mannitol per order
Administer a sedation bolus to reduce agitation and decrease ICP transiently

rationale: CPP = MAP – ICP. MAP = (108 + 2×68)/3 = 81 mmHg. CPP = 81 – 28 = 53 mmHg (target ≥ 60-70 mmHg). To improve CPP, either lower ICP or raise MAP. Head of bed at 30° with neutral head alignment reduces ICP by promoting venous drainage without compromising cerebral arterial inflow. Osmotic agents (hypertonic saline or mannitol) are first-line ICP-lowering interventions. Hypotonic saline (like D5W or 0.45% NaCl) is contraindicated in TBI — it worsens cerebral edema. Lowering the HOB increases ICP by impairing venous drainage. Sedation may reduce ICP transiently but does not address the underlying osmotic imbalance and is not the primary definitive intervention.
tags: Neuro / Increased Intracranial Pressure

ICP management: HOB 30° + neutral head position + osmotic therapy (hypertonic saline preferred in TBI, mannitol alternative). Monitor CPP ≥ 60-70 mmHg. Hypotonic fluids are absolutely contraindicated in cerebral edema.
takeaway: ICP management: HOB 30° + neutral head position + osmotic therapy (hypertonic saline preferred in TBI, mannitol alternative). Monitor CPP ≥ 60-70 mmHg. Hypotonic fluids are absolutely contraindicated in cerebral edema.

visualRationale.title: CPP = MAP − ICP: optimize both sides
visualRationale.caption: To raise CPP, either lower ICP or protect MAP. Both levers matter.
visualRationale.conclusion: HOB 30° + neutral head + hypertonic saline or mannitol targets ICP reduction to raise CPP.

stem: A nurse is caring for a patient with a subdural hematoma who suddenly develops a fixed and dilated right pupil, left-sided hemiplegia, and decreased respiratory rate. Which herniation syndrome is occurring and what is the IMMEDIATE priority?

options.text: Uncal (transtentorial) herniation — notify provider immediately, prepare for hyperventilation to PaCO2 30-35 mmHg, and assist with emergent neurosurgical intervention
Central herniation — administer high-dose methylprednisolone and obtain MRI
Upward herniation — lower the head of the bed flat and administer mannitol
Foramen magnum herniation — prepare for immediate LP to relieve pressure

rationale: The classic presentation of uncal (transtentorial) herniation is ipsilateral pupil dilation (CN III compression on the same side as the herniation) + contralateral motor deficits (corticospinal tract compression). The right fixed dilated pupil + left hemiplegia indicates the right temporal lobe uncus is herniating through the tentorium, compressing the right CN III and left motor pathways. Respiratory depression indicates brainstem compromise. This is a neurosurgical emergency. Temporary measures include brief hyperventilation (target PaCO2 30-35 mmHg) to cause cerebral vasoconstriction and reduce ICP acutely — used as a bridge to definitive intervention. LP is absolutely contraindicated with raised ICP and herniation. Methylprednisolone is not indicated in TBI.
tags: Neuro / Herniation Syndromes

Uncal herniation: ipsilateral blown pupil + contralateral motor deficit = CN III + brainstem compression. Emergency: hyperventilate (PaCO2 30-35) as bridge → neurosurgical decompression. LP is contraindicated.
takeaway: Uncal herniation: ipsilateral blown pupil + contralateral motor deficit = CN III + brainstem compression. Emergency: hyperventilate (PaCO2 30-35) as bridge → neurosurgical decompression. LP is contraindicated.

visualRationale.title: Uncal herniation recognition sequence
visualRationale.caption: Ipsilateral CN III compression + contralateral motor deficit = classic uncal herniation.
visualRationale.conclusion: LP is absolutely contraindicated with herniation. Decompression is the only definitive treatment.

stem: A patient in the ICU develops oliguria with urine output of 12 mL/hr for 4 consecutive hours. Current labs: creatinine 3.8 mg/dL (baseline 1.0), BUN 68 mg/dL, urine sodium 8 mEq/L, urine specific gravity 1.032. Which type of AKI does this pattern indicate and what is the FIRST intervention?

options.text: Intrinsic (renal) AKI — administer furosemide to force diuresis
Pre-renal AKI — administer an IV fluid challenge and reassess urine output
Post-renal AKI — insert a urinary catheter to relieve obstruction
Intrinsic AKI from ATN — initiate continuous renal replacement therapy (CRRT)

rationale: Pre-renal AKI is caused by decreased renal perfusion and is characterized by: low urine sodium (< 20 mEq/L), high urine specific gravity (> 1.020), BUN:Creatinine ratio > 20:1 (here 68/3.8 ≈ 18, borderline), and oliguria. The kidney is trying to conserve sodium and concentrate urine in response to perceived volume depletion. Urine Na 8 mEq/L and SG 1.032 are strongly pre-renal. The first intervention is a fluid challenge (typically 500 mL isotonic crystalloid) and reassessing urine output. If UO responds, pre-renal confirmed. Furosemide in pre-renal AKI worsens hypovolemia. CRRT is premature without determining reversibility. Catheterization is indicated if post-renal is suspected (high residual volume).
tags: Renal / Acute Kidney Injury

Pre-renal AKI markers: urine Na < 20, FENa < 1%, high SG, high BUN:Cr ratio. Kidney is compensating. Treat with fluids. Intrinsic AKI (ATN): urine Na > 40, FENa > 2%, isosthenuria (SG ~1.010). Don't give lasix to a dry kidney.
takeaway: Pre-renal AKI markers: urine Na < 20, FENa < 1%, high SG, high BUN:Cr ratio. Kidney is compensating. Treat with fluids. Intrinsic AKI (ATN): urine Na > 40, FENa > 2%, isosthenuria (SG ~1.010). Don't give lasix to a dry kidney.

visualRationale.title: Pre-renal vs. intrinsic AKI: urine tells the story
visualRationale.caption: The kidney conserves Na+ and concentrates urine in pre-renal — it is still functioning.
visualRationale.conclusion: Low urine Na + high SG = pre-renal → give fluids. High urine Na + isosthenuria = ATN.

stem: A patient on continuous renal replacement therapy (CRRT) suddenly develops hemodynamic instability with BP dropping from 112/70 to 72/44 mmHg. The CRRT circuit alarm activates indicating a clotted filter. What is the priority nursing action?

options.text: Increase the vasopressor rate and continue running CRRT through the clotted filter
Discontinue CRRT, return blood to the patient if possible, and notify the provider and CRRT team
Administer a heparin bolus to attempt to clear the clot from the filter
Decrease the CRRT blood flow rate and reassess in 30 minutes

rationale: A clotted CRRT filter is a mechanical failure requiring circuit takedown. Running blood through a clotted filter is dangerous — it increases extracorporeal resistance, reduces ultrafiltration, and can cause hemolysis or circuit rupture. When hemodynamic instability accompanies circuit clotting, the priority is to: (1) take the circuit down, (2) return any remaining patient blood in the circuit (per protocol and if the line is patent) to prevent additional blood loss, (3) notify the provider and CRRT team to assess anticoagulation and set up a new circuit. Bolusing heparin into a clotted filter does not dissolve the clot and is inappropriate. Continuing to run through a clotted circuit worsens the situation.
tags: Renal / Renal Replacement Therapy

Clotted CRRT circuit + hemodynamic instability: STOP CRRT → return blood if able → call provider → new circuit. A clotted filter is not salvageable. Preventing clotting (adequate anticoagulation, optimal flow rates) is better than reacting to it.
takeaway: Clotted CRRT circuit + hemodynamic instability: STOP CRRT → return blood if able → call provider → new circuit. A clotted filter is not salvageable. Preventing clotting (adequate anticoagulation, optimal flow rates) is better than reacting to it.

stem: A nurse enters a patient's room and finds them unresponsive with no pulse. CPR is initiated. The monitor shows pulseless ventricular tachycardia (pVT). Two minutes of CPR have been completed and the defibrillator is ready. What is the correct sequence of actions?

options.text: Administer epinephrine 1 mg IV, then defibrillate, then resume CPR
Defibrillate (200J biphasic), resume CPR immediately for 2 minutes, then reassess rhythm — administer epinephrine after the second shock
Defibrillate, administer amiodarone 300 mg IV, then resume CPR
Defibrillate twice in rapid succession before resuming CPR to maximize shock efficacy

rationale: Per current ACLS guidelines, pVT/VF is treated with: immediate defibrillation (200J biphasic) → resume CPR immediately for 2 minutes (do not pause to reassess immediately after shock) → rhythm check at 2 minutes. Epinephrine 1 mg IV is given every 3-5 minutes, starting after the first or second shock cycle (not before the first shock in shockable rhythms — defibrillation is the first-line treatment). Amiodarone 300 mg (or lidocaine) is given after the third shock if still in refractory VF/pVT. Double sequential defibrillation is not standard practice. Giving epi before the first shock is not recommended — shock first.
tags: Cardiac / Cardiac Arrest / ACLS

ACLS shockable rhythm sequence: Shock (200J) → CPR 2 min → Rhythm check → Shock again if still VF/pVT → CPR 2 min → Epi 1mg → Shock if persists → Amiodarone 300mg. SHOCK is always the first action in VF/pVT.
takeaway: ACLS shockable rhythm sequence: Shock (200J) → CPR 2 min → Rhythm check → Shock again if still VF/pVT → CPR 2 min → Epi 1mg → Shock if persists → Amiodarone 300mg. SHOCK is always the first action in VF/pVT.

visualRationale.title: ACLS shockable rhythm (VF/pVT) sequence
visualRationale.caption: Defibrillation is always first. CPR before drugs — every 2-minute cycle.
visualRationale.conclusion: SHOCK first. Never give epinephrine before the first defibrillation in shockable rhythms.

stem: A patient achieves return of spontaneous circulation (ROSC) after 18 minutes of cardiac arrest from VF. The patient remains unresponsive. Which post-ROSC intervention is HIGHEST priority?

options.text: Perform emergent coronary angiography regardless of rhythm or 12-lead findings
Initiate targeted temperature management (TTM) at 32-36°C and obtain 12-lead ECG to evaluate for STEMI
Administer methylprednisolone to prevent post-arrest neurological injury
Extubate the patient to assess neurological function and GCS

rationale: Post-ROSC priorities follow the 'H's and T's' framework and post-cardiac arrest care bundles: (1) Targeted temperature management (TTM) at 32-36°C for 24 hours is neuroprotective for comatose survivors and is a Class I recommendation. (2) Obtain 12-lead ECG immediately — STEMI post-arrest requires emergent PCI regardless of neurological status. (3) Avoid hypoxia (SpO2 94-98%), hyperoxia, hypotension, hypo/hyperglycemia, and hyperthermia. Emergent angio is indicated for STEMI post-ROSC but should not be done before the 12-lead confirms the need. Steroids are not indicated post-arrest for neuroprotection. The patient remains intubated and sedated during TTM — extubation is not appropriate acutely.
tags: Cardiac / Cardiac Arrest / Post-Arrest Care

Post-ROSC priorities: 12-lead ECG (STEMI → cath lab), TTM 32-36°C for 24h (neuroprotection), avoid secondary brain injury (hypoxia, hypotension, hyperthermia, hyperglycemia). Neurological prognosis assessment occurs only after 72h post-normothermia.
takeaway: Post-ROSC priorities: 12-lead ECG (STEMI → cath lab), TTM 32-36°C for 24h (neuroprotection), avoid secondary brain injury (hypoxia, hypotension, hyperthermia, hyperglycemia). Neurological prognosis assessment occurs only after 72h post-normothermia.

visualRationale.title: Post-ROSC care bundle — 4 immediate priorities
visualRationale.caption: After cardiac arrest, secondary brain injury is the #1 preventable cause of death.

nodes.label: 12-lead ECG immediately
nodes.value: → STEMI? → emergent PCI regardless of neuro status

visualRationale.conclusion: TTM + 12-lead + avoid secondary injury = the post-ROSC bundle. Do not extubate acutely.

stem: A neurosurgical ICU patient post-pituitary surgery develops polyuria with urine output of 800 mL/hr, serum sodium of 154 mEq/L, serum osmolality 320 mOsm/kg, and urine specific gravity of 1.001. Which condition is this and what is the FIRST nursing intervention?

options.text: SIADH — restrict fluids to 1000 mL/day and notify provider
Central diabetes insipidus — administer desmopressin (DDAVP) as ordered and replace urinary fluid losses IV
Cerebral salt wasting — administer hypertonic saline
Psychogenic polydipsia — restrict fluid intake and reassure the patient

rationale: Central diabetes insipidus (DI) after pituitary surgery is caused by deficiency of ADH (antidiuretic hormone/vasopressin). Classic features: massive polyuria (often > 300 mL/hr), very dilute urine (SG 1.001-1.005, osmolality < 200), hypernatremia, and high serum osmolality. Treatment: (1) DDAVP (desmopressin) is the synthetic ADH analog of choice, administered IV/intranasal/SQ. (2) Replace urinary losses with hypotonic IV fluids (D5W or 0.45% NS) to correct hypernatremia gradually (reduce Na no faster than 10-12 mEq/L per 24 hours). SIADH is the opposite: hyponatremia + concentrated urine. Cerebral salt wasting causes hyponatremia + high urine sodium — not this presentation. Psychogenic polydipsia occurs in conscious patients who are drinking excessively — not applicable post-surgery.
tags: Endocrine / SIADH vs. DI

Central DI after pituitary surgery: polyuria + dilute urine (SG < 1.005) + hypernatremia = ADH deficiency. Treat with DDAVP + hypotonic fluid replacement. Do not over-correct sodium (max 10-12 mEq/L/24h) or risk cerebral edema.
takeaway: Central DI after pituitary surgery: polyuria + dilute urine (SG < 1.005) + hypernatremia = ADH deficiency. Treat with DDAVP + hypotonic fluid replacement. Do not over-correct sodium (max 10-12 mEq/L/24h) or risk cerebral edema.

visualRationale.title: Central DI vs. SIADH: opposite ADH extremes
visualRationale.caption: Pituitary surgery can cause either — urine and sodium labs point the way.
visualRationale.conclusion: SG <1.005 + hypernatremia = DI → DDAVP. SG >1.020 + hyponatremia = SIADH → restrict fluids.

stem: A 68-year-old male with ischemic cardiomyopathy is in the ICU. His telemetry suddenly shows wide-complex tachycardia at 190 bpm. He is diaphoretic, BP 72/40, and unresponsive to verbal stimuli. Which intervention is the highest priority?

options.text: Administer IV amiodarone 150 mg over 10 minutes
Perform synchronized cardioversion at 100 J
Perform immediate unsynchronized defibrillation at 200 J
Obtain a 12-lead ECG to confirm ventricular tachycardia

rationale: This patient is in pulseless ventricular tachycardia (VT) or ventricular fibrillation (VF) given the hemodynamic collapse. Pulseless VT/VF requires immediate unsynchronized defibrillation. Synchronized cardioversion (B) is used for unstable rhythms with a pulse. Amiodarone (A) is an adjunct after defibrillation, not the first step. Obtaining a 12-lead (D) wastes critical time in a pulseless patient.
tags: Cardiac Dysrhythmias
Pulseless VT = treat like VF: immediate unsynchronized defibrillation, no delay.
takeaway: Pulseless VT = treat like VF: immediate unsynchronized defibrillation, no delay.

stem: A 54-year-old female post-cardiac surgery develops a rhythm showing a prolonged QTc of 580 ms with polymorphic ventricular tachycardia that twists around the isoelectric baseline. Her potassium is 3.1 mEq/L and magnesium is 1.2 mg/dL. Which treatment is most appropriate?

options.text: IV amiodarone 150 mg bolus
IV magnesium sulfate 2 g over 5 minutes
Synchronized cardioversion at 200 J
IV lidocaine 1.5 mg/kg bolus

rationale: This is torsades de pointes (TdP), a form of polymorphic VT associated with QTc prolongation. IV magnesium sulfate is first-line treatment for TdP regardless of serum magnesium level. Amiodarone (A) further prolongs the QTc and is contraindicated in TdP. Synchronized cardioversion (C) is reserved for hemodynamically unstable TdP but does not prevent recurrence. Lidocaine (D) is not effective for TdP.
tags: Cardiac Dysrhythmias
Torsades de pointes = IV magnesium sulfate 2 g; avoid QT-prolonging agents like amiodarone.
takeaway: Torsades de pointes = IV magnesium sulfate 2 g; avoid QT-prolonging agents like amiodarone.

stem: A patient's rhythm strip shows no association between P waves and QRS complexes. The atrial rate is 80 bpm and the ventricular rate is 38 bpm. The QRS complexes are wide. The patient is lightheaded with BP 88/54. Which intervention is indicated?

options.text: IV atropine 0.5 mg
Transcutaneous pacing
IV adenosine 6 mg rapid push
Observation and serial ECGs

rationale: This is complete (third-degree) heart block with a ventricular escape rhythm (rate 38, wide QRS = infranodal block). The patient is symptomatic and hemodynamically unstable. Transcutaneous pacing is the immediate intervention while preparing for transvenous pacing. Atropine (A) works at the AV node level and is ineffective for infranodal block with wide QRS escape. Adenosine (C) would be dangerous and is used for SVT. Observation (D) is inappropriate with hemodynamic compromise.
tags: Cardiac Dysrhythmias
Complete heart block with wide QRS escape and hemodynamic instability = transcutaneous pacing; atropine is ineffective for infranodal block.
takeaway: Complete heart block with wide QRS escape and hemodynamic instability = transcutaneous pacing; atropine is ineffective for infranodal block.

stem: A 72-year-old presents with SVT at 200 bpm. The 12-lead ECG shows a right bundle branch block (RBBB) pattern with an RSR' in V1. His BP is 110/70 and he is alert. Vagal maneuvers failed. Which statement guides next management?

options.text: The wide complex rules out SVT; treat as VT with amiodarone
Adenosine 6 mg IV can be given safely as it may diagnose and treat SVT with aberrancy
Verapamil is preferred over adenosine because it is more effective for wide-complex tachycardia
Immediate synchronized cardioversion is mandatory for all wide-complex tachycardias

rationale: SVT with aberrant conduction (e.g., RBBB) mimics VT on ECG. In a hemodynamically stable patient with a wide-complex tachycardia suspected to be SVT with aberrancy, adenosine 6 mg IV is safe and diagnostic — it will terminate SVT and transiently block VT to reveal the underlying rhythm. Verapamil (C) is dangerous if the rhythm is actually VT, as it can cause hemodynamic collapse. Cardioversion (D) is not mandatory in stable patients. Automatically treating as VT (A) without adenosine trial is overly aggressive in stable patients.
tags: Cardiac Dysrhythmias
Stable wide-complex tachycardia that may be SVT with aberrancy: adenosine is safe and diagnostic; never give verapamil to undifferentiated wide-complex tachycardia.
takeaway: Stable wide-complex tachycardia that may be SVT with aberrancy: adenosine is safe and diagnostic; never give verapamil to undifferentiated wide-complex tachycardia.

stem: A 61-year-old male presents with crushing chest pain. His 12-lead ECG shows ST elevation in leads II, III, and aVF with reciprocal ST depression in leads I and aVL. He develops hypotension and bradycardia when nitroglycerin is administered. Which action is most appropriate?

options.text: Increase the nitroglycerin infusion rate to improve preload
Administer IV fluids 250–500 mL normal saline and withhold further nitrates
Administer IV metoprolol to control heart rate
Place the patient in Trendelenburg position and administer atropine

rationale: ST elevation in II, III, aVF indicates inferior STEMI, which involves the RCA. The RCA also supplies the right ventricle in ~50% of patients, causing right ventricular MI (RVMI). RVMI is preload-dependent; nitroglycerin reduces preload and causes severe hypotension in RVMI — it is contraindicated. IV fluid bolus is the first-line treatment. Metoprolol (C) causes further bradycardia and hypotension. Trendelenburg (D) is not recommended and atropine alone does not address the hemodynamic problem.
tags: ACS 12-Lead Patterns
Inferior STEMI + hypotension after nitroglycerin = suspect right ventricular MI; give IV fluids, hold nitrates.
takeaway: Inferior STEMI + hypotension after nitroglycerin = suspect right ventricular MI; give IV fluids, hold nitrates.

stem: A 58-year-old female in the CCU 4 days post-anterior STEMI suddenly develops a new harsh holosystolic murmur at the left sternal border, worsening dyspnea, and SpO2 dropping to 88% on 4L NC. Chest X-ray shows pulmonary edema. Swan-Ganz catheter shows a step-up in oxygen saturation from the RA to the RV. Which post-MI complication is most likely?

options.text: Papillary muscle rupture with acute mitral regurgitation
Ventricular septal defect (VSD)
Left ventricular free wall rupture
Cardiac tamponade

rationale: The step-up in oxygen saturation from RA to RV on the pulmonary artery catheter is pathognomonic for a left-to-right shunt — a post-MI ventricular septal defect (VSD). Oxygenated blood from the LV shunts into the RV, raising RV O2 saturation. Papillary muscle rupture (A) causes acute MR with pulmonary edema and a murmur but no O2 step-up. Free wall rupture (C) causes tamponade, not an intracardiac shunt. Tamponade (D) presents with Beck's triad and pulsus paradoxus, not an O2 step-up.
tags: Post-MI Complications
Oxygen saturation step-up from RA to RV on PA catheter after MI = ventricular septal defect.
takeaway: Oxygen saturation step-up from RA to RV on PA catheter after MI = ventricular septal defect.

stem: Two ICU patients are both hypotensive with MAP <65 mmHg. Patient A has CO 1.8 L/min, SVR 1800 dyn/s/cm5, PCWP 24 mmHg, and cool/mottled extremities. Patient B has CO 8.5 L/min, SVR 350 dyn/s/cm5, PCWP 8 mmHg, and warm/flushed extremities. Which correctly pairs each patient with their shock type?

options.text: Patient A = distributive shock; Patient B = cardiogenic shock
Patient A = cardiogenic shock; Patient B = distributive shock
Both patients have cardiogenic shock with different presentations
Patient A = obstructive shock; Patient B = hypovolemic shock

rationale: Cardiogenic shock (Patient A): low CO, high SVR (compensatory vasoconstriction), elevated PCWP (pulmonary congestion), cool extremities (poor perfusion). Distributive shock such as septic shock (Patient B): high CO (hyperdynamic state), low SVR (vasodilation), low PCWP (relative hypovolemia), warm/flushed extremities (vasodilation). This distinction is critical because treatment is opposite: cardiogenic shock needs inotropes/diuretics, while distributive shock needs vasopressors/fluids.
tags: Cardiogenic vs Distributive Shock
Cardiogenic shock: low CO + high SVR + high PCWP. Distributive shock: high CO + low SVR + low PCWP.
takeaway: Cardiogenic shock: low CO + high SVR + high PCWP. Distributive shock: high CO + low SVR + low PCWP.

stem: A 65-year-old male is 5 days post-anterior STEMI. He suddenly becomes severely dyspneic with new S3 gallop, an apical holosystolic murmur radiating to the axilla, and flash pulmonary edema. Echocardiogram shows a flail posterior mitral leaflet. Which complication and mechanism are correctly identified?

options.text: VSD due to septal infarction causing left-to-right shunt
Papillary muscle rupture causing acute severe mitral regurgitation
Left ventricular aneurysm causing paradoxical wall motion
Dressler syndrome causing pericardial effusion

rationale: Papillary muscle rupture is a mechanical complication of MI occurring 3–7 days post-infarct. The flail mitral leaflet on echo, apical holosystolic murmur radiating to the axilla, and flash pulmonary edema confirm acute severe MR. VSD (A) would show an O2 step-up on PA catheter. LV aneurysm (C) does not cause acute murmur or flash pulmonary edema. Dressler syndrome (D) presents weeks later with pleuritis and fever.
tags: Post-MI Complications
Papillary muscle rupture: flail mitral leaflet on echo + apical murmur radiating to axilla + flash pulmonary edema 3–7 days post-MI.
takeaway: Papillary muscle rupture: flail mitral leaflet on echo + apical murmur radiating to axilla + flash pulmonary edema 3–7 days post-MI.

stem: A patient in cardiogenic shock after anterior MI has BP 80/50, HR 118, CO 1.6 L/min, and SVR 2100 dyn/s/cm5. After 2 L of IV fluid, no improvement. Which vasoactive agent is most appropriate as initial pharmacologic support?

options.text: Norepinephrine 0.1–0.3 mcg/kg/min
Dobutamine 5–10 mcg/kg/min
Vasopressin 0.03–0.04 units/min
Phenylephrine 100–200 mcg/min

rationale: The primary problem in cardiogenic shock is pump failure (low CO). Dobutamine is an inotrope that increases contractility and CO. The SVR is already elevated (compensatory), so a pure vasopressor (norepinephrine, vasopressin, phenylephrine) would worsen afterload and further reduce CO. Norepinephrine (A) may be added if dobutamine causes hypotension, but dobutamine alone is the first choice for cardiogenic shock without refractory hypotension. Vasopressin (C) and phenylephrine (D) are pure vasopressors contraindicated as first agents in cardiogenic shock.
tags: Cardiogenic vs Distributive Shock
Cardiogenic shock first-line pharmacology = dobutamine (inotrope); vasopressors worsen afterload when CO is already low.
takeaway: Cardiogenic shock first-line pharmacology = dobutamine (inotrope); vasopressors worsen afterload when CO is already low.

stem: A patient on day 3 of an ICU admission for sepsis develops atrial fibrillation with rapid ventricular response (RVR) at 140 bpm. BP is 104/62. He is on IV norepinephrine 0.15 mcg/kg/min. Which management strategy is most appropriate?

options.text: Immediate synchronized cardioversion at 200 J
IV diltiazem 0.25 mg/kg over 2 minutes
Rate control with IV metoprolol 5 mg
Treat the underlying sepsis and consider rate control with IV amiodarone

rationale: New-onset AF in sepsis is often caused by the systemic inflammatory state and hemodynamic stress. IV amiodarone is preferred in hemodynamically compromised patients on vasopressors because it provides rate control with less negative inotropy than beta-blockers or calcium channel blockers. IV diltiazem (B) and metoprolol (C) can cause profound hypotension in vasopressor-dependent patients. Cardioversion (A) is appropriate for unstable AF but AF is likely to recur without treating sepsis.
tags: Cardiac Dysrhythmias
AF with RVR in sepsis: treat the underlying cause first; use amiodarone for rate control in hemodynamically compromised patients on vasopressors.
takeaway: AF with RVR in sepsis: treat the underlying cause first; use amiodarone for rate control in hemodynamically compromised patients on vasopressors.

stem: A 22-year-old male arrives after a motor vehicle accident with a C5 spinal cord injury. BP is 78/50, HR 48, and he has warm, dry skin below the level of injury. Which type of shock is this, and what is the first-line vasopressor?

options.text: Hypovolemic shock; give IV normal saline 2 L bolus
Spinal shock; give IV atropine and elevate legs
Neurogenic shock; give IV norepinephrine and maintain MAP ≥85 mmHg
Distributive shock from hemorrhage; give 2 units packed red blood cells

rationale: Neurogenic shock results from disruption of sympathetic tone in cervical/high thoracic SCI, causing vasodilation (warm skin, low SVR) and bradycardia (loss of sympathetic cardiac acceleration). MAP target is 85–90 mmHg for the first 7 days to preserve spinal cord perfusion. Norepinephrine is preferred (alpha + beta1 activity addresses both hypotension and bradycardia). Hypovolemic shock (A) presents with cool/clammy skin and tachycardia. Spinal shock (B) refers to temporary flaccid paralysis and areflexia below the lesion — a neurological, not hemodynamic, phenomenon. PRBC (D) is used for hemorrhagic shock, not neurogenic shock.
tags: Acute Spinal Cord Injury
Neurogenic shock = bradycardia + hypotension + warm skin after SCI; first-line vasopressor is norepinephrine; target MAP ≥85 mmHg for 7 days.
takeaway: Neurogenic shock = bradycardia + hypotension + warm skin after SCI; first-line vasopressor is norepinephrine; target MAP ≥85 mmHg for 7 days.

stem: A patient with a T4 complete spinal cord injury becomes suddenly hypertensive (BP 210/120) with pounding headache, flushing and diaphoresis above the lesion, and pallor below. His Foley catheter is found to be kinked. Which action is the priority?

options.text: Administer IV labetalol 20 mg for hypertensive emergency
Elevate the head of bed 90° and immediately relieve the triggering stimulus
Place the patient supine and administer IV phenylephrine
Administer sublingual nitroglycerin and call neurosurgery

rationale: This is autonomic dysreflexia (AD), a life-threatening hypertensive emergency unique to SCI at T6 or above, triggered by a noxious stimulus below the level of injury (most commonly bladder distension). First step: sit the patient upright (reduces BP via orthostatic effect) and immediately identify and remove the triggering stimulus. Relieving the catheter kink will often resolve the episode. IV antihypertensives (A) are used only if the BP does not drop after stimulus removal. Supine position (C) worsens hypertension. Phenylephrine is contraindicated as it raises BP further.
tags: Acute Spinal Cord Injury
Autonomic dysreflexia: sit upright, find and remove the stimulus (usually distended bladder/bowel) — antihypertensives are second-line only.
takeaway: Autonomic dysreflexia: sit upright, find and remove the stimulus (usually distended bladder/bowel) — antihypertensives are second-line only.

stem: A 45-year-old woman has been seizing for 35 minutes despite IV lorazepam 4 mg and IV fosphenytoin 20 mg/kg PE. She is now in refractory status epilepticus (RSE). Which intervention is most appropriate?

options.text: Repeat IV lorazepam 2 mg and increase fosphenytoin dose
Initiate IV propofol or midazolam infusion with continuous EEG monitoring
Administer oral levetiracetam 1000 mg via NG tube
Perform emergent EEG and await results before further treatment

rationale: Refractory status epilepticus (RSE) is defined as seizures persisting despite adequate doses of two or more first-line anticonvulsants (benzodiazepine + second agent). The next step is anesthetic-dose therapy: IV propofol, midazolam, or pentobarbital infusion with continuous EEG monitoring to achieve burst suppression. Repeating benzodiazepines (A) is ineffective once RSE is established. Oral/NG levetiracetam (C) has too slow an onset for active seizures. Waiting for EEG (D) without treating is dangerous — every minute of seizure activity causes neuronal injury.
tags: Status Epilepticus
Refractory status epilepticus (after benzo + second agent) = anesthetic infusion (propofol or midazolam) + continuous EEG monitoring.
takeaway: Refractory status epilepticus (after benzo + second agent) = anesthetic infusion (propofol or midazolam) + continuous EEG monitoring.

stem: A 67-year-old hypertensive male presents with sudden onset severe headache ('worst of his life'), nuchal rigidity, and photophobia. Non-contrast CT head is negative. Which diagnosis must be excluded, and what is the next step?

options.text: Ischemic stroke; administer IV tPA within 4.5 hours of onset
Hypertensive encephalopathy; administer IV labetalol to lower MAP by 25%
Subarachnoid hemorrhage; perform lumbar puncture to detect xanthochromia
Meningitis; obtain blood cultures and start IV antibiotics immediately

rationale: Thunderclap headache with nuchal rigidity and photophobia is subarachnoid hemorrhage (SAH) until proven otherwise. Non-contrast CT detects ~95% of SAH within 6 hours but sensitivity decreases with time. A negative CT does not exclude SAH. Lumbar puncture looking for xanthochromia (yellow CSF from RBC breakdown) is the definitive next step. tPA (A) is contraindicated in hemorrhagic stroke. Labetalol (B) for hypertensive encephalopathy is incorrect given this classic SAH presentation. Meningitis (D) is possible but the 'worst of my life' headache with negative CT points to SAH first.
tags: Stroke Differentiation
Thunderclap headache + negative CT = LP for xanthochromia to exclude subarachnoid hemorrhage.
takeaway: Thunderclap headache + negative CT = LP for xanthochromia to exclude subarachnoid hemorrhage.

stem: A 55-year-old female presents with right-sided hemiplegia and aphasia, onset 2 hours ago. Non-contrast CT shows no hemorrhage. Which statement correctly describes IV alteplase (tPA) eligibility?

options.text: tPA is absolutely contraindicated if BP is above 140/90 mmHg at the time of administration
tPA can be given if BP is controlled to <185/110 mmHg and there are no other absolute contraindications
tPA should be withheld if the patient is on any anticoagulant regardless of INR
tPA is indicated up to 6 hours after symptom onset for all ischemic stroke patients

rationale: For IV alteplase in ischemic stroke, BP must be <185/110 mmHg at time of administration and maintained <180/105 mmHg for 24 hours post-infusion. BP can be treated with IV labetalol or nicardipine to achieve this threshold. The window is 0–4.5 hours for most patients. Anticoagulants are a contraindication only if INR >1.7 or LMWH received in the prior 24 hours — not all anticoagulants (C is wrong). The 6-hour window (D) applies to endovascular thrombectomy, not IV tPA.
tags: Stroke Differentiation
IV tPA eligibility: no hemorrhage on CT, symptom onset <4.5 hours, BP controlled to <185/110 mmHg before administration.
takeaway: IV tPA eligibility: no hemorrhage on CT, symptom onset <4.5 hours, BP controlled to <185/110 mmHg before administration.

stem: A 30-year-old male is to be evaluated for brain death following anoxic brain injury. Before formal brain death examination can be conducted, which prerequisite condition must be confirmed?

options.text: Flat EEG for at least 30 minutes
Absence of confounders: normothermia, no sedating drugs, no severe metabolic derangement, and adequate MAP
No spontaneous respirations for at least 10 minutes off the ventilator
Fixed and dilated pupils bilaterally

rationale: Brain death determination requires ruling out reversible mimics: hypothermia (core temp <36°C), CNS depressants (sedatives, paralytics, barbiturates), severe metabolic disturbances (hyponatremia, hypoglycemia, hepatic failure), and hypotension. Only after these confounders are excluded can clinical brain death examination proceed. EEG (A) is not required for brain death diagnosis by clinical criteria in most US guidelines. Apnea testing (C) is one component of the exam but not a standalone prerequisite. Fixed pupils (D) are a component of the neurological exam, not a prerequisite.
tags: Brain Death
Brain death prerequisites: normothermia, no CNS depressants, no metabolic confounders, adequate hemodynamics — all must be confirmed before clinical exam.
takeaway: Brain death prerequisites: normothermia, no CNS depressants, no metabolic confounders, adequate hemodynamics — all must be confirmed before clinical exam.

stem: A 14-year-old with a ventriculoperitoneal (VP) shunt for hydrocephalus presents with headache, vomiting, and altered mental status. Which assessment finding is most consistent with VP shunt malfunction?

options.text: Bilateral equal pupils 3 mm reactive, GCS 15
Boggy reservoir, bradycardia, and hypertension (Cushing's triad)
Fever 39.2°C with nuchal rigidity suggesting shunt infection
Papilledema, Cushing's triad, and failure of the shunt reservoir to refill after compression

rationale: VP shunt malfunction causes increased ICP. Clinical signs include Cushing's triad (hypertension + bradycardia + irregular respirations), papilledema, and altered mental status. The shunt reservoir tap test: compressing and releasing the shunt reservoir — if it fails to refill, there is distal obstruction; if it cannot be compressed, there is proximal obstruction. Option C (fever + nuchal rigidity) suggests shunt infection, not pure malfunction. Options A and B are incomplete presentations.
tags: Post-Neurosurgery Monitoring
VP shunt malfunction = signs of elevated ICP (Cushing's triad, papilledema, altered mental status) + shunt reservoir that fails to refill after compression.
takeaway: VP shunt malfunction = signs of elevated ICP (Cushing's triad, papilledema, altered mental status) + shunt reservoir that fails to refill after compression.

stem: A patient 12 hours post-craniotomy for subdural hematoma evacuation becomes increasingly difficult to arouse, with anisocoria (right pupil 6 mm non-reactive; left 3 mm reactive) and progressive left-sided weakness. What is the most critical concern and immediate action?

options.text: Increased ICP from re-bleeding or cerebral edema; notify neurosurgery immediately and obtain CT head STAT
Opioid over-sedation; administer IV naloxone 0.4 mg
Surgical site infection; obtain blood cultures and start broad-spectrum antibiotics
Cerebral vasospasm; start IV nimodipine and fluid resuscitation

rationale: Anisocoria with a fixed, dilated pupil ipsilateral to the craniotomy with contralateral motor weakness is a classic sign of uncal herniation due to re-bleeding or cerebral edema compressing CN III. This is a neurosurgical emergency. Notify neurosurgery immediately and obtain emergent CT head. Naloxone (B) does not explain focal neurological signs or anisocoria. Infection (C) presents over days with fever, not acute neurological deterioration. Vasospasm (D) is a complication of subarachnoid hemorrhage, not craniotomy for subdural hematoma.
tags: Post-Neurosurgery Monitoring
Post-craniotomy deterioration with fixed dilated pupil + contralateral weakness = herniation from re-bleeding; STAT CT and neurosurgery notification.
takeaway: Post-craniotomy deterioration with fixed dilated pupil + contralateral weakness = herniation from re-bleeding; STAT CT and neurosurgery notification.

stem: A patient presents with loss of pain and temperature sensation below T8 bilaterally, but preserved proprioception, vibration, and motor function. Which spinal cord syndrome is present, and what is the typical mechanism?

options.text: Central cord syndrome from hyperextension injury in older patients
Brown-Séquard syndrome from hemisection of the cord
Anterior cord syndrome from anterior spinal artery occlusion or flexion injury
Posterior cord syndrome from compression of dorsal columns

rationale: Anterior cord syndrome: bilateral loss of motor function and pain/temperature sensation (spinothalamic and corticospinal tracts anteriorly) with preserved proprioception and vibration (posterior columns intact). Caused by anterior spinal artery occlusion or burst fracture. Central cord syndrome (A) causes greater motor weakness in upper than lower extremities with variable sensory loss — classic in elderly with hyperextension. Brown-Séquard (B) is hemicord lesion: ipsilateral motor/proprioception loss, contralateral pain/temperature loss. Posterior cord syndrome (D) is rare and causes isolated proprioception/vibration loss.
tags: Acute Spinal Cord Injury
Anterior cord syndrome: bilateral motor + pain/temperature loss, proprioception preserved — caused by anterior spinal artery occlusion or flexion injury.
takeaway: Anterior cord syndrome: bilateral motor + pain/temperature loss, proprioception preserved — caused by anterior spinal artery occlusion or flexion injury.

stem: A 52-year-old male with epilepsy arrives via EMS actively seizing. EMS administered diazepam 10 mg IM 15 minutes ago. He continues to seize. Which is the most appropriate next medication?

options.text: IV lorazepam 4 mg IV push
IV fosphenytoin 20 mg/kg PE at 150 mg PE/min
IV valproic acid 40 mg/kg over 10 minutes
IV ketamine 1–2 mg/kg push

rationale: This patient received a benzodiazepine (first-line) and continues to seize — this is established status epilepticus. The next step per AES/NCS guidelines is a second-line agent: IV fosphenytoin (20 mg/kg PE), IV valproic acid (40 mg/kg), or IV levetiracetam (60 mg/kg). Fosphenytoin is listed here as the clearest correct answer. Valproic acid (C) is also correct as a second-line agent and either is acceptable. Ketamine (D) is used for refractory SE, not established SE.
tags: Status Epilepticus
Established status epilepticus after benzodiazepine = second-line agent: fosphenytoin, valproic acid, or levetiracetam IV.
takeaway: Established status epilepticus after benzodiazepine = second-line agent: fosphenytoin, valproic acid, or levetiracetam IV.

stem: A 58-year-old male with cirrhosis presents with hematemesis and melena. BP 88/50, HR 122. He has spider angiomata and ascites. Endoscopy reveals actively bleeding esophageal varices. After initial resuscitation, which pharmacologic therapy should be initiated immediately?

options.text: IV pantoprazole 80 mg bolus then 8 mg/hr infusion
IV octreotide 50 mcg bolus then 50 mcg/hr infusion
IV vasopressin 0.4 units/min
Oral propranolol 20 mg for portal hypertension reduction

rationale: Octreotide (somatostatin analog) reduces splanchnic blood flow and portal pressure, decreasing variceal bleeding. It should be started immediately upon suspicion of variceal hemorrhage and continued for 3–5 days post-endoscopy. PPIs (A) are indicated for non-variceal bleeds (peptic ulcer disease) but not variceal bleeding. Vasopressin (C) is an older agent with significant cardiac side effects, largely replaced by octreotide. Oral propranolol (D) is for primary/secondary prophylaxis of varices, not active hemorrhage management.
tags: GI Hemorrhage
Acute variceal hemorrhage: octreotide immediately + urgent endoscopy; PPIs are for non-variceal (ulcer) bleeds.
takeaway: Acute variceal hemorrhage: octreotide immediately + urgent endoscopy; PPIs are for non-variceal (ulcer) bleeds.

stem: A 72-year-old female on NSAIDs presents with frank hematochezia (bright red blood per rectum), HR 105, BP 100/60. She denies upper GI symptoms. Which statement correctly differentiates her likely bleed source?

options.text: Melena always indicates an upper GI source and hematochezia always indicates a lower GI source
Hematochezia with hemodynamic instability in this patient is most likely from a brisk upper GI bleed until proven otherwise
Upper GI bleeds always present with coffee-ground emesis and hematemesis
An NG tube lavage that is negative for blood completely rules out upper GI bleeding

rationale: While hematochezia typically indicates a lower GI bleed, brisk upper GI hemorrhage (from a duodenal or gastric ulcer) with rapid transit can present as bright red rectal bleeding. In a hemodynamically unstable patient on NSAIDs, upper GI source must be excluded first. Melena typically indicates upper GI source but can occur with right colon bleeding (A is too absolute). Upper GI bleeds do not always present with hematemesis (C). NG lavage has approximately 15–25% false negative rate for duodenal ulcers (D is incorrect).
tags: GI Hemorrhage
Hematochezia + hemodynamic instability = consider brisk upper GI bleed; upper endoscopy before colonoscopy.
takeaway: Hematochezia + hemodynamic instability = consider brisk upper GI bleed; upper endoscopy before colonoscopy.

stem: A 28-year-old female with acute liver failure from acetaminophen overdose develops confusion, asterixis, and INR 4.2. Which statement about coagulopathy management is most accurate?

options.text: FFP should be given immediately to correct the INR to <2.0 regardless of bleeding status
FFP or vitamin K should be given routinely because the elevated INR increases bleeding risk
INR should not be corrected with FFP unless active bleeding or invasive procedure is planned, as INR reflects synthetic dysfunction and volume-loading worsens portal hypertension
Vitamin K 10 mg IV daily will correct the INR within 2 hours in acute liver failure

rationale: In acute liver failure, elevated INR reflects hepatic synthetic dysfunction but is a poor predictor of bleeding risk. Routine FFP correction is not recommended because it only temporarily lowers INR, volume overload worsens ascites and portal hypertension, and it confounds prognostic scoring (INR is used in MELD/King's College Criteria). FFP is reserved for active bleeding or pre-procedural coverage. Vitamin K (D) may help if deficiency contributes, but takes 24–48 hours and will not correct INR in pure synthetic failure.
tags: Acute Liver Failure
Acute liver failure coagulopathy: do not routinely correct INR with FFP; reserve for active bleeding or procedures — INR is prognostic, not a treatment target.
takeaway: Acute liver failure coagulopathy: do not routinely correct INR with FFP; reserve for active bleeding or procedures — INR is prognostic, not a treatment target.

stem: A patient with acute liver failure and Grade III hepatic encephalopathy (marked confusion, incoherent speech) is being managed in the ICU. Which intervention most directly addresses the pathophysiology of hepatic encephalopathy?

options.text: Lactulose 30 mL every 4–6 hours to achieve 2–4 loose stools per day
IV dextrose 50% to correct hypoglycemia
Head of bed elevated to 30° to reduce ICP
Restriction of all dietary protein

rationale: Hepatic encephalopathy is driven by ammonia accumulation from gut bacterial metabolism of protein. Lactulose is the first-line treatment — it acidifies the gut, converting ammonia (NH3) to ammonium (NH4+) which is not absorbed, and acts as a cathartic to evacuate nitrogenous waste. Target: 2–4 soft stools/day. Dextrose (B) treats hypoglycemia (common in ALF) but does not address encephalopathy directly. HOB 30° (C) reduces ICP in advanced ALF but does not treat the cause. Protein restriction (D) is outdated — adequate protein intake is essential.
tags: Acute Liver Failure
Hepatic encephalopathy first-line treatment = lactulose to reduce ammonia by acidifying gut and promoting catharsis; target 2–4 loose stools/day.
takeaway: Hepatic encephalopathy first-line treatment = lactulose to reduce ammonia by acidifying gut and promoting catharsis; target 2–4 loose stools/day.

stem: A septic patient develops oozing from IV sites, hematuria, and petechiae. Labs: platelets 42,000, PT 24 sec, aPTT 68 sec, fibrinogen 85 mg/dL, D-dimer markedly elevated. Which blood product is most urgently needed to address the most critical coagulopathy component?

options.text: Platelets — transfuse 1 apheresis unit to raise count above 50,000
Fresh frozen plasma (FFP) — replaces all clotting factors
Cryoprecipitate — replaces fibrinogen, factor VIII, and vWF; indicated for fibrinogen <150 mg/dL with active bleeding
Packed red blood cells — restore oxygen-carrying capacity

rationale: In DIC with active bleeding, the most critical lab abnormality here is severely low fibrinogen (85 mg/dL; normal 200–400 mg/dL). Fibrinogen <150 mg/dL with active bleeding is the primary indication for cryoprecipitate, which contains concentrated fibrinogen, factor VIII, vWF, and factor XIII. FFP (B) contains all factors but in dilute concentrations — large volumes needed to raise fibrinogen significantly, risking volume overload. Platelets (A) are indicated if count <50,000 with active bleeding but fibrinogen correction is more urgent here. PRBCs (D) address anemia, not coagulopathy.
tags: DIC
DIC with fibrinogen <150 mg/dL and active bleeding = cryoprecipitate; FFP for factor replacement, platelets for thrombocytopenia — each product has a specific indication.
takeaway: DIC with fibrinogen <150 mg/dL and active bleeding = cryoprecipitate; FFP for factor replacement, platelets for thrombocytopenia — each product has a specific indication.

stem: A patient with acute promyelocytic leukemia (APL) develops DIC. Which lab pattern is most consistent with consumptive coagulopathy (DIC) rather than liver failure?

options.text: Elevated PT, elevated aPTT, low fibrinogen, markedly elevated D-dimer, and low platelets
Elevated PT, elevated aPTT, low fibrinogen, normal D-dimer, and low platelets
Elevated PT only with normal fibrinogen, low platelets, and normal D-dimer
Low fibrinogen only with normal PT, aPTT, and platelets

rationale: DIC is characterized by simultaneous coagulation activation and fibrinolysis: elevated PT and aPTT (factor consumption), low fibrinogen (consumed), markedly elevated D-dimer (fibrin degradation products from fibrinolysis), and thrombocytopenia (platelet consumption). The hallmark distinguishing DIC from liver failure is markedly elevated D-dimer/FDPs, since fibrinolysis is the key pathologic feature. Liver failure also causes elevated PT/aPTT and low fibrinogen but D-dimer is only mildly elevated. Option B (normal D-dimer) would suggest liver failure rather than DIC.
tags: DIC
DIC hallmark: elevated PT + aPTT + low fibrinogen + low platelets + markedly elevated D-dimer (fibrinolysis); D-dimer distinguishes DIC from liver failure.
takeaway: DIC hallmark: elevated PT + aPTT + low fibrinogen + low platelets + markedly elevated D-dimer (fibrinolysis); D-dimer distinguishes DIC from liver failure.

stem: A patient 24 hours post-damage control laparotomy has received 14 L of crystalloid. Urine output has dropped to 10 mL/hr despite adequate MAP. He has tense abdominal distension, peak airway pressures of 42 cmH2O, and increasing vasopressor requirements. Intravesicular bladder pressure is 28 mmHg. Which intervention is indicated?

options.text: Increase vasopressor dose and diuretics to reduce fluid overload
Paracentesis to drain ascitic fluid
Surgical decompressive laparotomy
Increase PEEP to improve oxygenation

rationale: Abdominal compartment syndrome (ACS) is defined as intra-abdominal pressure (IAP) >20 mmHg with new organ dysfunction. This patient has IAP 28 mmHg with oliguria (renal failure), elevated peak airway pressures (respiratory failure), and hemodynamic instability — fulfilling ACS criteria. The only definitive treatment is surgical decompressive laparotomy. Vasopressors and diuretics (A) will not reduce IAP. Paracentesis (B) addresses free fluid but not edematous bowel causing ACS. Increasing PEEP (D) worsens hemodynamic compromise and does not address the cause.
tags: Abdominal Compartment Syndrome
Abdominal compartment syndrome (IAP >20 mmHg + organ dysfunction) = surgical decompressive laparotomy; no medical therapy is definitive.
takeaway: Abdominal compartment syndrome (IAP >20 mmHg + organ dysfunction) = surgical decompressive laparotomy; no medical therapy is definitive.

stem: A patient with acute pancreatitis has a Ranson's criteria score of 5 at 48 hours. His BUN has risen from 14 to 28 mg/dL and hematocrit has fallen from 44% to 32%. Which statement regarding his severity and management is most accurate?

options.text: Ranson's score ≥3 predicts severe pancreatitis with significant morbidity and mortality; aggressive fluid resuscitation and ICU care are warranted
Ranson's score alone predicts pancreatic necrosis; immediate CT with contrast is mandatory
Rising BUN indicates renal recovery and declining hematocrit indicates hemodilution from adequate fluids
Early ERCP is indicated within 12 hours for all severe pancreatitis regardless of etiology

rationale: Ranson's criteria: ≥3 points = severe pancreatitis with mortality 15–40%; ≥5 points carries approximately 40% mortality. Aggressive IV fluid resuscitation (Lactated Ringer's preferred over NS for its anti-inflammatory properties) is the cornerstone of management. Rising BUN in pancreatitis indicates pancreatic necrosis/third spacing and poor prognosis, not renal recovery (C is wrong). CT with contrast (B) is indicated to assess for necrosis but not emergently in all cases and carries risk of worsening AKI. Early ERCP (D) is only indicated if biliary pancreatitis with biliary obstruction or cholangitis.
tags: Acute Pancreatitis
Ranson's ≥3 = severe pancreatitis; rising BUN and falling hematocrit signal poor prognosis; treat with aggressive LR resuscitation in the ICU.
takeaway: Ranson's ≥3 = severe pancreatitis; rising BUN and falling hematocrit signal poor prognosis; treat with aggressive LR resuscitation in the ICU.

stem: An ICU patient on mechanical ventilation for ARDS is started on IV pantoprazole. Which is the primary indication for stress ulcer prophylaxis in critically ill patients?

options.text: All ICU patients require stress ulcer prophylaxis to prevent gastroparesis
Patients with coagulopathy (INR >1.5, platelet <50K) or mechanical ventilation >48 hours are at highest risk and benefit from prophylaxis
Stress ulcer prophylaxis should be oral omeprazole because IV PPIs increase C. difficile risk
H2 blockers are contraindicated in the ICU due to drug interactions with vasopressors

rationale: High-risk criteria for stress ulcer bleeding in the ICU include mechanical ventilation >48 hours and coagulopathy (INR >1.5 or platelets <50,000). These patients have significantly higher risk of clinically significant GI bleeding and benefit from prophylaxis. Not all ICU patients require prophylaxis — over-prescribing PPIs increases risk of C. difficile colitis, VAP, and hypomagnesemia (A is wrong). Both IV and oral formulations are used (C is incorrect). H2 blockers (famotidine) are appropriate alternatives, not contraindicated (D is wrong).
tags: GI Hemorrhage
Stress ulcer prophylaxis indications: mechanical ventilation >48 hours OR coagulopathy — not required for all ICU patients.
takeaway: Stress ulcer prophylaxis indications: mechanical ventilation >48 hours OR coagulopathy — not required for all ICU patients.

stem: To measure intravesicular bladder pressure as a surrogate for intra-abdominal pressure (IAP), the nurse instills 25 mL of sterile saline via the urinary catheter. After 1 minute equilibration, the pressure transducer reads 18 mmHg with the patient supine at end-expiration. Which interpretation and action are correct?

options.text: IAP 18 mmHg = normal; no intervention needed
IAP 18 mmHg = Grade II intra-abdominal hypertension (IAH); monitor closely, optimize positioning, remove unnecessary fluids, and reassess for organ dysfunction
IAP 18 mmHg = abdominal compartment syndrome; prepare for immediate surgical decompression
Bladder pressure measurement is only valid if measured with 60 mL of saline instilled

rationale: WSACS classification: Grade I = 12–15 mmHg, Grade II = 16–20 mmHg, Grade III = 21–25 mmHg, Grade IV >25 mmHg. IAP 18 mmHg = Grade II IAH, not yet ACS (which requires IAP >20 mmHg with new organ dysfunction). Interventions for Grade II include: head of bed ≤30°, NG decompression, removing excess fluid, optimizing sedation/analgesia, and frequent reassessment. Immediate surgery (C) is premature at 18 mmHg without organ dysfunction. Standard technique uses 25 mL, not 60 mL (D).
tags: Abdominal Compartment Syndrome
IAP 12–20 mmHg = intra-abdominal hypertension (IAH); ACS requires IAP >20 mmHg plus new organ dysfunction — treat the cause, reassess serially.
takeaway: IAP 12–20 mmHg = intra-abdominal hypertension (IAH); ACS requires IAP >20 mmHg plus new organ dysfunction — treat the cause, reassess serially.

stem: A 67-year-old male in the CVICU is post-op day 1 following mitral valve replacement. His pulmonary artery catheter readings are: PAP 42/24 mmHg, PCWP 22 mmHg, CVP 14 mmHg, CO 3.2 L/min. BP is 88/54 mmHg. Which interpretation BEST describes this hemodynamic profile?

options.text: Right ventricular failure with pulmonary hypertension
Left ventricular failure with elevated filling pressures
Distributive shock with low systemic vascular resistance
Hypovolemia with compensatory tachycardia

rationale: An elevated PCWP (normal 6–12 mmHg) of 22 mmHg reflects elevated left ventricular end-diastolic pressure, indicating left ventricular failure and fluid backup into the pulmonary circulation. The elevated PAP is secondary to the high PCWP. Low CO (normal 4–8 L/min) confirms impaired forward flow. CVP elevation indicates right-sided congestion from the failing left ventricle. Distributive shock would show low SVR and high CO. Hypovolemia would present with low CVP, low PCWP, and high SVR. Pure RV failure would show elevated CVP and PAP without the elevated PCWP.
tags: Hemodynamic Monitoring / PA Catheter
PCWP >18 mmHg = left heart failure / pulmonary congestion. Low CO + elevated PCWP + elevated PAP = classic cardiogenic shock pattern. PCWP reflects LVEDP when the mitral valve is intact.
takeaway: PCWP >18 mmHg = left heart failure / pulmonary congestion. Low CO + elevated PCWP + elevated PAP = classic cardiogenic shock pattern. PCWP reflects LVEDP when the mitral valve is intact.

stem: A 72-year-old woman with septic shock is on norepinephrine 0.15 mcg/kg/min and vasopressin 0.03 units/min. Her thermodilution CO is 8.4 L/min, BSA is 1.7 m², MAP is 65 mmHg, CVP is 6 mmHg. Calculate her cardiac index (CI) and systemic vascular resistance (SVR). Which result correctly identifies her current hemodynamic state?

options.text: CI 4.9 L/min/m², SVR 556 dyn·s·cm⁻⁵ — hyperdynamic with low resistance
CI 2.1 L/min/m², SVR 1120 dyn·s·cm⁻⁵ — cardiogenic pattern with high resistance
CI 4.9 L/min/m², SVR 1280 dyn·s·cm⁻⁵ — normal CI with borderline SVR
CI 3.2 L/min/m², SVR 556 dyn·s·cm⁻⁵ — compensated distributive shock

rationale: CI = CO ÷ BSA = 8.4 ÷ 1.7 = 4.94 L/min/m² (normal 2.5–4.0; this is elevated). SVR = [(MAP − CVP) ÷ CO] × 80 = [(65 − 6) ÷ 8.4] × 80 = [59 ÷ 8.4] × 80 = 7.02 × 80 ≈ 561 dyn·s·cm⁻⁵ (normal 800–1200; this is low). This hyperdynamic, low-SVR state is the hallmark of distributive (septic) shock: high CO due to inflammatory vasodilation, low SVR because of pathologic vasodilation, and vasopressors are compensating the MAP. The vasopressors are working but SVR remains below normal.
tags: Hemodynamic Monitoring / CO/CI/SVR
CI = CO ÷ BSA. SVR = [(MAP − CVP) ÷ CO] × 80. Septic shock = high CI + low SVR = hyperdynamic. Cardiogenic shock = low CI + high SVR = hypodynamic. Know both formulas cold.
takeaway: CI = CO ÷ BSA. SVR = [(MAP − CVP) ÷ CO] × 80. Septic shock = high CI + low SVR = hyperdynamic. Cardiogenic shock = low CI + high SVR = hypodynamic. Know both formulas cold.

stem: The CCU receives two patients simultaneously. Patient A: post-PE arrest, HR 138, BP 70/40, JVD present, trachea deviated left, SpO₂ 78% on 15L NRB. Patient B: 3 days post-abdominal surgery, HR 124, BP 82/50, T 39.8°C, WBC 28,000, lactate 5.2 mmol/L. Which correctly matches the shock type to hemodynamic mechanism?

options.text: Patient A = distributive shock; Patient B = obstructive shock
Patient A = obstructive shock; Patient B = distributive shock
Both patients have obstructive shock from different causes
Both patients have distributive shock; the tracheal deviation is incidental

rationale: Patient A has obstructive shock: massive PE causes outflow obstruction at the pulmonary vasculature, preventing right heart ejection → JVD from RV backup, hypotension, and severe hypoxemia. The tracheal deviation plus clinical context suggests tension pneumothorax may coexist. The mechanism is mechanical obstruction to flow. Patient B has distributive shock (sepsis): post-op infection causing systemic inflammatory vasodilation, high fever, leukocytosis, and elevated lactate indicating tissue hypoperfusion despite adequate (or high) cardiac output. Distributive = vasodilation; obstructive = mechanical blockage.
tags: Shock Differentiation / Distributive vs Obstructive

Obstructive shock = mechanical block to flow (PE, tension PTX, tamponade) → JVD + hypotension + no fever. Distributive shock = pathologic vasodilation (sepsis, anaphylaxis, neurogenic) → warm/flushed early, high fever, lactate elevation. Mechanism drives treatment.
takeaway: Obstructive shock = mechanical block to flow (PE, tension PTX, tamponade) → JVD + hypotension + no fever. Distributive shock = pathologic vasodilation (sepsis, anaphylaxis, neurogenic) → warm/flushed early, high fever, lactate elevation. Mechanism drives treatment.

stem: A 55-year-old presents to the ED with anaphylaxis after a bee sting. BP is 74/38 mmHg, HR 142, SpO₂ 91%, skin is flushed/warm with urticaria. He received epinephrine 0.3 mg IM 8 minutes ago with no improvement. Which SECOND-LINE intervention is MOST appropriate to address the hemodynamic instability?

options.text: Norepinephrine 0.1–0.2 mcg/kg/min IV infusion
Normal saline 250 mL bolus and reassess
Dopamine 5 mcg/kg/min IV infusion
Repeat epinephrine 0.3 mg IM and add IV diphenhydramine

rationale: Refractory anaphylactic shock unresponsive to epinephrine IM requires vasopressor support. Norepinephrine is first-line vasopressor for anaphylaxis refractory to epinephrine due to its strong alpha-1 (vasoconstriction) and moderate beta-1 (inotropy) effects, directly counteracting the massive histamine-mediated vasodilation. IV fluid boluses alone are insufficient for refractory hemodynamic collapse. Dopamine is second-line and less preferred vs. norepinephrine for anaphylaxis. Repeating IM epinephrine (3rd option) is appropriate if IV access is unavailable, but IV vasopressor is superior with access present.
tags: Shock Differentiation / Perfusion Assessment

Epinephrine IM = first-line anaphylaxis. If refractory after 2 doses or with established IV access, escalate to IV epinephrine infusion or norepinephrine. Anaphylaxis = distributive shock → vasopressors restore SVR.
takeaway: Epinephrine IM = first-line anaphylaxis. If refractory after 2 doses or with established IV access, escalate to IV epinephrine infusion or norepinephrine. Anaphylaxis = distributive shock → vasopressors restore SVR.

stem: A 63-year-old post-cardiac surgery patient has the following assessment: MAP 58 mmHg, ScvO₂ 54%, lactate 4.1 mmol/L rising from 2.3 two hours ago, urine output 12 mL/hr × 3 hours, capillary refill 4 seconds, skin mottled to knees. Which finding is the MOST reliable indicator that oxygen delivery is inadequate to meet tissue demands?

options.text: MAP 58 mmHg below target of 65 mmHg
ScvO₂ 54% with rising lactate indicating oxygen extraction > delivery
Urine output 12 mL/hr indicating renal hypoperfusion
Capillary refill 4 seconds indicating peripheral vasoconstriction

rationale: ScvO₂ (central venous oxygen saturation) normally 70–80% reflects the balance between oxygen delivery (DO₂) and consumption (VO₂). ScvO₂ of 54% means tissues are extracting far more oxygen than normal (>25% extraction), confirming DO₂ is severely inadequate. Combined with a rising lactate (anaerobic metabolism), this is the strongest evidence of global tissue hypoperfusion. Low MAP is a trigger, but MAP alone doesn't quantify tissue adequacy. Oliguria and mottling are signs of hypoperfusion but are less specific and quantitative than ScvO₂ + lactate trend together.
tags: Perfusion Assessment

ScvO₂ <70% = tissues compensating for low DO₂ by extracting more O₂. ScvO₂ <65% = critical. Rising lactate + falling ScvO₂ = most reliable evidence of DO₂/VO₂ mismatch requiring immediate intervention.
takeaway: ScvO₂ <70% = tissues compensating for low DO₂ by extracting more O₂. ScvO₂ <65% = critical. Rising lactate + falling ScvO₂ = most reliable evidence of DO₂/VO₂ mismatch requiring immediate intervention.

stem: A patient with ARDS is on volume-controlled ventilation with the following settings: FiO2 0.80, PEEP 10 cmH2O, tidal volume (TV) 480 mL, RR 22. The patient weighs 60 kg (ideal body weight). ABGs: pH 7.28, PaCO2 52 mmHg, PaO2 58 mmHg, SpO2 88%. The intensivist asks the nurse to assist in adjusting ventilator settings per the ARDSnet protocol. Which change is MOST consistent with lung-protective ventilation?

options.text: Increase tidal volume to 600 mL to improve CO2 clearance
Reduce tidal volume to 360 mL (6 mL/kg IBW) and increase RR to 28
Reduce PEEP to 5 cmH2O to decrease barotrauma risk
Increase FiO2 to 1.0 and decrease RR to 18 to allow permissive hypercapnia

rationale: The ARDSnet protocol mandates a tidal volume of 4-8 mL/kg ideal body weight (IBW), with a target of 6 mL/kg IBW. For a 60 kg IBW patient, TV = 360 mL. The current TV of 480 mL (8 mL/kg) is at the upper limit; reducing to 6 mL/kg is the priority lung-protective change. Increasing RR to compensate for reduced TV is appropriate to maintain minute ventilation and manage the pH 7.28 (permissive hypercapnia is acceptable down to pH 7.20 in ARDSnet). Increasing TV violates lung protection. Reducing PEEP worsens oxygenation in ARDS. Maximizing FiO2 without adjusting TV does not address barotrauma/volutrauma.
tags: Respiratory / Ventilator Management
ARDSnet lung protection: TV 4-8 mL/kg IBW (target 6), plateau pressure ≤ 30 cmH2O, adequate PEEP to maintain oxygenation, permissive hypercapnia allowed to pH ≥ 7.20.
takeaway: ARDSnet lung protection: TV 4-8 mL/kg IBW (target 6), plateau pressure ≤ 30 cmH2O, adequate PEEP to maintain oxygenation, permissive hypercapnia allowed to pH ≥ 7.20.

visualRationale.title: ARDSnet lung-protective ventilation targets
visualRationale.caption: Lower tidal volumes prevent volutrauma. Permissive hypercapnia is acceptable to protect the lung.

metrics.label: Plateau pressure
metrics.directionLabel: safety ceiling — check with inspiratory hold

visualRationale.conclusion: Lung protection = lower TV + controlled Pplat ≤ 30 + adequate PEEP. Accept pH ≥ 7.20.

stem: A 68-year-old patient with COPD is admitted for acute exacerbation. ABGs on 2L nasal cannula: pH 7.32, PaCO2 62 mmHg, HCO3 30 mEq/L, PaO2 55 mmHg. Which interpretation and priority action is MOST appropriate?

options.text: Respiratory alkalosis with metabolic compensation; increase O2 to 5L/min
Respiratory acidosis with metabolic compensation (chronic); initiate non-invasive positive pressure ventilation (NIPPV) and notify provider
Metabolic alkalosis with respiratory compensation; administer sodium bicarbonate
Mixed acidosis; prepare for emergent intubation

rationale: pH 7.32 (acidotic), PaCO2 62 (elevated — respiratory acidosis), HCO3 30 (elevated — compensatory metabolic alkalosis). The elevated bicarbonate indicates chronic CO2 retention with renal compensation — classic COPD pattern. The primary problem is respiratory acidosis. With pH 7.32 and PaO2 55 mmHg, this patient meets criteria for NIPPV (BiPAP) which can reduce work of breathing, improve ventilation, and avoid intubation. High-flow O2 must be used cautiously in COPD to avoid eliminating the hypoxic drive, though SpO2 target of 88-92% is acceptable. Sodium bicarb is not indicated. Emergent intubation is not yet warranted with this pH.
tags: Respiratory / ABG Interpretation

In COPD exacerbation with respiratory acidosis and elevated HCO3: the compensation is chronic. Treat with NIPPV (BiPAP) first. Target SpO2 88-92% to preserve hypoxic drive. Intubation is a last resort.
takeaway: In COPD exacerbation with respiratory acidosis and elevated HCO3: the compensation is chronic. Treat with NIPPV (BiPAP) first. Target SpO2 88-92% to preserve hypoxic drive. Intubation is a last resort.

visualRationale.title: COPD exacerbation ABG: chronic respiratory acidosis
visualRationale.caption: Elevated HCO3 = renal compensation = chronic CO2 retention. This is not an acute emergency pattern alone.
visualRationale.conclusion: Chronic respiratory acidosis. Treat: NIPPV (BiPAP), target SpO2 88-92%, avoid high-flow O2.

stem: A nurse is caring for a mechanically ventilated patient in the ICU. The high-pressure ventilator alarm suddenly activates. The nurse notices the patient is agitated, breath sounds are absent on the left, the trachea appears deviated to the right, and SpO2 is dropping from 97% to 84%. What is the PRIORITY nursing action?

options.text: Increase FiO2 to 1.0 and suction the endotracheal tube
Disconnect the patient from the ventilator and manually bag-ventilate while calling for immediate provider assistance
Administer a sedation bolus to decrease patient agitation and airway resistance
Obtain a portable chest X-ray to confirm suspected pneumothorax

rationale: Absent unilateral breath sounds, tracheal deviation, agitation, high-pressure alarm, and acute SpO2 decline are classic signs of tension pneumothorax — a life-threatening emergency. The tracheal deviation away from the affected side indicates mediastinal shift with cardiovascular compromise imminent. The immediate nursing priority is to manually ventilate (hand-bagging) to assess and temporarily manage oxygenation while the provider is called for emergent needle decompression. Suctioning will not address pneumothorax. Increasing FiO2 alone does not treat the mechanical obstruction. Sedation is inappropriate. An X-ray delays definitive treatment when the patient is hemodynamically compromised — tension pneumothorax is a clinical diagnosis.
tags: Respiratory / Airway Management

Tension pneumothorax triad in ventilated patients: absent unilateral breath sounds + tracheal deviation (away from affected side) + hemodynamic deterioration. Disconnect and bag → call provider → emergent needle decompression. Do NOT delay for X-ray.
takeaway: Tension pneumothorax triad in ventilated patients: absent unilateral breath sounds + tracheal deviation (away from affected side) + hemodynamic deterioration. Disconnect and bag → call provider → emergent needle decompression. Do NOT delay for X-ray.

visualRationale.title: Tension pneumothorax: recognize and act
visualRationale.caption: Clinical diagnosis — do NOT wait for X-ray when the patient is decompensating on the vent.

nodes.label: High-pressure vent alarm + unilateral absent breath sounds
Tracheal deviation away from affected side
Emergent needle decompression
nodes.value: 2nd ICS, MCL → chest tube for definitive treatment

visualRationale.conclusion: Tension PTX = clinical diagnosis. Act before imaging. Every minute delays risks cardiac arrest.

stem: A patient has been mechanically ventilated for 5 days following ARDS. The provider orders a spontaneous breathing trial (SBT). Which assessment finding would indicate the patient has PASSED the SBT and is ready for extubation consideration?

options.text: RR 30/min, SpO2 90% on FiO2 0.40, patient using significant accessory muscles at 30 minutes
RR 18/min, SpO2 96% on FiO2 0.40, TV 450 mL, minimal accessory muscle use, alert and cooperative after 30 minutes
RR 24/min, SpO2 94%, patient becomes diaphoretic at 20 minutes; SBT stopped early
RR 14/min, SpO2 98%, but patient has copious secretions requiring suctioning every 5 minutes

rationale: A successful SBT is characterized by: RR < 25/min, SpO2 ≥ 90-95%, adequate tidal volume (typically > 4-5 mL/kg IBW), absence of significant respiratory distress (no accessory muscle use, diaphoresis, or agitation), and hemodynamic stability for at least 30 minutes. Option B meets all criteria. Option A has tachypnea and accessory muscle use — SBT failure. Option C required early termination due to distress — SBT failure. Option D: despite adequate ventilatory parameters, copious secretions requiring q5min suctioning indicate inability to protect/clear the airway — this is a separate extubation contraindication.
tags: Respiratory / Weaning and Extubation

SBT success criteria: RR < 25, SpO2 ≥ 90%, adequate TV, no accessory muscle use, hemodynamically stable, tolerates 30 minutes. Passing the SBT is necessary but not sufficient — also assess cough strength and secretion management before extubation.
takeaway: SBT success criteria: RR < 25, SpO2 ≥ 90%, adequate TV, no accessory muscle use, hemodynamically stable, tolerates 30 minutes. Passing the SBT is necessary but not sufficient — also assess cough strength and secretion management before extubation.

stem: A 55-year-old patient presents with acute respiratory failure. ABGs: pH 7.22, PaCO2 35 mmHg, HCO3 14 mEq/L, PaO2 88 mmHg on 4L NC. Lactic acid is 6.8 mmol/L. What type of respiratory failure is present and what is the underlying mechanism?

options.text: Type I (hypoxemic) respiratory failure due to ventilation-perfusion mismatch
Type II (hypercapnic) respiratory failure due to pump failure
Type I respiratory failure with a superimposed metabolic acidosis due to tissue hypoperfusion
Pure metabolic acidosis with appropriate respiratory compensation; no respiratory failure

rationale: PaO2 88 mmHg on 4L NC is hypoxemic relative to O2 delivery (P:F ratio would require full FiO2 calculation, but supplemental O2 requirement establishes Type I failure). The pH 7.22 (acidotic), PaCO2 35 (normal-low — not retaining CO2), HCO3 14 (low — metabolic acidosis) pattern indicates a metabolic acidosis with partial respiratory compensation (lungs are blowing off CO2 to compensate). Lactic acid 6.8 signals severe tissue hypoperfusion/anaerobic metabolism — likely septic or cardiogenic shock. This is Type I respiratory failure (oxygenation failure from parenchymal disease or V/Q mismatch) superimposed on a high anion gap metabolic acidosis from lactic acidosis.
tags: Respiratory / Respiratory Failure

Classify respiratory failure: Type I = hypoxemic (PaO2 low, PaCO2 normal/low); Type II = hypercapnic (PaCO2 high, pH low). Metabolic acidosis can coexist — always check pH, HCO3, lactate, and anion gap together.
takeaway: Classify respiratory failure: Type I = hypoxemic (PaO2 low, PaCO2 normal/low); Type II = hypercapnic (PaCO2 high, pH low). Metabolic acidosis can coexist — always check pH, HCO3, lactate, and anion gap together.

visualRationale.title: Type I vs. Type II respiratory failure
visualRationale.caption: Classify by which failure dominates: oxygenation (gas exchange) or ventilation (pump).
visualRationale.conclusion: High PaCO2 + low pH = Type II. Low PaO2 with normal PaCO2 = Type I. Both can coexist.

stem: A mechanically ventilated patient develops sudden desaturation (SpO2 90% → 78%). The nurse performs the DOPE mnemonic assessment. Which finding would indicate the problem is 'Obstruction'?

options.text: The ETT has migrated and breath sounds are unilateral
A mucus plug is visualized in the ETT and resolved after suctioning
The ventilator circuit is disconnected at the Y-piece
A new pneumothorax is identified on chest X-ray

rationale: The DOPE mnemonic for acute ventilator desaturation: D = Displacement (ETT migration/dislodgement), O = Obstruction (mucus plug, biting ETT, kinked tubing), P = Pneumothorax, E = Equipment failure. A mucus plug visualized and cleared by suctioning is the classic example of Obstruction. ETT migration causing unilateral breath sounds = Displacement. Disconnected circuit = Equipment failure. Pneumothorax on X-ray = Pneumothorax. Systematically applying DOPE ensures the cause is identified and corrected quickly.
tags: Respiratory / Ventilator Management

DOPE mnemonic for vent desaturation: Displacement → Obstruction → Pneumothorax → Equipment. Always disconnect and hand-bag if uncertain — this immediately tests for equipment failure and buys time while assessing the other categories.
takeaway: DOPE mnemonic for vent desaturation: Displacement → Obstruction → Pneumothorax → Equipment. Always disconnect and hand-bag if uncertain — this immediately tests for equipment failure and buys time while assessing the other categories.

visualRationale.title: DOPE mnemonic: acute vent desaturation
visualRationale.caption: Systematic four-cause framework prevents missing the diagnosis during crisis.

nodes.label: D — Displacement
nodes.value: ETT migrated or dislodged → check position, capnography

nodes.label: O — Obstruction
nodes.value: Mucus plug, biting, kinked circuit → suction ETT

nodes.label: P — Pneumothorax
nodes.value: Absent breath sounds, tracheal deviation → needle decompression

nodes.label: E — Equipment failure
nodes.value: Disconnection or malfunction → disconnect and bag manually

visualRationale.conclusion: Disconnect and bag FIRST if uncertain — immediately tests E while managing oxygenation.

stem: A patient with severe ARDS has a PaO2/FiO2 (P:F ratio) of 82 despite FiO2 0.80 and PEEP 14 cmH2O. The intensivist considers prone positioning. Which nursing assessment finding is a CONTRAINDICATION to prone positioning?

options.text: SpO2 88% on current settings
Unstable spinal fracture identified on imaging
BMI of 36
Recent central line placement 48 hours ago

rationale: Prone positioning significantly improves oxygenation in severe ARDS (P:F < 150) by recruiting dorsal lung segments and redistributing perfusion. Contraindications include: unstable spinal fractures (risk of cord injury with turning), open abdominal wounds, hemodynamic instability not responsive to vasopressors, elevated ICP, and recent tracheal surgery. An unstable spinal fracture is an absolute contraindication. Low SpO2 (88%) is the indication FOR proning — it is why the intervention is being considered. Obesity (BMI 36) requires additional staff and planning but is not a contraindication. A 48-hour-old central line is appropriate — lines must be secured but do not contraindicate proning.
tags: Respiratory / ARDS Management

Prone positioning indications: severe ARDS (P:F < 150) on optimal vent settings. Key absolute contraindications: unstable spinal injury, open abdomen, uncontrolled elevated ICP. Lines and tubes can be managed — the spine cannot be compromised.
takeaway: Prone positioning indications: severe ARDS (P:F < 150) on optimal vent settings. Key absolute contraindications: unstable spinal injury, open abdomen, uncontrolled elevated ICP. Lines and tubes can be managed — the spine cannot be compromised.

visualRationale.title: Prone positioning decision gate
visualRationale.caption: Severe ARDS benefits from proning — but check absolute contraindications first.

nodes.label: P:F ratio < 150 despite FiO2 >0.60 and PEEP ≥ 5?
Check absolute contraindications
nodes.value: Unstable spine, open abdomen, uncontrolled ICP

nodes.label: Absolute contraindication present?
nodes.value: → do NOT prone — consider alternative rescue strategies

visualRationale.conclusion: Unstable spinal injury = absolute contraindication. Lines and tubes are manageable. Spine is not.

stem: A nurse is caring for a patient who was extubated 3 hours ago following a 6-day intubation. The patient is now becoming increasingly stridorous, with a RR of 32 and SpO2 declining to 91%. What is the priority intervention?

options.text: Apply a non-rebreather mask at 15L/min and notify the provider
Administer nebulized racemic epinephrine and place the patient upright, call provider immediately, and prepare for possible re-intubation
Encourage the patient to take slow, deep breaths to reduce stridor
Obtain a throat culture and start empiric antibiotics for suspected laryngeal infection

rationale: Post-extubation stridor indicates laryngeal or subglottic edema, which is a medical emergency with risk of complete airway obstruction.
